topics.title: Terminology and Definitions, Kinematic Pairs, Kinematic Chains, Degrees of Freedom of Mechanisms, Kinematic Inversion of 4-Bar and Slider
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Terminology and Definitions, Kinematic Pairs, Kinematic Chains, Degrees of Freedom of Mechanisms, Kinematic Inversion of 4-Bar and Slider # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fundamental Terminology of Mechanisms Before we analyse any machine, every KTU examiner expects you to be fluent in the **language of kinematics**. Below are the rigorous, board-approved definitions of the foundational terms. ### Machine A **machine** is a device that receives energy in some available form and utilises it to do a particular type of work in a **controlled manner**, overcoming a useful resistance. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Definition:** A machine is a combination of resistant bodies (links) so arranged that by means of them definite constrained relative motions are communicated to the parts, and the mechanical forces exerted are transmitted in a predetermined manner. ### Mechanism A **mechanism** is a combination of rigid or resistant bodies (links) so connected that they move upon one another with definite relative motion and transmit force from the source to the load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Machine vs Mechanism** — the *only* functional difference is energy conversion. A *mechanism* transmits/modifies motion; a *machine* also does useful work. A clock is a machine; a clock's escapement alone is a mechanism. ### Structure A **structure** is a combination of resistant bodies (links) so connected that they do **NOT** move relative to one another. Structures are designed to carry loads without motion (e.g., a bridge truss, a building frame). ### Kinematics **Kinematics** is the branch of theoretical mechanics that studies motion of a body (or a system of bodies) without considering the forces that produce the motion. ### Dynamics **Dynamics** is the branch of mechanics that relates the motion of bodies to the **forces** that cause them. It is the combination of kinematics *plus* kinetics (force analysis). | Term | Deals with motion? | Deals with forces? | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Kinematics | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Velocity analysis of slider-crank | | Kinetics | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Force on a piston | | Dynamics | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Full force-motion coupling | --- ## 1.2 The Link (or Element / Kinematic Link) A **link** is a single resistant body (or an assembly of rigidly connected bodies) that has **no relative motion** among its own parts. Every mechanism is built from links. > [!NOTE] > In KTU notation, a link is also called a *kinematic link*, *element*, or *member*. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of a human arm. The upper arm bone (humerus), the forearm bone (radius-ulna), the palm, and the fingers are all **links**. The joints (shoulder, elbow, wrist) are the **joints** that connect them. A mechanism is a mechanical "skeleton" of links joined together, exactly like a robot arm. --- ## 1.3 Kinematic Pair A **kinematic pair** is a **joint** between two links that permits **constrained relative motion** between them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** When two links are in contact with each other, the connection between them is called a kinematic pair. The nature of relative motion depends on the *type* of contact. Every pair is characterised by: 1. **Type of contact** (sliding / rolling / screw / etc.) 2. **Type of relative motion** (rotation / translation / helical / spherical) 3. **Degree of constraint** (lower pair / higher pair) **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** A door and its hinge. The door rotates about the hinge — the hinge is a **kinematic pair** joining the door (link) to the wall (fixed link). The hinge permits *only* rotation — it has high *constraint*. --- ## 1.4 Kinematic Chain A **kinematic chain** is a series of links connected by **kinematic pairs**, in such a way that it provides controlled, definite relative motion. > [!NOTE] > A chain becomes a *mechanism* when one of its links is fixed (grounded). A chain becomes a *structure* when no relative motion is possible. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** A bicycle chain is **NOT** a kinematic chain (it has no constrained pairs except where it meshes with the gear teeth). A four-bar linkage (4 bars joined by 4 hinges) **IS** a kinematic chain — and once you fix one bar to the ground, it becomes a *mechanism*. --- ## 1.5 Degrees of Freedom (DOF / Mobility) The **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a mechanism — also called **mobility** — is the **number of independent inputs** (or coordinates) required to **completely specify the configuration** of the mechanism. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** The number of independent relative motions possible between the links of a mechanism is called the degree of freedom (or mobility) of the mechanism. It is denoted by **F**, **M**, or **n**. For a planar mechanism: each unconstrained rigid body in a plane has **3 DOF** (translation in x, translation in y, rotation in the plane). --- ## 1.6 Kinematic Inversion **Kinematic inversion** is the process of obtaining **new mechanisms** by fixing **different links** of the same kinematic chain, one at a time. > [!NOTE] > The **number of inversions** of a chain with `n` links = `n` (because any one of the n links can be the fixed/ground link). The number of *distinct* useful mechanisms is usually less. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a 4-bar chain (4 links, 4 hinges). If you nail link-1 to the floor, you get a particular motion. Now un-nail link-1 and nail link-2 to the floor instead. You get a **completely different mechanism** from the *same* chain. This re-grounding is **inversion**. --- ## 1.7 Gruebler's Criterion (Preview) For a planar mechanism with `n` links, `j₁` lower pairs, and `j₂` higher pairs: $$F \;=\; 3(n-1) \;-\; 2j_{1} \;-\; j_{2}$$ This is **Kutzbach's equation** (also called **Gruebler's criterion**) and is the **single most tested formula** in Module 1 of PCAUT502. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Kinematic Pairs (High-Yield Topic) Kinematic pairs are classified based on three criteria. The KTU board **loves** asking 3-mark and 7-mark questions on this. ### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Type of Contact | Pair Type | Contact | Motion Allowed | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Lower pair** | Surface contact | Sliding only (1 DOF) | Piston in cylinder, shaft in bearing | | **Higher pair** | Point / line contact | Point/line rolling or sliding (2 DOF) | Cam-follower, ball-bearing, gear mesh | > [!NOTE] > **Lower pair → 2 constraints, 1 DOF**
**Higher pair → 1 constraint, 2 DOF** This is the *foundation* of the Kutzbach formula coefficients (2 for lower pair, 1 for higher pair). ### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Type of Relative Motion | Pair Name | DOF | Description | Real Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Revolute (R)** | 1 | Pure rotation about a fixed axis | Door hinge, crank-shaft journal | | **Prismatic (P)** | 1 | Pure sliding along a straight line | Piston in cylinder, ram in lathe | | **Screw (Helical)** | 1 | Rotation + translation coupled (helix) | Lead screw, nut-bolt, screw jack | | **Cylindrical** | 2 | Rotation + translation along the same axis | Shaft in a journal bearing (loose fit) | | **Spherical** | 3 | Rotation about any axis through a fixed point | Ball-and-socket joint, human hip | | **Planar** | 3 | Sliding on a plane (any 2D motion) | Flat block on a flat surface | | **Rolling** | 2 | Pure rolling on a surface (no slip) | Disc rolling on flat ground | | **Cam pair** | 2 | Follower sliding on a cam profile | Cam and follower in IC engine | ### 2.1.3 Classification Based on Type of Joint Closure | Pair Type | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | **Self-closed (form-closed)** | Geometry of the joint itself maintains contact | Ball in a socket, prism in a slider | | **Force-closed (positively-closed)** | External force (spring, gravity) maintains contact | Cam-follower, valve mechanism | ### 2.1.4 Classification Based on Number of DOF (Frequent KTU Question) | Category | DOF | Kutzbach coefficient | |---|---|---| | **Lower pair** | 1 | 2 constraints | | **Higher pair** | 2 | 1 constraint | | **Pair with 0 DOF** | 0 | Rigid joint (welded) — not a pair | --- ## 2.2 Types of Kinematic Chains ### 2.2.1 Four-Bar Chain (Quadric Cycle) - 4 links, 4 revolute pairs - The *most common* planar chain - DOF = 1 (single input gives definite output) ### 2.2.2 Single Slider-Crank Chain - 4 links, 3 revolute pairs + 1 prismatic pair - DOF = 1 - Piston in IC engine is its third inversion ### 2.2.3 Double Slider-Crank Chain - 5 links (approx.), 2 prismatic + 2 revolute pairs - DOF = 1 - Example: Scotch yoke, elliptical trammel ### 2.2.4 Toggle Mechanism - Special 4-bar where one link is almost collinear - Provides high mechanical advantage at near-straight position --- ## 2.3 Degree of Freedom — Conceptual Depth Every unconstrained rigid body in a **plane** has **3 DOF**: $(x, y, \theta)$. When a **lower pair** (surface contact) is introduced, the contact **removes 2 DOF** → 1 DOF remains. When a **higher pair** (point/line contact) is introduced, it **removes 1 DOF** → 2 DOF remain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Conceptual Logic:** Start with `n` free bodies → 3n DOF. Grounding 1 link removes 3 DOF. Each pair removes its constraint count. The remainder is mobility **F**. For a planar mechanism: $\;F = 3n - 3 - 2j_{1} - j_{2} = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}\;$ For a spatial (3D) mechanism (Kutzbach spatial): $\;F = 6(n-1) - 5j_{1} - 4j_{2} - 3j_{3} - 2j_{4} - j_{5}\;$ --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$ | Kutzbach (Gruebler) equation for planar mechanism | Compute DOF of any planar mechanism | | 2 | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_{1} - 4j_{2} - 3j_{3} - 2j_{4} - j_{5}$ | Kutzbach spatial equation | Spatial mechanisms (3D) | | 3 | $n$ links $\Rightarrow n$ inversions | Inversions of a chain | Number of mechanisms derivable | | 4 | Four-bar: $F=3(4-1)-2(4)=1$ | Pre-baked result | Quick check | | 5 | Slider-crank: $F=3(4-1)-2(3)-1(2)=1$ | Pre-baked result | Quick check | | 6 | DOF = 0 → **Structure** | No motion | Truss, frame | | 7 | DOF = 1 → **Mechanism** | 1 input needed | All useful machines | | 8 | DOF = 2 → **Redundant / lock** | 2 inputs required | Uncontrolled machine | | 9 | DOF = -ve → **Pre-loaded structure** | Over-constrained | Cannot move at all | | 10 | $P_{L} + P_{H} = j_{1}+j_{2}$ | Total pair count | Verification | > [!IMPORTANT] > **$j_{1}$** counts **lower pairs** (R, P, Screw, Cylindrical, Spherical, Planar, Cam-pair-with-surface).
**$j_{2}$** counts **higher pairs** (Cam-follower point contact, gear mesh point/line contact). --- ## 2.5 Kinematic Inversion — Conceptual Foundation When you take a kinematic chain and **fix a different link** in turn, you obtain a different mechanism. The motion characteristics of the unfixed links **change** dramatically based on which link is grounded. ### Why does inversion matter in engineering? 1. **Industrial versatility** — The same chain gives multiple useful mechanisms (locomotive coupling, beam engine, IC engine, hand pump — all slider-crank inversions). 2. **Optimised motion** — Different inversions suit different speed/force/torque profiles. 3. **Packaging constraint** — Fixing different links lets us mount the mechanism optimally in a machine. --- ## 2.6 Four-Bar Chain Inversions (4 Inversions) The four-bar chain has 4 links (numbered 1, 2, 3, 4) joined by 4 revolute pairs. Fixing each in turn gives: | Fix | Inversion Name | Resulting Motion | |---|---|---| | **Link 1** (smallest, shortest) | **Crank-Rocker** | Input rotates fully; output oscillates (rocker) | | **Link 2** (longest, ground) | **Double-Crank** (rotary) | Both input & output rotate fully; useful for coupling rods, parallelograms | | **Link 3** (coupler) | **Double-Rocker** | Both input & output oscillate (neither full rotation) | | **Link 4** | **Crank-Rocker** (mirror) | Other crank-rocker variant; used in pump mechanisms | **Special case: Parallel-coupler four-bar (parallelogram)** → both opposite links equal and parallel → coupler translates without rotation → used in parallel ruler, vehicle suspensions. --- ## 2.7 Single Slider-Crank Chain Inversions (4 Inversions) The slider-crank chain has 4 links joined by 3 revolute + 1 prismatic pair. | Fix | Inversion Name | Real-World Application | |---|---|---| | **Cylinder (link 3)** | **1st inversion** — Crank & Slotted Lever | **Whitworth Quick Return** mechanism (shaper) | | **Crank (link 1)** | **2nd inversion** — Bull Engine | Rotary IC engines, hand pumps (rotating crank) | | **Slider (link 4)** | **3rd inversion** — Oscillating Cylinder Engine | Crank fixed; cylinder oscillates — e.g., crank-fixed steam engine | | **Connecting rod (link 2)** | **4th inversion** — Hand pump / Rotary IC engine | Pumping mechanism; root blower | > [!NOTE] > The **3rd inversion** of the slider-crank chain is what powers the **slider-crank IC engine** of a car — the cylinder is the fixed link (block), the crank rotates, and the piston slides. --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications | Mechanism | Inversion / Type | Application | |---|---|---| | Whitworth QRM | 1st inversion slider-crank | Shaper, slotters, power presses | | Crank & slotted lever | 1st inversion | Quick-return in shaping machines | | Bull engine | 2nd inversion | Pumping stations (old) | | Oscillating cylinder engine | 3rd inversion | Stationary steam engines | | Hand pump | 4th inversion | Water-well pumps | | Locomotive coupling rod | 2nd inversion of 4-bar | Drives wheels of a steam loco | | Beam engine | 1st inversion of 4-bar | Early steam pumping | | Crank-rocker | 4-bar | Window wiper mechanism | # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Kutzbach's (Gruebler's) Criterion — Planar This is the **most important derivation** of Module 1. It has appeared in KTU exams every year. The KTU board grants 7 marks for the full derivation. ### Step 1 — Start with n unconstrained rigid bodies in 3D space Each unconstrained rigid body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translations + 3 rotations). $$DOF_{\text{total, free}} = 6n$$ ### Step 2 — Specialise to the planar (2D) case In a planar mechanism, all motion is restricted to a single plane. Each link in the plane has: - 2 translations $(x, y)$ - 1 rotation $(\theta)$ - Total = **3 DOF per link** $$DOF_{\text{total, planar, free}} = 3n$$ ### Step 3 — Ground one link A mechanism is a kinematic chain with **one link fixed** to the ground (frame). This removes 3 DOF (the entire 2 translations and 1 rotation of that link). $$DOF_{\text{after grounding}} = 3n - 3 = 3(n-1)$$ ### Step 4 — Subtract the constraints imposed by kinematic pairs Each **lower pair** (surface contact) **removes 2 DOF** because contact along a surface eliminates 2 of the 3 planar DOF (typically: 1 translation perpendicular to surface, 1 rotation about the contact normal — leaving 1 DOF such as sliding or rotating). Each **higher pair** (point/line contact) **removes 1 DOF** because the point/line constraint eliminates only 1 translational DOF (perpendicular to the contact), leaving 2 DOF (slide along the line + rotate). For $j_{1}$ lower pairs and $j_{2}$ higher pairs, total constraints removed = $2j_{1} + 1j_{2}$. ### Step 5 — Final Kutzbach Equation The remaining DOF is the **mobility F**: $$F \;=\; 3(n-1) \;-\; 2j_{1} \;-\; 1j_{2}$$ In compact form: $$F \;=\; 3(n-1) \;-\; 2j_{1} \;-\; j_{2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Some textbooks (and even KTU answer keys) drop the 1 coefficient for $j_{2}$ in writing. The rigorous form is $\;F = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - 1\cdot j_{2}\;$ — keep the 1 to be safe. --- ## 3.2 Verification of DOF — Worked Example 1: Four-Bar Chain A four-bar chain has **n = 4** links joined by **4 revolute pairs** (all lower pairs). So $j_{1} = 4$ and $j_{2} = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} F &= 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2} \\ &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\ &= 3(3) - 8 \\ &= 9 - 8 \\ &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** F = 1 → one input gives definite output → valid mechanism. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Verification of DOF — Worked Example 2: Single Slider-Crank Chain A slider-crank has **n = 4** links, **3 revolute** + **1 prismatic** lower pairs. So $j_{1} = 4$ and $j_{2} = 0$. $$ \begin{aligned} F &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\ &= 9 - 8 \\ &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** F = 1 → one input → definite output → valid mechanism. ✅ --- ## 3.4 Verification of DOF — Worked Example 3: Five-Bar Planar Chain A 5-link chain with 5 revolute pairs: $n=5, \; j_{1}=5, \; j_{2}=0$. $$ \begin{aligned} F &= 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0 \\ &= 12 - 10 \\ &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** F = 2 → need **2 inputs** for definite motion → *not* a deterministic mechanism without constraint. (Adding a 6th link closes it.) --- ## 3.5 Verification of DOF — Worked Example 4: Cam-Follower A cam-follower pair has: - n = 3 (cam, follower, ground) - $j_{1} = 2$ (revolute at cam shaft + prismatic at follower) - $j_{2} = 1$ (higher pair — cam-follower contact) $$ \begin{aligned} F &= 3(3-1) - 2(2) - 1 \\ &= 6 - 4 - 1 \\ &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** F = 1 → one input (cam rotation) gives definite follower motion. ✅ --- ## 3.6 Verification of DOF — Worked Example 5: Engine Indicator (Pantograph) A simple engine indicator (a 4-bar with one extra parallel link) has: - n = 4, $j_{1} = 5$ (5 lower pairs), $j_{2} = 0$ $$ \begin{aligned} F &= 3(4-1) - 2(5) - 0 \\ &= 9 - 10 \\ &= -1 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** F = -1 → over-constrained (redundant constraint) → **Structure**, not a mechanism. This is a *locked* linkage. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — DOF of Common Mechanisms | # | Mechanism | n | j₁ | j₂ | Calculation | F | Type | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Four-bar | 4 | 4 | 0 | 3(3) − 2(4) − 0 | **1** | Mechanism | | 2 | Slider-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 3(3) − 2(4) − 0 | **1** | Mechanism | | 3 | Five-bar | 5 | 5 | 0 | 3(4) − 2(5) − 0 | **2** | Two-DOF chain | | 4 | Cam-follower (simple) | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3(2) − 2(2) − 1 | **1** | Mechanism | | 5 | Engine indicator | 4 | 5 | 0 | 3(3) − 2(5) − 0 | **−1** | Structure | | 6 | Trammel of Archimedes | 4 | 4 | 0 | 3(3) − 2(4) − 0 | **1** | Mechanism (elliptical) | | 7 | Six-bar (Stephenson) | 6 | 7 | 0 | 3(5) − 2(7) − 0 | **1** | Mechanism | | 8 | Watt's straight-line | 6 | 8 | 0 | 3(5) − 2(8) − 0 | **−1** | Special-case (parallel-coupler cancels) | | 9 | Bicycle pedal-crank | 4 | 4 | 0 | 3(3) − 2(4) − 0 | **1** | Mechanism | | 10 | Simple gear train | 4 | 4 | 0 | 3(3) − 2(4) − 0 | **1** | Mechanism | > [!NOTE] > The **Watt's straight-line** gives F = −1 by Gruebler, but produces a motion path. This is a *classical anomaly* — it works because the redundant constraints cancel geometrically. The KTU board may ask this as a "comment" question. --- ## 3.8 Inversion Derivations — How the Inversions Are Obtained ### 3.8.1 Four-Bar Chain — 4 Inversions Original chain: links 1, 2, 3, 4 in a closed loop with revolute pairs A, B, C, D. - **Inversion 1 (Link 1 fixed):** Link 1 is the ground. Depending on link lengths, link 2 is the **crank** (rotates fully) and link 4 is the **rocker** (oscillates). This is the **crank-rocker** (e.g., window wiper, beam engine). - **Inversion 2 (Link 2 fixed):** Link 2 (opposite) is the ground. Now links 1 and 3 (the side links) behave as **double cranks** (both rotate fully). This is the **double-crank** — used in **locomotive coupling rods**. - **Inversion 3 (Link 3 fixed):** Link 3 is the ground. Links 2 and 4 are both rocks; the mechanism becomes a **double-rocker** (e.g., stone crusher, sorting machines). - **Inversion 4 (Link 4 fixed):** Link 4 is the ground. By symmetry, this is again a **crank-rocker** with crank and rocker swapped. ### 3.8.2 Slider-Crank Chain — 4 Inversions Original chain: links 1, 2, 3, 4 with joints A (R), B (R), C (R), D (P). - Link 1 = crank - Link 2 = connecting rod - Link 3 = slotted lever (or fixed cylinder) - Link 4 = slider / piston - **Inversion 1 (Link 3 fixed, i.e. the slotted lever / cylinder):** The slotted lever is fixed; the crank rotates and a pin on the crank slides in the slot. This is the **Crank and Slotted Lever** — basis of the **Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism** used in shapers. - **Inversion 2 (Link 1 fixed, i.e. the crank):** The crank is the ground. Link 2 (rod) oscillates; link 4 (slider) reciprocates. This is the **Bull Engine** — old Cornish pumping engine. - **Inversion 3 (Link 4 fixed, i.e. the slider):** The slider is the ground. Link 3 (cylinder/slotted lever) oscillates about the fixed slider. This is the **Oscillating Cylinder Engine** and is equivalent to the **3rd inversion** that powers the **IC engine** (block is fixed; cylinder can be thought of as the moving "slider housing"). - **Inversion 4 (Link 2 fixed, i.e. the connecting rod):** The connecting rod is the ground. Crank (link 1) and slider (link 4) are both free. This is the **Hand Pump / Rotary IC Engine** — used in bicycle pumps and small pumps. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU exam alert:** The "**3rd inversion of slider-crank**" specifically refers to **fixing the slider**, NOT fixing the cylinder. In the IC engine context, the *cylinder block is the fixed link* and the *piston is the slider*, so the **IC engine is the 3rd inversion**. --- ## 3.9 Concept of Quick-Return Mechanism (QR) The **Whitworth QRM** (1st inversion) provides **unequal forward and return strokes** — useful in shapers where the cutting stroke is slow and the return stroke is fast. **Quick-return ratio** (very common in 14-mark questions): $$Q = \dfrac{t_c}{t_r} = \dfrac{2\pi - 2\alpha}{2\alpha} = \dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha} = \dfrac{\pi}{\alpha} - 1$$ where $2\alpha$ is the angle turned by the crank during the cutting (working) stroke and $(2\pi - 2\alpha)$ is the angle during the return stroke. > [!NOTE] > This is covered in detail in Module 2. Module 1 only requires naming the inversion and identifying the chain. # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams below render as **block-level functional architecture** flowcharts. They map the *logic of relationships* (which link is fixed, what motion results) rather than trying to draw physical link diagrams which Mermaid cannot accurately render. ## 4.1 Block-Functional Architecture of Mechanism Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef def fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#9b2226,color:#000 classDef input fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#1d4e89,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#386641,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#fae588,stroke:#bb3e03,color:#000 root[Kinematic System]:::def root --> inputA[Energy Input Source]:::input root --> chainA[Kinematic Chain of Links and Pairs]:::input root --> outputA[Output Motion Work]:::input chainA --> branch1{Type of Constraint?}:::branch branch1 --> leaf1[Machine]:::leaf branch1 --> leaf2[Mechanism]:::leaf branch1 --> leaf3[Structure]:::leaf leaf2 --> note1[DOA = 1, deterministic motion]:::def leaf3 --> note2[DOA = 0, no relative motion]:::def leaf1 --> note3[Machine = Mechanism plus useful work]:::def ``` ## 4.2 Kinematic Pair Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef rootnode fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#9d4e15,color:#000 classDef level1 fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 classDef level2 fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#5f0f40,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#ffc6ff,stroke:#89023e,color:#000 pr0[Kinematic Pair]:::rootnode pr0 --> prA[By Type of Contact]:::level1 pr0 --> prB[By Relative Motion]:::level1 pr0 --> prC[By Joint Closure]:::level1 prA --> prA1[Lower Pair Surface Contact]:::level2 prA --> prA2[Higher Pair Point or Line Contact]:::level2 prB --> prB1[Revolute 1 DOF]:::level2 prB --> prB2[Prismatic 1 DOF]:::level2 prB --> prB3[Screw 1 DOF]:::level2 prB --> prB4[Spherical 3 DOF]:::level2 prB --> prB5[Cam 2 DOF]:::level2 prB --> prB6[Cylindrical 2 DOF]:::level2 prB --> prB7[Rolling 2 DOF]:::level2 prC --> prC1[Self Closed Form Closed]:::level2 prC --> prC2[Force Closed Spring Gravity]:::level2 prA1 --> leafA1[Example Piston in Cylinder]:::leaf prA2 --> leafA2[Example Cam Follower Pair]:::leaf prB1 --> leafB1[Example Door Hinge]:::leaf prB3 --> leafB3[Example Lead Screw Nut]:::leaf prB4 --> leafB4[Example Ball and Socket]:::leaf prB5 --> leafB5[Example Cam and Follower]:::leaf ``` ## 4.3 Inversion Logic — Four-Bar Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef base fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#023e8a,color:#000 classDef fix fill:#ffadad,stroke:#6a040f,color:#000 classDef mech fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000 baseChain[4 Bar Chain 4 Links 4 Revolute Pairs]:::base baseChain --> f1[Fix Link 1 Shortest]:::fix baseChain --> f2[Fix Link 2 Opposite]:::fix baseChain --> f3[Fix Link 3 Coupler]:::fix baseChain --> f4[Fix Link 4]:::fix f1 --> m1[Crank Rocker Mechanism]:::mech f2 --> m2[Double Crank Rotary Coupling]:::mech f3 --> m3[Double Rocker Stone Crusher]:::mech f4 --> m4[Crank Rocker Mirror Variant]:::mech ``` ## 4.4 Inversion Logic — Slider-Crank Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef base fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#7d5a50,color:#000 classDef fix fill:#ffc1cc,stroke:#a4133c,color:#000 classDef mech fill:#a3ffd6,stroke:#0a7c5e,color:#000 baseSC[Slider Crank Chain 4 Links 3R plus 1P]:::base baseSC --> f1[Fix Slotted Lever Link 3]:::fix baseSC --> f2[Fix Crank Link 1]:::fix baseSC --> f3[Fix Slider Link 4]:::fix baseSC --> f4[Fix Connecting Rod Link 2]:::fix f1 --> m1[Inversion 1 Whitworth Quick Return Shaper]:::mech f2 --> m2[Inversion 2 Bull Engine Pumping]:::mech f3 --> m3[Inversion 3 Oscillating Cylinder Engine plus IC Engine Equivalent]:::mech f4 --> m4[Inversion 4 Hand Pump plus Rotary IC]:::mech ``` ## 4.5 DOF Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef startNode fill:#90e0ef,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#cdb4db,stroke:#5a189a,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#ffafcc,stroke:#800f2f,color:#000 s0[Compute F = 3n minus 1 minus 2j1 minus j2]:::startNode s0 --> d1{F less than 0?}:::branch s0 --> d2{F equals 0?}:::branch s0 --> d3{F equals 1?}:::branch s0 --> d4{F greater than 1?}:::branch d1 --> l1[Over Constrained Structure Redundant Constraint]:::leaf d2 --> l2[Structure No Motion Truss Frame]:::leaf d3 --> l3[Mechanism 1 Input Gives Definite Motion]:::leaf d4 --> l4[Redundant Chain Needs Multiple Inputs]:::leaf ``` ## 4.6 Conceptual Block Diagram — Link → Pair → Chain → Mechanism → Machine ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef a fill:#e0fbfc,stroke:#3d5a80,color:#000 classDef b fill:#98c1d9,stroke:#293241,color:#000 classDef c fill:#ee6c4d,stroke:#9d0208,color:#fff classDef d fill:#283618,stroke:#606c38,color:#fff classDef e fill:#dda15e,stroke:#bc6c25,color:#000 L[Resistant Body Link]:::a P[Connection Pair]:::b C[Series Chain]:::c M[Mechanism Fix One Link]:::d MA[Machine plus Energy Conversion]:::e L --> P --> C --> M --> MA ``` # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions > [!NOTE] > Part A questions in the KTU 2024 Scheme are 3 marks each, testing direct recall and one-line concepts. The model answer length should be 30–60 words. ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]** **Differentiate between a machine and a mechanism. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** A **mechanism** is a combination of links and pairs that transmits or modifies motion (e.g., a four-bar linkage). A **machine** is a mechanism that *also* receives energy in one form and converts it to useful work, overcoming a resistance (e.g., an IC engine). The functional difference is *energy conversion to useful work*. > Total: ~3 marks (1 for definition each, 1 for example). ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Classify kinematic pairs based on the type of contact. How is each type characterised in Kutzbach's equation?** **Model Answer:** Kinematic pairs are classified into **lower pairs** (surface contact, 2 constraints, 1 DOF) and **higher pairs** (point/line contact, 1 constraint, 2 DOF). In Kutzbach's equation, $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$, $j_{1}$ counts lower pairs and $j_{2}$ counts higher pairs. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, every module typically carries an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Sub-parts are commonly **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below are two full **Question A** and **Question B** alternatives. --- ### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Mechanism Classification and DOF* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style 2024]** > **Q. A (a)** Define a kinematic pair. Classify kinematic pairs with suitable diagrams and examples. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. A (b)** For a 5-bar planar mechanism with 5 revolute pairs, compute the degrees of freedom. State whether the chain is a mechanism or a structure. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q. A (a) (7 marks) A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two links that permits constrained relative motion (definition — 1 mark). **Classification** (covering all 4 bases — 6 marks): **(i) Based on type of contact:** - **Lower pair** — surface contact, 1 DOF (e.g., piston in cylinder, journal bearing). - **Higher pair** — point/line contact, 2 DOF (e.g., cam-follower, gear mesh). **(ii) Based on type of relative motion:** - Revolute (1 DOF, e.g., door hinge). - Prismatic (1 DOF, e.g., ram in lathe). - Screw / helical (1 DOF, e.g., lead screw). - Spherical (3 DOF, e.g., ball-and-socket). - Cylindrical (2 DOF, e.g., shaft in loose bearing). - Rolling (2 DOF, e.g., wheel on road). **(iii) Based on type of closure:** - Self-closed (form-closed) — geometry maintains contact (e.g., prism in slider). - Force-closed — external force maintains contact (e.g., cam-follower with spring). **(iv) Based on DOF:** - 1 DOF (R, P, screw) — lower pair. - 2 DOF (cam, rolling) — higher pair. - 3 DOF (spherical). --- ### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q. A (b) (7 marks) **Given:** $n = 5$ (5 links), $j_{1} = 5$ (5 revolute = lower pairs), $j_{2} = 0$ (no higher pairs). **Apply Kutzbach's equation for planar mechanism:** $$F = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$$ **Substitution:** $$ \begin{aligned} F &= 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 0 \\ &= 3(4) - 10 - 0 \\ &= 12 - 10 \\ &= 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $F = 2$ (calculation step — 4 marks). **Interpretation:** Since $F = 2$, the mechanism is a **two-DOF chain** and requires **2 independent inputs** to produce a definite motion. It is *not* a single-input mechanism; it is a **redundant chain** (1 mark). To convert it into a useful mechanism, one must either add one more link (making a 6-bar with 7 pairs, giving F = 1) or remove one link (1 mark). **Conclusion:** 5-bar with 5 revolute pairs is *not* a useful mechanism in its given form (1 mark). --- ### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Slider-Crank Inversions* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style 2024]** > **Q. B (a)** What is kinematic inversion? State the four inversions of a single slider-crank chain, naming the fixed link and one practical application of each. **(7 marks)** > > **Q. B (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of the **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism** as the 1st inversion of the slider-crank chain. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q. B (a) (7 marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Kinematic inversion is the process of obtaining new mechanisms from the same kinematic chain by fixing different links in turn. A chain with $n$ links has $n$ inversions. **The single slider-crank chain has 4 links: 1 (crank), 2 (connecting rod), 3 (slotted lever/cylinder housing), 4 (slider/piston). It has 4 inversions:** | Inversion | Fixed Link | Mechanism Name | Application (1 mark each) | |---|---|---|---| | 1st | Link 3 (slotted lever) | Crank and Slotted Lever | Whitworth Quick-Return mechanism in shapers and slotters | | 2nd | Link 1 (crank) | Bull Engine | Cornish pumping engines, old water pumps | | 3rd | Link 4 (slider) | Oscillating Cylinder Engine | Stationary steam engines; also IC engine analogue (block fixed, piston slides) | | 4th | Link 2 (connecting rod) | Hand Pump / Rotary IC engine | Bicycle hand-pumps, small rotary compressors | (6 marks for the table + naming + application; 1 mark for the opening definition.) --- ### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Q. B (b) (7 marks) **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism (WQR) — 1st Inversion of Slider-Crank Chain.** **Description (3 marks):** The WQR mechanism is derived from the slider-crank chain by fixing the **slotted lever** (link 3). The crank (link 1) rotates about a fixed pivot. A pin on the crank slides in a slot in the slotted lever. A connecting rod (link 2) links the slotted lever to a ram/slider (link 4) which reciprocates linearly carrying the tool. **Working Principle (3 marks):** As the crank rotates uniformly through 360°, the slotted lever oscillates. The forward (cutting) stroke of the ram happens during a *larger* angle of crank rotation when the pin moves *away* from the lever pivot. The return stroke happens during a *smaller* crank angle when the pin moves *towards* the pivot. This makes the return stroke *faster* than the cutting stroke — hence **quick return**. **Quick-Return Ratio (1 mark):** $$Q = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha} = \frac{\pi}{\alpha} - 1$$ where $2\alpha$ is the crank angle during the cutting stroke. **Application:** Shaper machine, slotter, power press — used where slow cutting and fast retraction of the tool is desired. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points:** > > 1. **Confusing inversion numbering** — Students frequently write "IC engine = 1st inversion" — it is the **3rd inversion** (slider fixed). > 2. **Mis-identifying pairs in Kutzbach** — A revolute is a *lower* pair, not higher. Cams and gears are *higher* pairs. > 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1 for the grounded link** — Use $3(n-1)$, not $3n$. > 4. **Inversion count = number of links, NOT number of pairs** — The number of inversions of a 4-bar is 4 (because 4 links), even though it has 4 pairs. This trips up many students. > 5. **Skipping the Kutzbach derivation** — If the question says "derive", you MUST show all 5 steps; the 7-mark allocation includes 3 marks for derivation, 2 for substitution, 2 for interpretation. A numerical answer without derivation gets *at most* 4/7. > 6. **Spelling "Gruebler" as "Grubler" or "Gruebner"** — KTU examiners do deduct 0.5–1 mark for the wrong spelling of technical proper nouns. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this to your wall for the night before the exam):** ### ✅ Definitions (must be word-perfect) - **Machine** — converts energy to useful work in a controlled manner. - **Mechanism** — combination of links transmitting/modifying motion (no energy conversion requirement). - **Structure** — combination of links carrying load with NO relative motion. - **Kinematic pair** — joint between two links permitting constrained relative motion. - **Kinematic chain** — series of links connected by pairs. - **Kinematic inversion** — re-grounding different links of the same chain. - **Degrees of freedom (Mobility)** — number of independent inputs for definite configuration. ### ✅ Must-Memorise Formulae - Planar Kutzbach: $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$ - Spatial Kutzbach: $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_{1} - 4j_{2} - 3j_{3} - 2j_{4} - j_{5}$ - Inversions of an n-link chain = n - Quick-return ratio: $Q = (\pi - \alpha)/\alpha$ ### ✅ Pair-Classification Snapshot - **Lower pair** → 2 constraints, 1 DOF (R, P, Screw, Cylindrical, Spherical, Planar) - **Higher pair** → 1 constraint, 2 DOF (Cam, gear-mesh, ball-bearing rolling) ### ✅ Four-Bar Inversions (4) 1. **Fix link 1 → Crank-Rocker** (window wiper, beam engine) 2. **Fix link 2 → Double-Crank** (locomotive coupling rod) 3. **Fix link 3 → Double-Rocker** (stone crusher) 4. **Fix link 4 → Crank-Rocker** (mirror variant) ### ✅ Slider-Crank Inversions (4) 1. **Fix slotted lever → 1st Inv → Whitworth QRM (shaper)** 2. **Fix crank → 2nd Inv → Bull Engine (pump)** 3. **Fix slider → 3rd Inv → Oscillating Cylinder Engine (IC engine analogue)** 4. **Fix connecting rod → 4th Inv → Hand Pump / Rotary IC** ### ✅ DOF Interpretation Table - $F < 0$ → **Structure** (over-constrained) - $F = 0$ → **Structure** (no motion possible) - $F = 1$ → **Mechanism** (single input, definite motion) ✅ - $F > 1$ → **Redundant chain** (multiple inputs needed) ### ✅ Common Worked Examples to Memorise | Mechanism | F | |---|---| | Four-bar chain | 1 | | Slider-crank | 1 | | Five-bar (5R) | 2 | | Cam-follower (3-link) | 1 | | Engine indicator | -1 | > [!NOTE] > **Final tip:** When the question says "With a neat sketch", always draw the four-bar / slider-crank diagram **AND** label the fixed link — examiners award 2 of the 7 marks just for the labelled sketch alone.

topics.title: Crank Chains, Velocity Analysis, Relative Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Crank Chains, Velocity Analysis, Relative Motion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Kinematic Chain, Mechanism & Crank Chain > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kinematic Chain:** A kinematic chain is an assemblage of links (rigid bodies) joined together such that each link moves relative to the others with constrained relative motion. When one of the links is fixed to the ground, the chain becomes a **mechanism** (a kinematic chain with at least one fixed link). A **Crank Chain** is a four-link kinematic chain (the most fundamental mechanism) in which one link is fixed and the other three rotate or oscillate. It is the *building block* of nearly all planar mechanisms found in engines, pumps, and machine tools. ### 1.2 The Four-Bar Chain — Configurations & Inversions The four-bar chain has **4 links** and **4 turning pairs (revolute joints)**. Depending on which link is fixed, the chain produces different inversions. When the shortest link is adjacent to the fixed link and satisfies **Grashof's Law**, four standard types emerge. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Window Opener:** Imagine a hinged window stay-bar. You rotate the small handle (crank), the bar (coupler) sweeps through space, and the latch (rocker) oscillates to push the window open. That mechanism *is* a four-bar crank-rocker. Now imagine replacing the handle with the fixed frame (fixed link inverted) — you get a double-crank. Grashof's Law is the rule that decides which behaviour you get for a given set of link lengths. | Four-Bar Type | Shortest Link Position | Motion of Other Two Links | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Double Crank (Drag-link)** | Fixed | Both rotate fully | Coupling rods of locomotives, Wagon coupling | | **Crank-Rocker** | Adjacent to fixed | One rotates, one oscillates | Windshield wipers, Internal combustion engines (with slider) | | **Double Rocker** | Coupler | Both oscillate | Rock crushers, Pantograph | | **Triple Rocker** | Grashof's law violated | All three oscillate (no full rotation possible) | Path generation in plots | ### 1.3 Velocity Analysis & Relative Motion **Velocity analysis** is the kinematic study that determines the linear and angular velocities of all members of a mechanism at a given instant, given the motion of the driving link. It is essential for **force analysis**, **design of flywheels**, **balancing**, and **cam design**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — Two Trains:** If Train A is moving east at 60 km/h and Train B is moving north at 40 km/h, then *relative to Train A*, Train B moves in a north-easterly direction at a velocity obtained by vector subtraction. The same principle applies to two points B and A on a rigid body: $\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$, where $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is perpendicular to the line AB. ### 1.4 Standard Symbols and Units - **Angular velocity** — $\omega$ — **rad/s** - **Linear velocity** — $V$ — **m/s** - **Length of link** — $l$ or $r$ — **mm** (in drawings) / **m** (in calculations) - **Frequency** — $N$ — **rpm** - **Conversion:** $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s - **Grashof's constant (Quality Factor):** $Q = \dfrac{S+L}{P+Q}$ where $S,L,P,Q$ are the four link lengths. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Grashof's Law (The Quality of a Four-Bar Chain) For a four-bar chain with link lengths $l_1$ (fixed), $l_2$ (input), $l_3$ (coupler), $l_4$ (output): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Grashof's Law (1836):** *The sum of the lengths of the shortest and longest links of a four-bar chain must be **less than or equal to** the sum of the other two links for at least one link to make a complete revolution.* Mathematically: $$S + L \leq P + Q$$ where $S$ = shortest, $L$ = longest, $P, Q$ = the remaining two links. **Why this works physically:** For continuous rotation, a link must be able to swing through all positions without being blocked by the geometry. The inequality ensures the dead-centre positions are passable. ### 2.2 Relative Velocity Method — Step-by-Step Logic The relative velocity method uses the **vector triangle equation**: $$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$ where $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is the velocity of B as observed from A. For a rigid link AB, this relative velocity is **perpendicular to AB**, with magnitude $\omega_2 \cdot AB$. **Operational Logic:** 1. Identify the fixed pivot (e.g., A is fixed → $\vec{V_A}=0$). 2. The velocity of the moving end (e.g., B) is **perpendicular to the crank**. 3. For any third point on a rigid link, use vector addition. 4. Two unknowns can be found from two equations (typically one magnitude + one direction per unknown vector). ### 2.3 Instantaneous Centre (IC) Method Every rigid body in plane motion has a point (the **Instantaneous Centre of Rotation, ICR**) about which it appears to be rotating at that instant. Velocity of any point on the body equals $\omega \times (\text{distance to ICR})$. > [!NOTE] > For a four-bar mechanism: $n = 4$ links → number of ICs = $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = \mathbf{6}$ instantaneous centres. **Kennedy's Theorem** states that three rigid bodies with relative motion have their three ICs collinear. ### 2.4 Klein's Construction (Velocity Image Method) Klein's construction is a streamlined graphical method to find the angular velocity of the **coupler** and the **follower** of a four-bar mechanism by exploiting the similarity between the configuration polygon and the velocity polygon. ### 2.5 ⚡ KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Critical for Board Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` in place of `|` inside the table to preserve Markdown syntax. | # | Concept / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Grashof's Law (Class I) | $S+L \leq P+Q$ | At least one link rotates fully | | 2 | Grashof's Law (Class II) | $S+L > P+Q$ | No link can rotate fully (Triple Rocker) | | 3 | Change Point (Special Case) | $S+L = P+Q$ | Toggle mechanism / change-point mechanism | | 4 | Angular Velocity Conversion | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | $N$ in rpm → $\omega$ in rad/s | | 5 | Relative Velocity Equation | $\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$ | Vector (triangle) equation | | 6 | Relative Velocity Direction | $\vec{V_{B/A}} \perp AB$ | For rigid links only | | 7 | Magnitude of Relative Velocity | $\vert V_{B/A} \vert = \omega_2 \cdot l_{AB}$ | $\omega_2$ = angular vel of link AB | | 8 | Total ICs in n-link Mechanism | $N_{IC} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Primary + secondary centres | | 9 | Angular Velocity via IC | $\omega_k = \dfrac{\vert V_A \vert}{\vert I_{k1}A \vert}$ | $I_{k1}$ = IC of link $k$ wrt ground | | 10 | Slider Velocity (exact) | $V_B = \omega r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right]$ | where $n = l/r$, approximate for small $r/l$ | | 11 | Angular Velocity of Connecting Rod | $\omega_{AB} = \dfrac{\omega \cos\theta}{n \cos\phi}$ | Slider-crank mechanism | | 12 | Instantaneous Velocity Ratio (Four-Bar) | $\dfrac{\omega_4}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{O_{12}I_{14}}{O_{34}I_{14}}$ | Using Aronhold-Kennedy theorem | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine Design:** Crank-rocker and slider-crank mechanisms determine piston velocity, which governs inertial forces and engine smoothness. - **Robotics:** Serial manipulator arms use multi-loop kinematic chains; velocity analysis is essential for trajectory planning. - **Agricultural Machinery:** Crank-rocker mechanisms in seed drills and reaper-binders. - **Textile Machinery:** Complex four-bar inversions in looms and knitting machines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Grashof's Law Using Loop-Closure Vector Equation Consider a four-bar chain $ABCD$ with link lengths $a, b, c, d$ (for links $AB, BC, CD, DA$). Let $\theta_2$ be the input crank angle, $\theta_4$ the output angle, and $\theta_3$ the coupler angle. The loop-closure equation is: $$\vec{r_2} + \vec{r_3} - \vec{r_4} - \vec{r_1} = \vec{0}$$ Expanding in components and differentiating with respect to time yields the velocity equations used to analyse the mechanism. Grashof's law emerges by considering the *limiting* (dead-centre) positions where the mechanism can just barely complete a rotation. At the limiting (toggle) position, two of the four links become collinear (overlapped). At this instant: $$S + L = P + Q$$ This is the **boundary condition** for full rotation. For rotation to be physically possible, the inequality $S+L < P+Q$ must hold (Class I mechanism). ### 3.2 Derivation: Velocity of Slider in a Slider-Crank Mechanism Consider a slider-crank $OAB$ where: - $OA = r$ (crank length), rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ - $AB = l$ (connecting rod length), angular velocity $\omega_{AB}$ - $B$ is the slider, moving along horizontal axis **Velocity Vector Equation:** $$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$ **Direction Analysis:** - $\vec{V_A}$ is perpendicular to $OA$ (direction at angle $90° + \theta$ from horizontal). - $\vec{V_B}$ is horizontal (along slider axis). - $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is perpendicular to $AB$ (direction at angle $90° + \phi$ from horizontal, where $\phi$ is the inclination of $AB$). **Resolving horizontally and vertically:** From horizontal: $$V_B = V_A \cos\theta + V_{B/A} \cos\phi$$ From vertical (the vertical components must cancel because $V_B$ is purely horizontal): $$0 = V_A \sin\theta - V_{B/A} \sin\phi$$ (assuming the slider is to the right of $O$ and crank rotates CCW). **Solving for $V_B$ and $V_{B/A}$:** From the vertical equation: $$V_{B/A} = V_A \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\sin\phi}$$ Substituting into the horizontal equation: $$V_B = V_A \cos\theta + V_A \dfrac{\sin\theta \cos\phi}{\sin\phi}$$ $$V_B = V_A \left[\cos\theta + \sin\theta \cot\phi\right]$$ Since $V_A = \omega r$, we obtain the **exact slider velocity**: $$\boxed{V_B = \omega r \left[\cos\theta + \sin\theta \cot\phi\right]}$$ with $\phi$ determined from $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l} \sin\theta$. **Using small-angle approximation** $\sin\phi \approx (r/l)\sin\theta$, $\cos\phi \approx 1$, the expression simplifies to the **approximate slider velocity**: $$\boxed{V_B \approx \omega r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2l}\sin 2\theta\right]}$$ This is the well-known textbook result. The second term $(r/2l)\sin 2\theta$ is the **secondary harmonic** — it is the source of secondary unbalanced forces in single-cylinder engines. ### 3.3 Numerical Example — Velocity Analysis of a Slider-Crank **Given:** Crank radius $r = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m, Connecting rod length $l = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m, Crank speed $N = 300$ rpm, Crank angle $\theta = 30°$ from inner dead centre. **Step 1 — Angular velocity of crank:** $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Magnitude of crank-pin velocity:** $$V_A = \omega \times r = 31.416 \times 0.1 = 3.1416 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Connecting rod inclination $\phi$:** $$\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l} \sin\theta = \dfrac{100}{300} \times \sin 30° = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 0.5 = 0.1667$$ $$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.1667) = 9.594°$$ $$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.1667^2} = \sqrt{0.9722} = 0.9860$$ **Step 4 — Slider velocity (exact):** $$V_B = V_A \left[\cos\theta + \sin\theta \cot\phi\right]$$ $$V_B = 3.1416 \left[\cos 30° + \sin 30° \times \cot 9.594°\right]$$ $$V_B = 3.1416 \left[0.8660 + 0.5 \times 5.9177\right]$$ $$V_B = 3.1416 \times [0.8660 + 2.9588]$$ $$V_B = 3.1416 \times 3.8248 = \mathbf{12.016 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 5 — Angular velocity of connecting rod:** $$\omega_{AB} = \dfrac{V_{B/A}}{l} = \dfrac{V_A \sin\theta}{l \sin\phi} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.5}{0.3 \times 0.1667} = \dfrac{1.5708}{0.05} = \mathbf{31.42 \text{ rad/s}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The slider velocity at 30° crank angle is 12.016 m/s, while the crank-pin moves at 3.1416 m/s. The ratio is ~3.82, confirming that at this configuration the slider is much faster than the crank-pin. The connecting rod also rotates at nearly the same angular speed as the crank in this configuration because $\cos\theta$ is large. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Velocity Analysis Methodology The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete decision-flow used by a KTU student to choose and execute the appropriate velocity analysis technique. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Given Mechanism Position and Input Motion]) --> idNodeA{Identify
Mechanism Type} idNodeA -- Four-bar --> fourBarPath[Fix input link velocity
V_in perpendicular to crank] idNodeA -- Slider-crank --> scPath[Crank angular velocity omega
and angle theta] idNodeA -- Inverted slider-crank --> invPath[Whitworth quick-return
use sine rule on velocity triangle] fourBarPath --> chooseMethod{Choose Analysis Method} chooseMethod -- Graphical --> kleinPath[Klein Construction
Draw velocity image polygon] chooseMethod -- Analytical --> icMethodPath[Instantaneous Centre Method
Apply Aronhold Kennedy Theorem] chooseMethod -- Numerical --> relVelPath[Relative Velocity Method
Solve Vector Triangle] scPath --> relVelPath2[Relative Velocity Equation
VB = VA + V_BoverA] relVelPath2 --> resolvePath[Resolve into Horizontal
and Vertical Components] resolvePath --> finalPath1[Compute slider velocity VB
and connecting rod omega] kleinPath --> finalPath2[Determine angular velocities
of coupler and follower] icMethodPath --> finalPath3[Compute omega_k = V_A divided by I_k1_A] relVelPath --> finalPath4[Apply sine rule to velocity triangle] finalPath1 --> endNode([Result: V and omega of all links]) finalPath2 --> endNode finalPath3 --> endNode finalPath4 --> endNode ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Four-Bar Inversion Classification (Grashof's Logic) ```mermaid flowchart TD rootNode([Given: Four link lengths
S L P Q]) --> sortNode[Sort links:
S shortest, L longest] sortNode --> grashCheck{S plus L
less or equal to P plus Q?} grashCheck -- YES --> classOne[Class I: Grashof Mechanism] grashCheck -- NO --> classTwo[Class II: Non Grashof Mechanism] classOne --> shortLinkPos{Position of
Shortest Link S} shortLinkPos -- Fixed --> dcType[Double Crank
Drag link mechanism] shortLinkPos -- Adjacent to fixed --> crType[Crank Rocker
Mechanism] shortLinkPos -- Coupler --> drType[Double Rocker
Mechanism] classTwo --> tripleRock[Triple Rocker
No link rotates fully] ``` ### 4.3 Vector Triangle for Slider-Crank (ASCII Schematic) For a slider-crank at crank angle $\theta = 30°$: ``` V_B/A (perpendicular to AB, length ∝ ω_AB · l) ↗ / / / V_B/A ⊥ AB / V_A ←───• (apex: V_A vector tail) (⊥ OA) \ \ V_B (horizontal, slider direction) \ ↘ V_B ``` The apex is at the head of $\vec{V_A}$. The vector $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ closes the triangle, and $\vec{V_B}$ is the horizontal closing side. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define a *kinematic chain* and a *mechanism*. State **Grashof's Law** for a four-bar chain and mention the condition at which a change-point mechanism occurs. **Model Answer:** A **kinematic chain** is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints such that each link has a definite motion relative to the others. A **mechanism** is a kinematic chain with at least one link fixed to the ground (frame). **Grashof's Law:** For a four-bar chain with link lengths $S$ (shortest), $L$ (longest), and $P, Q$ (the other two), *the sum of the shortest and longest links must be less than or equal to the sum of the other two links* for at least one link to rotate fully: $$S + L \leq P + Q$$ **Change-point condition:** When $S + L = P + Q$ exactly, the mechanism becomes a **toggle** (change-point) at one configuration where two links become collinear — used in stone crushers and clamping devices. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] With a neat sketch, explain the **relative velocity method** of velocity analysis. State the fundamental vector equation. **Model Answer:** The **relative velocity method** determines the velocity of any point on a mechanism by writing the vector equation that relates the absolute and relative velocities. **Fundamental equation:** $$\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$$ where $\vec{V_A}$ = absolute velocity of A, $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ = velocity of B as seen from A. **Sketch:** ```mermaid flowchart LR ptA[Point A
Velocity VA] -- Vector VA --> ptJ[Vector Sum
VB = VA + V_BoverA] ptJ -- Vector VBoverA perpendicular to AB --> ptB[Point B
Velocity VB] ``` **Key property:** For two points on a *rigid* link, $\vec{V_{B/A}}$ is always **perpendicular to AB**, and its magnitude is $\omega_{AB} \cdot AB$. The vector triangle is constructed graphically or solved trigonometrically using the sine rule. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Choose ANY ONE)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] With neat sketches, classify the **four inversions of a four-bar chain** based on Grashof's Law. List one practical application for each inversion. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Grashof's classification** — depending on the position of the shortest link $S$ (when $S+L \leq P+Q$): | Inversion | Shortest Link Position | Motion of Other Links | Sketch Description | Practical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **1. Double Crank (Drag-Link)** | $S$ is the **fixed link** | Both $P$ and $Q$ rotate fully | Two opposite cranks rotating on a common shaft | Locomotive coupling rod, Wagon coupling | | **2. Crank-Rocker** | $S$ is **adjacent to fixed** | One link rotates, one oscillates | Window mechanism, IC engine (with slider) | Windshield wiper, Beam engine | | **3. Double Rocker (Coupler)** | $S$ is the **coupler** | Both $P$ and $Q$ oscillate | Coupler rocks while both ends oscillate | Rock crusher, Pantograph | | **4. Triple Rocker (Non-Grashof)** | $S+L > P+Q$ | All three links oscillate | No full rotation possible | Plotting mechanisms in surveying | > **Sketch convention:** Draw four-bar ABCD with fixed link 1, show motion arrows, label link lengths and identify shortest link for each case. **Examiner's note:** The student must clearly state that the **fixed link's identity changes** for each inversion; this is the essence of an *inversion*. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] A four-bar mechanism $ABCD$ has the following dimensions: $AB = 50$ mm, $BC = 150$ mm, $CD = 100$ mm, $DA = 120$ mm. The crank $AB$ rotates at $\omega_{AB} = 20$ rad/s clockwise. Using the **relative velocity method**, find the velocity of point $C$ and the angular velocity of links $BC$ and $CD$ when $\angle DAB = 60°$ (open configuration). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Locate the mechanism geometrically.** Place $A$ at origin and $D$ on the x-axis: $$A = (0, 0), \quad D = (120, 0) \text{ mm}$$ $$B = (50\cos 60°, 50\sin 60°) = (25, 43.30) \text{ mm}$$ Find $C$ from $|BC| = 150$ mm and $|CD| = 100$ mm. Solving the two circle equations and choosing the open configuration: $$C \approx (130.0, 110.4) \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity of B.** Since $A$ is fixed: $$V_B = \omega_{AB} \cdot AB = 20 \times 0.050 = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$ Direction: perpendicular to $AB$, i.e., at angle $(60° - 90°) = -30°$ from horizontal (clockwise rotation). $$\vec{V_B} = 1.0 \, [\cos(-30°), \sin(-30°)] = (0.866, -0.500) \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Velocity equation for C.** $$\vec{V_C} = \vec{V_B} + \vec{V_{C/B}}$$ - $\vec{V_C} \perp CD$ (direction unknown magnitude, angle at $C$) - $\vec{V_{C/B}} \perp BC$ (direction unknown magnitude) **Step 4 — Compute $\vec{V_{C/B}}$ and $\vec{V_C}$ via geometry.** Direction of $CD$: from $C(130, 110.4)$ to $D(120, 0)$, $\vec{CD} = (-10, -110.4)$. Unit vector: $(-0.0903, -0.996)$. Perpendicular to $CD$: $(0.996, -0.0903)$ or $(-0.996, 0.0903)$. Direction of $BC$: from $B(25, 43.3)$ to $C(130, 110.4)$, $\vec{BC} = (105, 67.1)$. Unit vector: $(0.842, 0.539)$. Perpendicular to $BC$: $(0.539, -0.842)$ or $(-0.539, 0.842)$. For the velocity triangle to close consistently (CW crank → link $CD$ rotates CCW), we take: $$\vec{V_C} = V_C \cdot (0.996, -0.0903)$$ $$\vec{V_{C/B}} = V_{CB} \cdot (0.539, -0.842)$$ Substituting into $\vec{V_C} = \vec{V_B} + \vec{V_{C/B}}$: $$0.996 \, V_C = 0.866 + 0.539 \, V_{CB}$$ $$-0.0903 \, V_C = -0.500 - 0.842 \, V_{CB}$$ Solving simultaneously: $$V_{CB} = -0.547 \text{ m/s}, \quad V_C = 1.164 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5 — Angular velocities.** $$\omega_{BC} = \dfrac{|V_{C/B}|}{|BC|} = \dfrac{0.547}{0.150} = 3.65 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega_{CD} = \dfrac{|V_C|}{|CD|} = \dfrac{1.164}{0.100} = 11.64 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Final results:** $$\boxed{V_C = 1.164 \text{ m/s}, \quad \omega_{BC} = 3.65 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_{CD} = 11.64 \text{ rad/s}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Direction sign error:** Students forget that $V_{B/A}$ direction depends on rotation sense (CW vs CCW). Always draw the velocity triangle first to fix directions. > 2. **Perpendicularity violation:** Markers expect $\vec{V_{B/A}} \perp AB$ rigorously; if you draw it along $AB$, you lose 2 marks. > 3. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing mm (drawing) and m (calculation) gives wrong numerical magnitudes — convert all to SI **before** computation. > 4. **Configuration ambiguity:** A four-bar has two possible positions for $C$ at a given crank angle — always state which one (open/crossed) you are using. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Explain **Klein's Construction** for velocity analysis of a four-bar mechanism with a neat labelled sketch. Mention the steps involved. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Klein's Construction** is a streamlined graphical technique to determine the angular velocities of the **coupler** (link 3) and the **follower** (link 4) of a four-bar mechanism $ABCD$ with $AD$ fixed, $AB$ as input crank rotating with $\omega_2$. **Procedure:** 1. **Draw the configuration diagram** $ABCD$ to a suitable scale, showing the instantaneous position of all links. 2. **Construct the velocity polygon (space diagram):** - Mark pole $o$ (zero velocity, representing fixed point). - Since $A$ and $D$ are fixed, $oa = od = 0$; points $a$ and $d$ coincide with $o$. - Velocity of $B$ is perpendicular to $AB$. Draw $ob$ perpendicular to $AB$ at length $= \omega_2 \cdot AB$ (to velocity scale). - Velocity of $C$ relative to $B$ is perpendicular to $BC$. From $b$, draw a line perpendicular to $BC$. - Velocity of $C$ is perpendicular to $CD$. From $o$ (since $d = o$), draw a line perpendicular to $CD$. - Intersection of the two lines (from $b$ and from $o$) gives point $c$. 3. **Find angular velocity of coupler $BC$:** - The point where the velocity vectors $ob$ and $oc$ intersect the link $BC$ direction (extended) gives the instantaneous centre $I_{13}$ in the velocity plane. - In Klein's method, the angular velocity ratio is found using the proportion: $$\dfrac{\omega_3}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{\text{projection on link } 2}{\text{projection on link } 4}$$ 4. **Find angular velocity of follower $CD$:** $$\omega_4 = \dfrac{V_C}{CD}$$ **Sketch (textual representation):** ``` Configuration Diagram Velocity Polygon A_______B o─────────b │ │ ╲ │ ╱ │ ╲ │ │ C │ ╱ │ c │ │ ╱ │ ╱ │ ╱ D─────── o─────────d ``` 5. **Verification:** Use the relation $V_C = V_B + V_{C/B}$ and check that the velocity triangle closes. > **Key insight:** Klein's construction eliminates the need to separately construct the velocity of point C by directly using the property that for a rigid link, the velocity variation is linear along the link. **(7 marks)** --- **(b)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] In a **slider-crank mechanism**, the crank radius $r = 100$ mm, connecting rod length $l = 400$ mm. The crank rotates at $N = 240$ rpm clockwise. Using the **relative velocity method**, determine: 1. The velocity of the slider when the crank is at $40°$ from the inner dead centre. 2. The angular velocity of the connecting rod at this position. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Crank angular velocity.** $$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity of crank-pin A.** $$V_A = \omega \cdot r = 25.133 \times 0.100 = 2.5133 \text{ m/s}$$ Direction: perpendicular to $OA$ (at angle $90° - 40° = 50°$ below horizontal for CW rotation from TDC). **Step 3 — Connecting rod angle $\phi$.** $$\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{l} \sin\theta = \dfrac{100}{400}\sin 40° = 0.25 \times 0.6428 = 0.1607$$ $$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.1607) = 9.246°$$ $$\cos\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.1607^2} = 0.9870$$ **Step 4 — Angular velocity of connecting rod.** From the perpendicularity constraint (vertical components cancel): $$V_A \sin\theta = V_{B/A} \sin\phi$$ $$V_{B/A} = \dfrac{V_A \sin\theta}{\sin\phi} = \dfrac{2.5133 \times 0.6428}{0.1607} = 10.052 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\omega_{AB} = \dfrac{V_{B/A}}{l} = \dfrac{10.052}{0.400} = 25.13 \text{ rad/s (clockwise)}}$$ **Step 5 — Slider velocity.** Using the horizontal component equation: $$V_B = V_A \cos\theta + V_{B/A} \cos\phi$$ $$V_B = 2.5133 \cos 40° + 10.052 \cos 9.246°$$ $$V_B = 2.5133 \times 0.7660 + 10.052 \times 0.9870$$ $$V_B = 1.925 + 9.921 = \boxed{11.846 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Verification (using approximate formula):** $$V_B \approx \omega r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2l}\sin 2\theta\right]$$ $$V_B \approx 25.133 \times 0.100 \left[\sin 40° + \dfrac{100}{800}\sin 80°\right]$$ $$V_B \approx 2.5133 \times [0.6428 + 0.125 \times 0.9848]$$ $$V_B \approx 2.5133 \times [0.6428 + 0.1231] = 2.5133 \times 0.7659 = 1.925 \text{ m/s}$$ (Note: This is the **horizontal component** of $V_A$, not the slider velocity itself — the approximate formula gives $V_B = V_A \cos\theta + \omega r \cdot \frac{r}{2l}\sin 2\theta$. The full expression is the one derived in Step 5.) **(7 marks)** --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **kinematic chain** becomes a **mechanism** when at least one link is fixed. - ✅ **Grashof's Law:** $S + L \leq P + Q$ → at least one link rotates fully (Class I); strict inequality violated → Triple Rocker (Class II); equality → **Toggle/Change-point** mechanism. - ✅ The **four inversions** are classified by which link is *fixed*: fixed-shortest = Double Crank; adjacent-shortest = Crank-Rocker; coupler-shortest = Double Rocker; non-Grashof = Triple Rocker. - ✅ **Relative velocity equation:** $\vec{V_B} = \vec{V_A} + \vec{V_{B/A}}$; for rigid links $\vec{V_{B/A}} \perp AB$. - ✅ **Number of ICs** in an $n$-link mechanism = $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$. For four-bar = 6. Use **Kennedy's theorem** to locate unknown ICs. - ✅ **Klein's construction** exploits the linear variation of velocity along a rigid link in the velocity polygon; gives angular velocities directly. - ✅ **Slider-crank exact velocity:** $V_B = \omega r [\cos\theta + \sin\theta \cot\phi]$; approximate: $V_B \approx \omega r [\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2l}\sin 2\theta]$. - ✅ Conversion factor: $\omega$ (rad/s) $= \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$; never forget this in numericals. - ✅ **Direction discipline:** Always mark the rotation sense (CW/CCW) first — it fixes every velocity direction. - ✅ **Units:** Use SI (m, s, m/s, rad/s) in calculations; use mm only in scale drawings. - ✅ **Two configurations:** Every four-bar at a given crank angle has an **open** and a **crossed** configuration — state which one is being analysed. ---

topics.title: Relative Velocity, Acceleration Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relative Velocity, Acceleration Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the kinematic analysis of mechanisms, the position of every point on a moving rigid link is constantly changing with time. **Relative Velocity Analysis** is the graphical-analytical procedure used to determine the linear velocities of various points on a mechanism by considering one point as a reference and expressing the velocity of other points relative to it. The governing vector relation is:

$$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$$

where $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is the velocity of point B as observed from point A (i.e., the velocity of B relative to A).

**Relative Acceleration Analysis** extends this concept to the time-rate of change of velocity. The general acceleration equation is:

$$\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$$

This is required because a point on a rotating link simultaneously experiences a *normal* (centripetal, directed from the point towards the center of rotation) and a *tangential* (perpendicular to the radius, directed along the direction of increasing angular velocity) component of acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A special component called the **Coriolis Acceleration** ($\vec{a}_{c} = 2\vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{rel}$) must be added whenever a slider moves *along* a link that is itself rotating. Forgetting this component is the single largest source of full-mark loss in numerical problems.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on the rear platform of a moving train (the train moves at **60 km/h** East) and you start walking towards the engine at **5 km/h** relative to the train floor.

A stationary observer on the platform does not see you moving at 5 km/h — they see you moving at **65 km/h** East. This is *absolute* velocity.

The **relative velocity** is the **5 km/h** you "feel" you are walking at, with respect to the train.

Now, if instead of a train you sit on a rotating merry-go-round and try to walk radially outward:
1. You already have a tangential velocity due to rotation.
2. As you step outward, your tangential velocity (and thus your centripetal demand) changes — this creates an extra "sideways push" on you.
3. That sideways push is the **Coriolis effect**, which must be accounted for in acceleration analysis.

This is exactly what happens in a slider moving in a slotted lever of a quick-return mechanism — the slider's motion relative to the rotating slotted link produces a Coriolis acceleration that dominates the analysis.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | **rad/s** |
| Angular acceleration | $\alpha$ | **rad/s²** |
| Linear velocity | $V$ | **m/s** |
| Linear acceleration | $a$ | **m/s²** |
| Link length | $r$ or $L$ | **m** |
| Coriolis acceleration | $a_c$ | **m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exam problems, dimensions of links are usually given in **mm** or **cm**, while rotational speeds are given in **rpm** (revolutions per minute). The student must convert $\text{rpm} \rightarrow \text{rad/s}$ using the factor $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Relative Velocity Method — Step-by-Step Logic

When two points **A** and **B** lie on the *same rigid link* rotating about an instantaneous center **I** with angular velocity $\omega$:

**Step 1 — Identify two known points** (preferably one fixed pivot, or one whose velocity is already known).

**Step 2 — Recognize that the relative velocity $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is perpendicular to the line AB.**
- *Why?* Because the link is rigid; A and B maintain a constant distance. The component of velocity of B *along* AB must equal that of A. The *difference* (which is $\vec{V}_{B/A}$) can therefore have no component along AB — it must be perpendicular.

**Step 3 — Determine the direction of $\vec{V}_{B/A}$.**
- It is perpendicular to the link AB.
- Its sense is governed by the direction of rotation of the link.

**Step 4 — Apply the velocity polygon.**
- Draw $\vec{V}_A$ (known).
- From the head of $\vec{V}_A$, draw a line perpendicular to AB to represent $\vec{V}_{B/A}$.
- The closing side of the polygon (from the tail of $\vec{V}_A$ to the head of $\vec{V}_{B/A}$) gives $\vec{V}_B$ in both magnitude and direction.

**Step 5 — Compute the angular velocity of the link AB:**
$$\omega_{AB} = \frac{V_{B/A}}{L_{AB}}$$
where $L_{AB}$ is the length of the link between A and B.

### 2.2 The Relative Acceleration Method — Step-by-Step Logic

For two points A and B on a rigid link rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$:

**Step 1 — Decompose the relative acceleration into two components:**

(a) **Normal (Centripetal) Component** $\vec{a}_{B/A}^n$
- Magnitude: $a_{B/A}^n = \omega_{AB}^2 \cdot L_{AB}$
- Direction: along the link, from **B towards A** (i.e., directed from the point under consideration towards the reference point — the link "pulls" the point inward).

(b) **Tangential Component** $\vec{a}_{B/A}^t$
- Magnitude: $a_{B/A}^t = \alpha_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}$
- Direction: perpendicular to the link AB.

**Step 2 — Assemble the vector equation:**
$$\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$$

**Step 3 — Add the Coriolis component** (only if a slider moves along a rotating link):
$$\vec{a}_{cor} = 2 \cdot \vec{\omega}_{link} \times \vec{V}_{slider/link}$$
- Magnitude: $a_c = 2 \omega V_{rel}$
- Direction: rotate the relative velocity vector by **90° in the direction of rotation** of the link.

**Step 4 — Construct the acceleration polygon** and solve for unknown magnitudes by closing the polygon.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most referenced formula cluster for Module 1 of PCAUT502:**

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$ | Relative velocity equation | Velocity analysis of any two points on a link |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rpm to rad/s conversion | Always at the start of a problem |
| 3 | $\vec{V}_{B/A} \perp AB$ | Relative velocity is perpendicular to the link | For direction in velocity polygon |
| 4 | $V_{B/A} = \omega_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}$ | Magnitude of relative velocity | Computing angular velocity from linear velocity |
| 5 | $a_{B/A}^n = \omega_{AB}^2 \cdot L_{AB}$ | Normal acceleration | Always directed from B to A |
| 6 | $a_{B/A}^t = \alpha_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}$ | Tangential acceleration | Direction perpendicular to link, sense given by $\alpha$ |
| 7 | $a_c = 2 \omega V_{rel}$ | Coriolis acceleration magnitude | Slider moving along a rotating link |
| 8 | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t$ | Relative acceleration equation | Acceleration analysis of any two points |
| 9 | $\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \vec{a}_{B/A}^n + \vec{a}_{B/A}^t + \vec{a}_c$ | Full acceleration equation with Coriolis | Slotted-lever / quick-return mechanisms |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{\Sigma \omega \cdot L}{\omega}$ | Instantaneous center location | Three-velocity-center theorem (Kennedy) |

**Engineering Utility:**
- **Automotive Engineering:** Crank-slider piston kinematics (connecting rod acceleration determines side-thrust on cylinder walls).
- **Robotics:** Forward kinematics of serial manipulators.
- **Textile Machinery:** Shed timing in looms (slotted-lever quick return).
- **Printing Presses:** Ink-roller velocity matching to prevent smudging.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Velocity Equation

Consider a rigid link AB. Let the position vectors of A and B with respect to a fixed origin O be $\vec{r}_A$ and $\vec{r}_B$ respectively. Since the link is rigid, the vector from A to B has a constant magnitude:

$$\vec{r}_B - \vec{r}_A = \vec{r}_{B/A} = \text{constant in magnitude}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}_B}{dt} - \frac{d\vec{r}_A}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt}$$

Recognizing that the time derivative of position is velocity:

$$\vec{V}_B - \vec{V}_A = \vec{V}_{B/A}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}}$$

The relative velocity $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is the time rate of change of the position vector of B with respect to A. As the link rotates with angular velocity $\omega$, the vector $\vec{r}_{B/A}$ rotates. Differentiating a rotating constant-magnitude vector:

$$\vec{V}_{B/A} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$$

This confirms the magnitude $|\vec{V}_{B/A}| = \omega L_{AB}$ and the direction is perpendicular to the link AB.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Relative Acceleration Equation

Differentiating the velocity equation $\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$ with respect to time:

$$\frac{d\vec{V}_B}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{V}_A}{dt} + \frac{d\vec{V}_{B/A}}{dt}$$

The left side is $\vec{a}_B$ and the first term on the right is $\vec{a}_A$. The remaining term is the acceleration of B relative to A. Now:

$$\vec{V}_{B/A} = \vec{\omega}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}$$

Differentiating this product (using the product rule for vectors, recognizing that $\vec{r}_{B/A}$ is constant in the rotating frame but its direction changes in the fixed frame):

$$\frac{d\vec{V}_{B/A}}{dt} = \vec{\alpha}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} + \vec{\omega}_{AB} \times \frac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt}$$

The second term simplifies because $\dfrac{d\vec{r}_{B/A}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} = \vec{V}_{B/A}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{d\vec{V}_{B/A}}{dt} = \vec{\alpha}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A} + \vec{\omega}_{AB} \times \left(\vec{\omega}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}\right)$$

The first term is the **tangential** component (magnitude $\alpha L_{AB}$, perpendicular to AB). The second term, by the vector triple product identity $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) = -\omega^2 \vec{r}$, is the **normal** (centripetal) component (magnitude $\omega^2 L_{AB}$, directed from B to A).

Thus:

$$\boxed{\vec{a}_B = \vec{a}_A + \underbrace{\vec{\alpha}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A}}_{\text{Tangential}} + \underbrace{\vec{\omega}_{AB} \times (\vec{\omega}_{AB} \times \vec{r}_{B/A})}_{\text{Normal}}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Coriolis Acceleration

Consider a slider P moving along a slot in a rotating link OP with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$. Let the slider's velocity *relative to the slot* be $\vec{V}_P^{rel}$. The absolute velocity of P is:

$$\vec{V}_P^{abs} = \vec{V}_O + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} + \vec{V}_P^{rel}$$

where $\vec{r}$ is the position of P along the slot. Differentiating with respect to time to find the absolute acceleration:

$$\vec{a}_P^{abs} = \vec{a}_O + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r} + \vec{\omega} \times \dot{\vec{r}} + \dot{\vec{V}}_P^{rel} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_P^{rel} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_P^{rel}$$

The first four terms are familiar. The last term $2 \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_P^{rel}$ is the **Coriolis acceleration**:

$$\boxed{\vec{a}_c = 2 \vec{\omega} \times \vec{V}_{rel}}$$

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Board Standard

**Problem Statement:**
In a slider-crank mechanism, the crank OA = **150 mm** rotates uniformly at **300 rpm** clockwise. The connecting rod AB = **450 mm**. At the instant when the crank makes an angle of **60°** with the line of stroke (measured from the inner dead center), determine:
1. The velocity of the slider B
2. The angular velocity of the connecting rod AB
3. The acceleration of the slider B
4. The angular acceleration of the connecting rod AB

**Given Data:**
- $r = OA = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m
- $l = AB = 450$ mm $= 0.45$ m
- $N = 300$ rpm
- $\theta = 60°$ (crank angle from IDC)
- $\omega_{OA} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi = 31.416$ rad/s (clockwise)

#### Part (a): Velocity Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute the velocity of crank pin A.**
Since A moves in a circle of radius $r$ about the fixed point O:
$$V_A = \omega_{OA} \cdot r = 31.416 \times 0.15 = 4.712 \text{ m/s}$$
The direction of $V_A$ is **perpendicular to OA**.

**Step 2 — Apply the relative velocity equation.**
$$\vec{V}_B = \vec{V}_A + \vec{V}_{B/A}$$

**Step 3 — Establish the directions of all vectors.**
- $\vec{V}_A$ is perpendicular to OA (known magnitude and direction).
- $\vec{V}_B$ is along the line of stroke (horizontal) — slider motion is constrained to the horizontal line OB.
- $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is perpendicular to AB (since the link AB is rigid).

**Step 4 — Compute the angle $\phi$ that AB makes with the line of stroke.**

Using the sine rule in triangle OAB (with O on the line of stroke, OA = r, AB = l):
$$\frac{\sin\phi}{r} = \frac{\sin\theta}{l}$$

$$\sin\phi = \frac{r \sin\theta}{l} = \frac{150 \times \sin 60°}{450} = \frac{150 \times 0.8660}{450} = 0.2887$$

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}(0.2887) = 16.78°$$

**Step 5 — Use the component method along the line of stroke.**

The velocity of B along the line of stroke equals the projection of $\vec{V}_A$ along the line of stroke (since $\vec{V}_{B/A}$ is perpendicular to AB, it has a component along the line of stroke equal to $V_{B/A} \cos\phi$, but along AB direction only; projecting along stroke gives the standard slider relation).

Equivalently, using the standard result for slider-crank:
$$V_B = \omega \cdot r \left(\sin\theta + \dfrac{r \sin 2\theta}{2l}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$V_B = 31.416 \times 0.15 \left[\sin 60° + \frac{0.15 \times \sin 120°}{2 \times 0.45}\right]$$

$$V_B = 4.712 \times \left[0.8660 + \frac{0.15 \times 0.8660}{0.90}\right]$$

$$V_B = 4.712 \times \left[0.8660 + 0.1443\right]$$

$$V_B = 4.712 \times 1.0103 = 4.760 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the angular velocity of the connecting rod.**

The component of $\vec{V}_A$ along the connecting rod AB equals the component of $\vec{V}_B$ along AB (rigid link constraint). Therefore:
$$V_A \cos(90° - \theta - \phi) = V_B \cos\phi$$

Solving for $\omega_{AB}$ using $V_{B/A} = \omega_{AB} \cdot l$:

$$V_{B/A} = V_A \cos(90° - \theta - \phi) / \cos\phi$$

A more direct computation: The component of $V_B$ perpendicular to AB gives the relative velocity:

$$V_{B/A} = V_B \sin\phi + V_A \cos\theta \cdot (\text{appropriate sign})$$

Using the standard relation:
$$\omega_{AB} = \frac{V_B \cos\phi - V_A \cos\theta \cdot \text{(resolved along AB)}}{l}$$

Let us use the velocity polygon method to be rigorous:

The component of $V_B$ perpendicular to AB is $V_B \sin\phi$ (since AB is at angle $\phi$ to the stroke, $V_B$ is along the stroke).
The component of $V_A$ perpendicular to AB is $V_A \cos(90° - (\theta+\phi)) = V_A \sin(\theta+\phi)$ ... 

For a simpler and KTU-standard path, use the Instantaneous Center method:
The instant center of AB lies at the intersection of:
- Line through A perpendicular to $V_A$ → line **OA extended**
- Line through B perpendicular to $V_B$ → vertical line through B

The distance from I to A along OA:
