- **Where it appears in mechanisms:** Quick-return mechanisms (Whitworth, crank-slotted lever), rotary IC engines, planetary gear trains.
- **Total acceleration of slider in a rotating link:** $a_{abs} = a_r + \alpha r + \omega^2 r + 2\omega v_r$ (vector sum of all four terms).
- **Units:** SI: $\text{m/s}^2$; FPS: $\text{ft/s}^2$.
- **KTU weightage:** 3 to 7 marks per ESE; commonly tested as a 3-mark direct question in Part A and as a 7-mark sub-part in Part B (especially with a numerical from crank-slotted lever or Whitworth mechanism).
- **Examiner's pet trick:** A question will give you $\omega$ and $v_r$ and ask only for $a_c$ — students who panic and start drawing full velocity/acceleration diagrams for a 3-marker lose 2 marks for "over-effort." For pure Coriolis questions, the formula is sufficient.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminology and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Terminology and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why "Terminology and Classification" is the Foundation Stone

Before we can analyse the velocity, acceleration, or force transmission of any mechanical system (four-bar, slider-crank, cam-follower, gear train, etc.), we must first speak its language. The KTU Module 2 opens with a strict vocabulary: *link, pair, chain, mechanism, machine, degree of freedom, inversion*. Every numerical problem that follows in Modules 2, 3, and 4 rests on these definitions — so this topic is the **"grammar"** of the entire course.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Examiner's Mantra:** *"If the student cannot correctly classify a kinematic pair or compute mobility, every later question — slider-crank analysis, inversion synthesis, flywheel design — collapses. So examiners routinely test this topic with 14-mark questions disguised as 'simple' descriptive ones."*

---

## 1.2 Formal KTU Definitions (Syllabus-Critical)

### 🔹 1. Kinematic Link (or Element)
A **kinematic link** is a rigid body (or an assembly of rigid bodies) which does not undergo any deformation under the action of forces. It must possess **two nodes (connection points)** to participate in a mechanism, and is considered the *fundamental building block* of any machine.

### 🔹 2. Kinematic Pair
A **kinematic pair** is a joint between two kinematic links that permits **relative motion** between them in a constrained manner. Each pair is characterised by:
- The *nature of contact* (lower or higher pair)
- The *type of relative motion* allowed (sliding, rolling, revolute, etc.)
- The *number of degrees of freedom* it permits

### 🔹 3. Kinematic Chain
A **kinematic chain** is an assembly of kinematic links connected by kinematic pairs. It is a *closed loop* (usually) of links with no fixed link identified yet.

### 🔹 4. Mechanism
A **mechanism** is a kinematic chain in which **one link is fixed (grounded)** and the relative motion of all other links is completely determined by the motion of one driving link.

### 🔹 5. Machine
A **machine** is a mechanism (or combination of mechanisms) that transmits and transforms motion so as to perform **useful mechanical work** against a resisting load.

### 🔹 6. Degree of Freedom (DOF) / Mobility (M)
The **degree of freedom (M)** of a mechanism is the **minimum number of independent inputs** (coordinates or drivers) required to completely specify the configuration of every link of the mechanism at any instant.

### 🔹 7. Inversion
An **inversion** is a new mechanism obtained by *fixing a different link* of the original kinematic chain. The relative motion among the links remains identical — only the *frame of reference* changes.

### 🔹 8. Gruebler's Equation / Kutzbach Criterion
The mathematical rule used to compute the mobility of a planar mechanism having rigid links and one-DOF lower pairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for quick recall:** *"Link → Pair → Chain → Mechanism → Machine"* is the **logical hierarchy**. Each higher-level entity is built from the previous one. A machine = mechanism + energy transformation; a mechanism = kinematic chain + fixed link.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm as a Mechanism"

Imagine your own **right arm**:
- *Upper arm, forearm, palm, fingers* = kinematic **links** (rigid bones).
- *Shoulder joint, elbow joint, wrist joint, knuckle joints* = kinematic **pairs** (revolute / sliding joints).
- The whole arm without fixing anything to a wall = a **kinematic chain**.
- Now *clamp your shoulder to a wall* (fix a link) and you have a **mechanism** — the position of your fingertips is fully determined by the angles you give at your shoulder and elbow.
- Add a *bicep muscle doing work* to lift a dumbbell — it becomes a **machine**.
- If you instead fix your *palm* to a wall and let your shoulder + elbow swing — that's an **inversion** of the same chain producing a totally different output motion.

This analogy makes the entire terminology permanently memorable.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Number of links | $n$ | 3 – 8 in standard problems |
| Lower pair count | $j_1$ | 1-DOF pairs (revolute, prismatic) |
| Higher pair count | $j_2$ | 2-DOF pairs (cam-follower, gear mesh) |
| Mobility / DOF | $M$ | Usually **1** for a determinate mechanism |
| Relative velocity magnitude | **bold v** | Vector quantity |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Concepts — Why Each Step Matters

### 🔸 Step 1 — Recognise the **Link**
A link is rigid *by definition*. A connecting rod or a crank is a single link even though it contains features (holes, bearings, webs). A *cushion of a hydraulic cylinder* is **NOT** a link because it deforms — it stores energy.

### 🔸 Step 2 — Identify the **Kinematic Pair** Type
Each pair is classified along **three axes** simultaneously:

**Axis-A: Nature of Contact**
- **Lower Pair (Surface / Area contact)** → 1 DOF, low wear, high load capacity. Examples: revolute, prismatic, cylindrical, spherical, helical, planar.
- **Higher Pair (Point / Line contact)** → 2 DOF, high wear, high precision. Examples: ball bearing rolling, cam-follower, gear teeth mesh.

**Axis-B: Type of Relative Motion**
- Sliding (Prismatic), Turning (Revolute), Rolling, Screw (Helical), Spherical, etc.

**Axis-C: Type of Closure**
- **Self-closed**: held together by gravity or geometry alone (e.g., a ball in a socket).
- **Force-closed**: requires an external force (spring) to maintain contact (e.g., cam-follower held by a spring).

### 🔸 Step 3 — Build the **Kinematic Chain**
A chain is *closed* if the first and last links are joined by a pair. For DOF analysis we prefer *closed* chains because every link then has well-defined relative motion.

### 🔸 Step 4 — Pick the **Fixed Link → Mechanism**
Fixing one link removes the ground's relative motion, turning the chain into a mechanism. If we fix *another* link instead → **inversion**.

### 🔸 Step 5 — Compute **Mobility (M)**
This is the *heart of the topic* — without M we cannot decide whether a given chain is even a *working mechanism* (must have $M \ge 1$), is *locked* ($M = 0$), or is a *redundant/over-constrained structure* ($M > 1$ — needs extra drivers or passive DOF such as idler rolls).

### 🔸 Step 6 — Verify **Grashof Condition** (preview of Module 3)
For four-bar chains: $S + L \le P + Q$ where $S$ = shortest, $L$ = longest, $P,Q$ = others.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | **Kutzbach Criterion** for planar mechanisms with single-DOF lower pairs | Planar |
| 2 | $M = 3N - 2P_1 - P_2$ | Alternative form: $N$ = moving links, $P_1$ = 1-DOF pairs, $P_2$ = 2-DOF pairs | Planar |
| 3 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | **Grübler Spatial Equation** | Spatial |
| 4 | DOF per pair type | Lower pair → 1 DOF; Higher pair → 2 DOF | Both |
| 5 | $j_1 + 2j_2 = 3n - 2$ | Rearranged Kutzbach for $M=1$ (commonly used to verify a "good" mechanism) | Planar |
| 6 | $\sum F_i = 3n$ | Total freedoms = $3n$ for $n$ unconstrained rigid bodies in plane | Planar |
| 7 | $\sum C_i = 2j_1 + j_2$ | Total constraints imposed by joints | Planar |

### 2.2.1 Quick Derivation Logic of Kutzbach Criterion

Each rigid body in a plane has **3 DOF** (two translations + one rotation). So $n$ *unconstrained* links would have $3n$ DOF.

Every 1-DOF lower pair removes **2 DOF** (because it imposes 2 constraints — e.g., a revolute joint removes 2 translations and allows 1 rotation, hence 2 constraints).

A 2-DOF higher pair removes only **1 DOF** (1 constraint).

Subtracting all constraints from the total freedom:

$$M = 3n - 2j_1 - 1\cdot j_2$$

But we usually subtract the *fixed link's* freedom too. The standard textbook form uses $(n-1)$ for the *moving* links while including the fixed link in $n$. Hence:

$$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$

### 2.2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robot manipulator design**: Mobile DOF of a serial arm = sum of DOF of each joint.
- **Suspension system of a car**: Designers verify $M = 1$ so the wheel follows the road deterministically.
- **Foldable furniture / deployable structures**: Mobility analysis predicts how the system unfolds.
- **Synthesis of new mechanisms**: Chebychev–Grashof synthesis of four-bars uses Grashof inequality directly.
- **Industrial automation**: Pick-and-place mechanisms must satisfy $M \ge 1$ and avoid *redundant DOF* which would cause uncontrolled motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick rule for KTU:** If $M = 0$ → **structure / frame** (no motion). If $M = 1$ → **good mechanism**. If $M = 2$ → **needs 2 drivers**. If $M < 0$ → **preloaded / over-constrained** (statically indeterminate structure).

---

## 2.3 Complete Classification Table — Kinematic Pairs (Must Memorise)

| Contact | Motion Type | Joint Name | DOF | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surface | Sliding | **Prismatic (Sliding)** | 1 | Piston in cylinder |
| Surface | Turning | **Revolute (Hinge)** | 1 | Door hinge, crankshaft journal |
| Surface | Combined slide + turn | **Cylindrical** | 2 | Footstep bearing |
| Surface | Screw motion | **Helical (Screw)** | 1 | Lead screw, nut-bolt |
| Surface | Ball-in-socket | **Spherical** | 3 | Ball-and-socket joint |
| Surface | Plane-on-plane | **Planar (Flat)** | 3 | Slider on a flat table |
| Point/Line | Rolling / sliding | **Higher pair (Cam, Gear)** | 2 | Cam-follower, gear mesh |

---

## 2.4 Classification of Mechanisms (Module 2 Overview)

1. **Simple Mechanisms** — four-bar chain, slider-crank chain, double-slider chain.
2. **Compound Mechanisms** — combination of two or more simple mechanisms sharing a common link.
3. **Complex Mechanisms** — for higher pairs (e.g., Geneva wheel, cam-follower).
4. **Spatial Mechanisms** — operate in 3D (robot arms, RSSR spatial four-bar).
5. **Planar Mechanisms** — all links move in parallel planes.

---

## 2.5 Why "Degree of Freedom" is the *Single Most Tested* Quantity

A 14-mark KTU board question on this topic almost always ends with: *"Compute the DOF of the mechanism shown."* The student must:
1. Count links $n$.
2. Classify every joint (lower vs higher).
3. Apply the correct equation.
4. **Conclude** whether $M=1$ (working mechanism), $M=0$ (structure), or $M>1$ (needs extra driver).
5. If a *redundant DOF* exists (e.g., rollers on a plate), subtract it explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Kutzbach Criterion

We shall derive *every line* so a KTU student can reproduce the equation on the answer script verbatim without memorising a formula card.

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumption

Consider $n$ rigid links, all free in a plane. Each link, in 2-D, possesses **3 degrees of freedom**:
- translation along $x$-axis,
- translation along $y$-axis,
- rotation about the $z$-axis.

So total freedom without any constraint:

$$\text{Total freedom of } n \text{ free rigid bodies in plane} = 3n$$

### 3.1.2 Effect of Adding a Joint

When two links are joined, the joint imposes *constraints*. The number of constraints a joint removes from the system equals the **number of relative freedoms that are eliminated**.

**Case A — Lower Pair (1 DOF joint):**
A revolute joint allows **only** rotation, killing 2 relative DOF. Hence a lower pair imposes **2 constraints**.

**Case B — Higher Pair (2 DOF joint):**
A cam-follower allows rolling + sliding (2 DOF), killing only 1 relative DOF. Hence a higher pair imposes **1 constraint**.

### 3.1.3 Total Constraints

If $j_1$ lower pairs and $j_2$ higher pairs are present, total constraints:

$$C_{\text{total}} = 2j_1 + j_2$$

### 3.1.4 First Form of the Equation

Mobility = total freedom of free links − constraints imposed by all joints:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3n - (2j_1 + j_2) \\
  &= 3n - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}$$

This equation, however, treats the **ground (fixed link)** as a moving link. We must subtract its 3 freedoms because it is not actually moving. So we replace $n$ with $(n-1)$ where $n$ now *includes* the fixed link:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3n - 3 - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical Kutzbach equation** for planar mechanisms with single-DOF lower pairs.

### 3.1.5 Rearrangement for Verification

For a *good mechanism* ($M = 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
2j_1 + j_2 &= 3(n-1) - 1 \\
2j_1 + j_2 &= 3n - 4
\end{aligned}$$

Or equivalently $j_1 + 2j_2 = 3n - 2$ (alternative rearrangement). This single line is often *asked verbatim* in 3-mark short questions.

### 3.1.6 Spatial Extension (Grübler)

In 3-D, a rigid body has 6 DOF (3 translations + 3 rotations). A joint of class $i$ (where $i$ = number of DOF it *retains*) imposes $(6 - i)$ constraints. Therefore, for joints retaining 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 DOF:

$$M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$$

where $j_i$ = number of joints of class $i$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Four-Bar Chain

A planar four-bar mechanism has 4 links (1 fixed + 3 moving) joined by 4 revolute joints.

Given:
- $n = 4$
- $j_1 = 4$ (all revolute → 1-DOF lower pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$

Apply Kutzbach:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
  &= 3(3) - 8 \\
  &= 9 - 8 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **Result: $M = 1$ — Deterministic mechanism. One input (driver crank) gives a fully-defined output (rocker/follower).**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Slider-Crank Mechanism

Links: fixed cylinder, piston, connecting rod, crankshaft → $n = 4$ (including ground).
Pairs: 3 revolute (crank-rod, rod-piston, piston-cylinder axis... wait — actually piston-cylinder is **prismatic**).

Re-examination:
- 1 — Revolute (crank-ground)
- 2 — Revolute (crank-rod)
- 3 — Revolute (rod-piston)
- 4 — Prismatic (piston-ground)

So $j_1 = 4$ (all 1-DOF lower pairs), $j_2 = 0$, $n = 4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \\
  &= 9 - 8 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

✅ $M = 1$. One input (crank rotation) → piston reciprocation determined.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Mechanism With a Higher Pair (Cam-Follower)

Cam (rotating) and follower (reciprocating), plus a frame and a roller on the follower.

Let us take:
- Cam, follower, roller, frame → $n = 4$.
- Revolute (cam-ground): $j_1$ part.
- Cam-follower contact: **higher pair** ($j_2$).
- Roller-follower revolute: $j_1$.
- Follower-frame prismatic: $j_1$.

So: $j_1 = 3$, $j_2 = 1$, $n = 4$.

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 1(1) \\
  &= 9 - 6 - 1 \\
  &= 2
\end{aligned}$$

⚠️ $M = 2$? But a normal cam-follower is driven by 1 input. The extra DOF is the *rotation of the roller* itself — a **passive / idler DOF** that does not affect output.

**Correction**: Subtract idler DOF:

$$M_{\text{effective}} = 2 - 1 = 1$$

✅ This is why examiners test this trap.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pair → DOF → Constraint Map

| Pair Type | Symbol | DOF Retained | Constraints Imposed | Notation in Kutzbach |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Revolute | $R$ | 1 | 2 | $j_1$ (lower) |
| Prismatic | $P$ | 1 | 2 | $j_1$ (lower) |
| Helical / Screw | $H$ | 1 | 2 | $j_1$ (lower) |
| Cylindrical | $C$ | 2 | 1 | counts half as lower pair |
| Spherical | $S$ | 3 | 3 | $j_3$ in spatial form |
| Planar (Flat) | $F$ | 3 | 3 | $j_3$ in spatial form |
| Rolling cam | — | 2 | 1 | $j_2$ (higher) |
| Gear mesh | — | 2 | 1 | $j_2$ (higher) |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example #4 — Spatial RSSR Mechanism

Robot arm joint with 4 links including ground, joints: Revolute (R), Revolute (R), Spherical (S), Revolute (R) → but let us take $n=4$, $j_1 = 2$ (revolutes), $j_3 = 1$ (spherical — 3 DOF joint), $j_1$ again = 1.

Apply **Grübler spatial** equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
  &= 6(3) - 5(3) - 3(1) \\
  &= 18 - 15 - 3 \\
  &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Result $M = 0$ indicates a **structure** unless one counts the spherical as providing an extra DOF in the formula. (This shows the importance of correct joint counting.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hierarchy of Kinematic Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Body] --> B[Kinematic Link]
    B --> C{Kinematic Pair}
    C -->|Surface Contact| D[Lower Pair - 1 DOF]
    C -->|Point or Line Contact| E[Higher Pair - 2 DOF]
    D --> F[Revolute]
    D --> G[Prismatic]
    D --> H[Helical Screw]
    D --> I[Spherical]
    E --> J[Cam Follower]
    E --> K[Gear Mesh]
    B --> L[Kinematic Chain]
    L --> M{Fix One Link}
    M -->|Yes| N[Mechanism]
    M -->|No| O[Free Chain]
    N --> P{Add Energy Source}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Machine]
    P -->|No| R[Pure Mechanism]
    N --> S{Compute Mobility M}
    S -->|M equals 0| T[Structure]
    S -->|M equals 1| U[Good Mechanism]
    S -->|M greater than 1| V[Needs Extra Driver]
    N --> W[Change Fixed Link]
    W --> X[Inverted Mechanism]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Kinematic Pairs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KP[Kinematic Pair] --> NOC{Nature of Contact}
    NOC --> LP[Lower Pair]
    NOC --> HP[Higher Pair]
    LP --> R[Revolute]
    LP --> PR[Prismatic]
    LP --> SC[Screw Helical]
    LP --> CY[Cylindrical]
    LP --> SP[Spherical]
    LP --> PL[Planar Flat]
    HP --> CAM[Cam Follower]
    HP --> GR[Gear Pair]
    HP --> BR[Ball Bearing Roller]
    LP --> CT{Closure Type}
    CT --> SCF[Self Closed]
    CT --> FCF[Force Closed]
    HP --> CT2{Closure Type}
    CT2 --> FCF2[Mostly Force Closed]
```

## 4.3 Mobility Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start With Chain] --> CN[Count n links including fixed]
    CN --> CP[Count Joints]
    CP --> CL[Lower Pair Count j1]
    CP --> CH[Higher Pair Count j2]
    CL --> AP[Apply Kutzbach M equals 3 of n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    CH --> AP
    AP --> CHK{M equals what}
    CHK -->|Zero| RES1[Structure or Frame]
    CHK -->|One| RES2[Good Deterministic Mechanism]
    CHK -->|Greater than One| RES3[Redundant DOF - Subtract Idler DOF]
    CHK -->|Less than Zero| RES4[Over Constrained Preloaded]
    RES3 --> AP2{Recheck With Idler Subtracted}
    AP2 -->|M equals 1| RES2
```

## 4.4 Inversion Concept — Four-Bar Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FB[Four Bar Kinematic Chain] --> F1[Fix Ground - Crank Rocker]
    FB --> F2[Fix Shortest Link - Double Crank]
    FB --> F3[Fix Link Adjacent to Shortest - Double Rocker]
    FB --> F4[Fix Opposite Link to Shortest - Drag Link Mechanism]
    FB --> F5[Fix Coupler - Parallel Motion Bennett Type]
```

## 4.5 Schematic of a Planar Four-Bar Linkage (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 4
    block:GROUND["Fixed Link Frame"]:1
    block:CRANK["Input Crank R - 1 DOF"]:1
    block:COUPLER["Coupler Link"]:1
    block:ROCKER["Output Rocker R"]:1
    GROUND -->|"Revolute j1"| CRANK
    CRANK -->|"Revolute j1"| COUPLER
    COUPLER -->|"Revolute j1"| ROCKER
    ROCKER -->|"Revolute j1"| GROUND
```

## 4.6 Schematic of Slider-Crank (Block View)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:FRAME2["Fixed Cylinder"]:1
    block:CRANK2["Crank Shaft R"]:1
    block:ROD["Connecting Rod"]:1
    block:PISTON["Slider Piston P"]:1
    FRAME2 -->|"Revolute j1"| CRANK2
    CRANK2 -->|"Revolute j1"| ROD
    ROD -->|"Revolute j1"| PISTON
    PISTON -->|"Prismatic j1"| FRAME2
```

# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🎯 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Define the following with one example each:**
(a) Kinematic Pair
(b) Kinematic Chain
(c) Mechanism

### 📝 Model Answer:

**(a) Kinematic Pair:**
A kinematic pair is a joint between two kinematic links that permits *constrained relative motion* between them.
> *Example:* A revolute joint between the connecting rod and the piston pin in an IC engine allows only rotation — relative motion is constrained.

**(b) Kinematic Chain:**
A kinematic chain is an assembly of several kinematic links connected by kinematic pairs such that the relative motion between any two links can be determined.
> *Example:* The piston-connecting rod-crankshaft assembly before fixing any link is a kinematic chain.

**(c) Mechanism:**
A mechanism is a kinematic chain in which **one link is fixed** (grounded) and the motion of every other link is fully determined by the motion of one driving link.
> *Example:* The slider-crank mechanism of an IC engine, where the cylinder is the fixed link and the crank is the driver.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between a lower pair and a higher pair. Give two examples of each.**

### 📝 Model Answer:

| Feature | Lower Pair | Higher Pair |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of contact | Surface / area contact | Point or line contact |
| DOF | 1 (most cases) | 2 |
| Load capacity | High (less wear) | Low (high wear) |
| Precision | Low | High |
| Examples | (i) Revolute joint (door hinge) (ii) Prismatic joint (piston-cylinder) | (i) Cam-follower pair (ii) Gear teeth mesh |

> *Exam Tip:* Always mention contact type, DOF, and one engineering example in the difference table — that guarantees full marks under KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.2 🎯 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **four-bar kinematic chain** and derive the **Kutzbach criterion** for the mobility of a planar mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A mechanism consists of **6 links and 7 lower pairs**. Determine its **mobility** and state whether it is a structure, mechanism, or needs extra driver. **(7 Marks)**

### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Sketch description.**
A four-bar kinematic chain consists of four rigid links: *fixed link (ground)*, *input crank*, *coupler*, and *output rocker*. They are connected by four revolute pairs, all of which are 1-DOF lower pairs.

**Step 2: Starting assumption — DOF of free rigid bodies.**
Each rigid body in a plane has 3 DOF (2 translations + 1 rotation). Hence for $n$ free bodies the total DOF is $3n$.

**Step 3: Effect of joints.**
A 1-DOF lower pair imposes 2 constraints.
A 2-DOF higher pair imposes 1 constraint.

**Step 4: Number of constraints removed.**
Total constraints: $C = 2j_1 + j_2$.

**Step 5: Net mobility.**

$$M = 3n - (2j_1 + j_2)$$

**Step 6: Subtracting ground freedom.**
If the fixed link is included in $n$, subtract its 3 DOF:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3n - 2j_1 - j_2 - 3
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Kutzbach criterion** for planar mechanisms.

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $n = 6$ (links including ground)
- $j_1 = 7$ (lower pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$

**Apply Kutzbach equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(6-1) - 2(7) - 0 \\
  &= 15 - 14 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:**
Since $M = 1$, the assembly is a **good deterministic mechanism**. A single input (driver) completely determines the position of every other link.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is a **kinematic pair**? Classify kinematic pairs based on **(i) nature of contact, (ii) type of relative motion, and (iii) type of closure**. Give one engineering example of each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cam-follower mechanism has **4 links, 3 revolute pairs, 1 prismatic pair, and 1 higher pair** (between cam and follower). Determine its mobility. If a **roller is mounted on the follower (with 1 extra revolute pair)**, find the corrected mobility. **(7 Marks)**

### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** A kinematic pair is a joint between two links that allows *constrained relative motion* between them.

**Classification:**

| Basis | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Nature of contact | Lower pair (surface contact) | Piston in cylinder |
|  | Higher pair (point/line contact) | Cam-follower contact |
| (ii) Type of relative motion | Sliding pair (prismatic) | Cross-slide of a milling machine |
|  | Turning pair (revolute) | Door hinge |
|  | Rolling pair | Ball bearing |
|  | Screw pair | Lead screw of a lathe |
| (iii) Type of closure | Self-closed pair | Ball in a socket |
|  | Force-closed pair | Cam-follower with spring |

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Without roller:**
- $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ (3 revolute + 1 prismatic), $j_2 = 1$

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 1(1) \\
  &= 9 - 8 - 1 \\
  &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

This says *structure*, which is wrong for a cam-follower. The error is missing the *roller's passive DOF*.

**With roller mounted (correct case):**
- $n = 5$ (add roller as a separate link)
- $j_1 = 5$ (3 R + 1 P + 1 R between roller and follower)
- $j_2 = 1$ (cam-follower)

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(5-1) - 2(5) - 1(1) \\
  &= 12 - 10 - 1 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

✅ Now $M = 1$. The extra link is an **idler** — its only DOF is the roller's spin, which does not affect output motion but must be subtracted as a passive DOF.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the fixed link in $n$** → wrong $n$ → cascading error.
> 2. **Mis-classifying a spherical or cylindrical joint as a simple lower pair** → wrong $j_1$ count.
> 3. **Ignoring the roller's passive DOF in cam-follower problems** → you will compute $M = 0$ and lose 4–6 marks.
> 4. **Writing "mechanism" without justifying the value of $M$** → examiners expect explicit numerical calculation.
> 5. **Using the spatial Grübler equation for a 2-D diagram** → wrong formula → full marks lost.
> 6. **Not stating units / units missing in Kutzbach-derived DOF** — DOF is *dimensionless*; stating "units = 1" is unnecessary and wastes time.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Hierarchy**: *Rigid Body → Link → Pair → Chain → Mechanism → Machine*.
- ✅ **Lower Pair = Surface Contact = 1 DOF (revolute, prismatic, screw, cylindrical, spherical, planar).**
- ✅ **Higher Pair = Point/Line Contact = 2 DOF (cam-follower, gear mesh, ball-bearing rolling).**
- ✅ **Kutzbach Criterion (Planar):** $\;M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- ✅ **Grübler Spatial Equation:** $\;M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- ✅ **For $M = 1$ mechanism:** $j_1 + 2j_2 = 3n - 2$ is the verification identity.
- ✅ **Inversion** = same chain, *different* fixed link → *different* output motion (no change in relative motion among links).
- ✅ **Idler / Redundant DOF** must be subtracted when an extra link has free spin (e.g., cam roller).
- ✅ **$M = 0$ → structure (no motion).** $M = 1$ → working mechanism. $M > 1$ → needs extra drivers or indicates redundant DOF. $M < 0$ → over-constrained.
- ✅ **Always identify** the *fixed link* and *driver* before computing $M$.
- ✅ **Quick exam mnemonic for pair DOFs:** *"Rev = 1, Prism = 1, Screw = 1, Cyld = 2, Sph = 3, Flat = 3"* — Surface pairs from low to high DOF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Roughly **25–30% of the 100-mark Theory of Machines paper** touches this topic directly (definitions + Kutzbach calculation), and *another 30–40%* depends on it indirectly (every mechanism analysis begins by stating links, pairs, and DOF). Master this module = master the foundation of the whole course.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — GOVERNORS AND CAMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Governors

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard Terminology):** A *governor* is a mechanical device used to automatically regulate the **mean speed** of an engine (or motor) by varying the supply of working fluid (fuel/steam) with the load, without the continuous attention of a human operator.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Think of a governor like a *cruise control* in a car — but in a mechanical, 18th-century style. Imagine a spinning merry-go-round with weights tied to ropes. As the carousel spins faster, the weights fly outward; as it slows, they fall inward. By connecting these weights to a fuel valve, the engine "feeds" itself more fuel when load drops (speed rises) and chokes off fuel when load increases (speed drops). The **centrifugal effect** of rotating masses is therefore the *heart* of every centrifugal governor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Purpose of a Governor:** To maintain **mean speed** nearly constant between the no-load and full-load conditions, *not* to correct instantaneous speed fluctuations (that is the job of a *flywheel*).

**Physical Constant used throughout this module:**

$$g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2} \quad \textbf{(Standard acceleration due to gravity)}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}\ \text{rad/s}, \quad \textbf{where}\ N\ \text{is in rpm}$$

---

### 1.2 Cams

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *cam* is a rotating machine element having a curved outline (or a curved groove) that imparts a prescribed motion (displacement, velocity, and acceleration) to a *follower* — a piece that remains in contact with the cam by gravity, spring, or positive drive.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A cam is like a *musical drummer* whose hands hit a drum (the follower) at different intensities as the drum is rotated. The cam's shape (its "groove" or "profile") determines exactly *how* the follower moves — up, down, fast, slow, momentarily at rest. The follower is the "drumstick's destination" that rises and falls in synchrony with the cam's rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cams are Crucial in KTU:** They are the backbone of internal combustion engines (valve actuation), automatic lathes, printing machines, and textile machinery — anywhere a complex, non-uniform motion is required from a uniform rotational input.

**Terminology Vocabulary (KTU Expected):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Base Circle** | Smallest circle that just touches the cam profile |
| **Trace Point** | Point on the follower whose motion defines the follower displacement |
| **Pitch Curve** | Locus of the trace point for a roller follower |
| **Pressure Angle (α)** | Angle between the direction of follower motion and the normal to the pitch curve at the point of contact |
| **Prime Circle** | Circle through the pitch curve that is concentric with the base circle |

---

## 2. Types of Governors — A Bird's-Eye View

| Governor | Key Feature | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Watt** | Two-ball centrifugal, *no* sleeve; arms are simply hinged | Low-speed, historical |
| **Porter** | Watt + central **sleeve** loaded by its own weight | Stationary steam engines |
| **Proell** | Balls on *upper* arms, sleeve weight on a link | Higher sensitivity than Porter |
| **Hartnell** | Bell-crank lever + **spring** loading | High-speed, precise control |
| **Hartung** | Two bell cranks, *intermediate* ball pivots | Very high speed (turbines) |

> [!NOTE]
> All centrifugal governors operate on the principle: $\textbf{Centrifugal Force on ball} \propto \omega^{2} r$, balanced against a *controlling force* (gravity of sleeve or spring tension).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Watt Governor

### Operating Principle
The **Watt governor** (1788) is the simplest centrifugal governor. Two balls are attached to a vertical spindle by arms, and the spindle rotates about a vertical axis. The balls *fly outward* against gravity as speed increases.

### Free-Body Diagram Logic (Steps)

- Let $m$ = mass of each ball, $r$ = radius of ball centre from spindle axis, $\theta$ = angle of arm with vertical.
- The ball moves in a horizontal circle of radius $r$ at angular speed $\omega$.
- The only forces on the ball are its weight $mg$ (vertical down) and the tension $T$ in the arm (along the arm).
- Vertical equilibrium: $T \cos\theta = mg$
- Horizontal (centrifugal) equilibrium: $T \sin\theta = m\omega^{2} r$
- Dividing: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega^{2} r}{g}$

### Height of the Watt Governor
For the balls at radius $r$, the height $h$ of the cone formed by the arms is:

$$h = \frac{r}{\tan\theta} = \frac{g}{\omega^{2}} = \frac{894}{N^{2}}\ \text{metres}\quad \text{(when N is in rpm)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU):** The Watt governor is **not isochronous** — the equilibrium speed *depends* on the ball radius. This is its main drawback. In modern engines, the Watt governor has been entirely superseded by the Porter, Proell, and Hartnell types.

---

## 2.2 The Porter Governor

### Why Porter Improved Watt
Watt's governor had no mechanism to control the *fuel supply* — it merely spun the balls. **Charles Porter (1870s)** added a **central sleeve** mounted on the spindle. The sleeve carries an additional weight and is connected to the throttle valve. As the sleeve rises or falls, it opens/closes the throttle.

### Free-Body of One Ball

Let:
- $m$ = mass of one ball
- $M$ = mass of sleeve
- $\alpha$ = angle of upper arm with vertical
- $\beta$ = angle of lower arm with vertical
- $r$ = radius of ball centre
- $T_1$ = tension in upper arm, $T_2$ = tension in lower arm

Vertical equilibrium (assuming both arms meet at a point on the sleeve axis):

$$T_1 \cos\alpha + T_2 \cos\beta = mg + F_{sleeve,\,component}$$

In the standard **Porter** configuration, the arms are symmetric ($\alpha = \beta$), and we obtain:

$$T_1 = T_2 = T$$

The vertical equilibrium of the ball + sleeve system yields:

$$W_{sleeve} + 2mg = 2T \cos\alpha + 2T \cos\alpha = 2T(\cos\alpha + \cos\alpha)$$

For the case $\alpha = \beta$ and equal arms:

$$W = 2T \sin\alpha \cdot \cot\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{W = 2T \sin\alpha}$$

where $W$ = total downward force on sleeve axis (sleeve weight + vertical components of ball weights).

### Including Sleeve Friction

> [!NOTE]
> When the sleeve has friction $F$ (force on the spindle due to friction in the guides), the equilibrium becomes:
> $$W \pm F = 2T \sin\alpha$$
> The **+ sign** corresponds to the sleeve *rising* (overspeed); the **− sign** to it *falling* (underspeed). This gives two *different* equilibrium speeds for the same sleeve position — a measure of **hunting**.

### Equilibrium Speed
Combining $T \sin\alpha = m\omega^{2} r$ with $W = 2T \sin\alpha$:

$$\boxed{\omega^{2} = \frac{W \cdot \tan\alpha}{2m \cdot r \cdot \sec\alpha} = \frac{W}{2m\,r}\left(\frac{\tan\alpha}{\sec\alpha}\right)}$$

Or, in the standard textbook form for a Porter governor with both arms equal and at angle $\alpha$:

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{W}{m}\cdot\frac{\tan\alpha}{r} \cdot \frac{1}{2\cos\alpha} \quad \text{(depending on convention)}$$

The clean, **KTU-board-expected** form is:

$$\boxed{\omega^{2} = \frac{W}{m}\cdot\frac{\tan\alpha}{r\cdot 2\cos\alpha} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N^{2} = \frac{895}{h}\cdot\left(\frac{W + \tan\alpha}{m}\right) \text{ (conventions vary)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Tip for KTU Numerical Solving:** Always define $\alpha$ as the angle the *upper arm* makes with the vertical, and remember the sleeve friction term $F$ (given in Newtons) to be added/subtracted from $W$ when computing the upper and lower limits of speed.

---

## 2.3 The Proell Governor

### Modification of Porter
In the **Proell governor**, the balls are mounted on the **upper arms** of an inverted link (instead of at the joint of upper/lower arms). The sleeve weight is transmitted to the balls via a *link attached to the spindle*.

### Force Balance
For the Proell governor (one ball, axis symmetric):

- Centrifugal force: $F_c = m \omega^{2} r$
- The component of $F_c$ along the upper arm = $F_c \tan\alpha$ (transmitted to sleeve)
- The sleeve weight per ball: $W/2$

Equilibrium along the upper arm:

$$F_c \tan\alpha = \frac{W}{2}$$

Substituting $F_c = m\omega^{2} r$:

$$\boxed{\omega^{2} = \frac{W}{2m\,r \tan\alpha}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Proell governor is **more sensitive** than the Porter because the controlling force $W$ is balanced by a *larger* component ($F_c \tan\alpha$) of the centrifugal force. The balls respond to smaller speed changes.

---

## 2.4 The Hartnell Governor

### Mechanism
The **Hartnell governor** uses a **bell-crank lever** rotating about a fixed pivot. The ball is at one end, and a roller at the other end of the lever pushes against a **compression spring**. The spring is mounted on the central spindle.

### Geometric Ratio
Let:
- $a$ = distance from fulcrum to ball pivot
- $b$ = distance from fulcrum to roller
- $R$ = radius of ball path at max sleeve position (max speed)
- $R_0$ = radius of ball path at min sleeve position (min speed)
- $x$ = lift of sleeve (max sleeve lift)
- $x_0$ = initial sleeve lift (spring preload position)

Geometric ratio:

$$\frac{x}{a} = \frac{R - R_0}{b} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{R - R_0}{x} = \frac{b}{a}}$$

### Force Balance on Bell Crank
Taking moments about the fulcrum for one bell crank:

$$F_c \cdot a = S \cdot b$$

where $S$ is the spring force on the roller. Summing over both bell cranks (two balls), the total centrifugal force is $2 F_c$ on the same lever system:

$$2 F_c \cdot a = S \cdot b \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{2 a}{b} F_c = \frac{2a}{b} m \omega^{2} R$$

### Spring Force Equation
The spring obeys Hooke's law with preload $S_0$ at radius $R_0$:

$$S = S_0 + k(R - R_0)$$

where $k$ is the spring stiffness.

### Sensitivity / Speed Range
The **sensitiveness** of the Hartnell governor is:

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitiveness} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}} = 2\,\frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}}$$

---

## 2.5 The Hartung Governor

### Construction
The **Hartung governor** is a refinement of the Hartnell for **very high-speed** applications (steam turbines, turbochargers). It uses **two bell-crank levers** per ball, with the ball at an *intermediate* pivot — neither at the end nor at the fulcrum.

### Force Relation
For one bell crank (with ball at intermediate position):

$$F_c \cdot (a_1 - a_2) = S \cdot b$$

where $a_1$ and $a_2$ are the lever arms on either side of the ball pivot. The total spring force is computed using both bell cranks and summed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Hartung governor is rarely numerically tested. Focus on its *existence* as a Hartnell variant for high-speed precision applications.

---

## 2.6 Quality of Governors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four key terms every KTU student must internalize:**

### (a) Hunting
The continuous fluctuation of the engine speed above and below the mean speed, *even after* the load is steady. Caused by excessive governor sensitivity or friction in moving parts.

### (b) Sensitiveness ($S$)
$$\boxed{S = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}}}$$
A sensitive governor has a *small* range of speed between no-load and full-load.

### (c) Stability
A governor is **stable** if, after a small disturbance, it returns to its original equilibrium position.

### (d) Isochronism
A governor is **isochronous** if it runs at the *same* equilibrium speed at *all* radii of rotation. The Watt governor is **not** isochronous. The Hartnell governor can be made isochronous by carefully selecting the lever ratio and spring.

### Comparison Table

| Property | Watt | Porter | Proell | Hartnell |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sleeve friction considered | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (uses spring) |
| Sleeve weight | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (replaced by spring) |
| Sensitivity | Low | Medium | High | Very high |
| Used for | Historic | Low-speed | Medium | High-speed |

---

## 2.7 Inertia Governors

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *inertia governor* uses the **inertia of the balls** (not centrifugal force) to control the throttle. It responds to *changes* in speed, not steady-state speed.

### Pickering Governor (Classic Example)
- A spring-loaded bell crank carries a ball.
- An additional small flyweight (or "sensitivity weight") is mounted on the bell crank.
- When the engine speed *changes* (accelerates or decelerates), the inertia of the flyweight causes the bell crank to move, operating the throttle.
- During *steady* running, the inertia force is zero, and the governor does nothing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why inertia governors matter (KTU):** They anticipate load changes *before* speed has changed appreciably — giving faster response. Used in racing engines and precision governors.

---

## 2.8 Governor Speed Control — Sensitiveness Formula

The **sensitiveness** of a governor is the *ratio* of the difference between the maximum and minimum equilibrium speeds to the mean equilibrium speed.

$$\boxed{\text{Sensitiveness} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}} = 2 \cdot \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{max} + N_{min}}}$$

For a **Porter governor with friction**:

$$N_{1}^{2} = \frac{(W + F)}{2mg} \cdot \frac{r}{\tan\alpha \cdot \sec\alpha}\quad \text{(upper limit, sleeve rising)}$$

$$N_{2}^{2} = \frac{(W - F)}{2mg} \cdot \frac{r}{\tan\alpha \cdot \sec\alpha}\quad \text{(lower limit, sleeve falling)}$$

$$N_{mean} = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}$$

---

## 2.9 Cams and Followers — Classification

### 2.9.1 Classification of Followers (by shape of contacting surface)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Knife-edge** | Sharp edge contact | Theory only, high wear |
| **Roller** | Roller at the tip | Most common in IC engines |
| **Flat-faced** | Flat end surface | High-speed, low wear |
| **Spherical-faced (mushroom)** | Hemispherical tip | Better contact than flat |

### 2.9.2 Classification of Followers (by motion)

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Translating/Reciprocating** | Moves in a straight line (up/down) |
| **Oscillating** | Rotates about a fixed pivot |

### 2.9.3 Classification of Followers (by position of follower axis)

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **In-line (centred)** | Follower axis passes through cam axis |
| **Offset** | Follower axis is offset by a distance $e$ from cam axis |

### 2.9.4 Classification of Cams

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Radial/Disc Cam** | Cylindrical disc with contoured profile — most common |
| **Cylindrical Cam (Barrel)** | Groove cut on a cylinder, follower rides in groove |
| **Conjugate (Two-Disc) Cam** | Two discs with matched grooves — controls two followers in a constrained path |
| **Eccentric Cam** | Disc with off-centre circular profile — produces simple harmonic motion |

---

## 2.10 Displacement Diagrams — The Four Standard Motions

A **displacement diagram** is a graph of follower displacement $y$ vs cam angle $\theta$ (or follower displacement vs time, if cam rotates at constant speed).

The four KTU-mandated motions are:

### (a) Uniform Velocity
Displacement is linear in cam angle.

### (b) Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
Displacement is sinusoidal — half a sine wave during outstroke, half during return.

### (c) Uniform Acceleration and Retardation
Constant acceleration during first half of stroke, constant deceleration during second half.

### (d) Cycloidal Motion
Most refined — bounded velocity and acceleration at all times.

---

## 2.11 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for revision the night before the exam.**

### A. Governor Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal force on ball | $F_c = m \omega^{2} r = \dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$ | N |
| Ball angular speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| Height of Watt governor | $h = \dfrac{g}{\omega^{2}} = \dfrac{894}{N^{2}}$ (m, N in rpm) | m |
| Porter (no friction) | $W = 2 T \sin\alpha$ | N |
| Porter (with friction) | $W \pm F = 2 T \sin\alpha$ | N |
| Proell | $F_c \tan\alpha = \dfrac{W}{2}$ | N |
| Proell $\omega^{2}$ | $\omega^{2} = \dfrac{W}{2 m r \tan\alpha}$ | rad²/s² |
| Hartnell bell-crank | $F_c \cdot a = S \cdot b$ | N·m |
| Hartnell spring force | $S = S_0 + k(R - R_0)$ | N |
| Sensitiveness | $S = \dfrac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N_{mean}}$ | dimensionless |

### B. Cam Motion Formulas (All for Outstroke; $\beta_o$ = outstroke angle)

| Motion | Displacement $y(\theta)$ | Velocity $v(\theta)$ | Acceleration $a(\theta)$ | Max $\vert v \vert$ | Max $\vert a \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniform Velocity | $y = \dfrac{h}{\beta_o}\,\theta$ | $v = \dfrac{h}{\beta_o}$ | $0$ | $\dfrac{h}{\beta_o}$ | $\infty$ (at stroke ends) |
| SHM | $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left(1 - \cos\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$ | $v = \dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta_o}\,\sin\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta_o}$ | $a = -\dfrac{\pi^{2} h}{2\beta_o^{2}}\,\cos\dfrac{\pi\theta}{\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{\pi^{2} h}{2\beta_o^{2}}$ |
| Uniform Accel. (1st half) | $y = 2h\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta_o}\right)^{2}$ | $v = \dfrac{4h\theta}{\beta_o^{2}}$ | $a = \dfrac{4h}{\beta_o^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{4h}{\beta_o^{2}}$ |
| Cycloidal | $y = h\left(\dfrac{\theta}{\beta_o} - \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sin\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$ | $v = \dfrac{h}{\beta_o}\left(1 - \cos\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$ | $a = \dfrac{2\pi h}{\beta_o^{2}}\,\sin\dfrac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h}{\beta_o^{2}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the Maximum Velocity and Acceleration values** — they are the *most* tested formulas in Part A of the KTU exam.

### C. Cam Profile with Offset

For a knife-edge follower with offset $e$, the **pressure angle** is given by:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \mp e}{R + y}$$

where the **− sign** is for a follower offset *toward* the direction of decreasing $y$ (e.g., offset such that it moves *away* from the cam centre during the rise), and **+ sign** for offset in the opposite direction. $R$ is the prime-circle radius.

# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Speed of a Watt Governor

We start with the two equilibrium equations for one ball of mass $m$ rotating at radius $r$ and arm angle $\theta$ to the vertical.

**Step 1:** Resolve the arm tension $T$ into vertical and horizontal components.

The arm is at angle $\theta$ to the vertical. So:

- Vertical component: $T \cos\theta$ (upward along the arm)
- Horizontal component: $T \sin\theta$ (radially outward)

**Step 2:** Apply vertical equilibrium (no vertical motion of ball at steady state).

$$T \cos\theta = mg \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply horizontal (radial) equilibrium. The ball moves in a horizontal circle, so the net radial force equals the centripetal force $m\omega^{2} r$ directed *inward*. The arm tension's horizontal component provides this.

$$T \sin\theta = m \omega^{2} r \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4:** Divide (ii) by (i) to eliminate $T$.

$$\frac{T \sin\theta}{T \cos\theta} = \frac{m \omega^{2} r}{mg}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{\omega^{2} r}{g} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the height of the governor. The height $h$ of the cone is the vertical leg of the right triangle whose hypotenuse is the arm length $L$ and whose base is $r$. So $r = L \sin\theta$ and $h = L \cos\theta$.

$$\frac{r}{h} = \tan\theta$$

Substituting from (iii):

$$\frac{r}{h} = \frac{\omega^{2} r}{g}$$

$$\boxed{h = \frac{g}{\omega^{2}} = \frac{895}{N^{2}}\ \text{metres (for N in rpm)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 895 (or 894.4) value comes from $\dfrac{g \cdot 3600}{4\pi^{2}}$. Different KTU texts use slightly different approximations — always check the question's specified constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equilibrium Speed of a Porter Governor (with friction)

Consider a Porter governor with sleeve weight $W$, sleeve friction $F$ (force), ball mass $m$, ball radius $r$, and arm angle $\alpha$ (upper arm to vertical).

**Step 1:** Vertical equilibrium of the ball. The arm tension $T$ is at angle $\alpha$ to the vertical.

$$T \cos\alpha = mg + (\text{component of sleeve load on ball})$$

For the case where both upper and lower arms have angle $\alpha$ and the sleeve load per ball is $W/2$:

$$T \cos\alpha = mg + \frac{W}{2} \cdot (\text{geometric factor})$$

In the **standard Porter governor** with the sleeve weight $W$ transmitted equally to both balls, the simplified equilibrium is:

$$T \cos\alpha = mg + \frac{W}{2}\quad \text{(treating $W$ as a downward force on the joint between upper and lower arms)}$$

**Step 2:** Horizontal equilibrium of the ball.

$$T \sin\alpha = m\omega^{2} r \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** Divide (ii) by (i):

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{m\omega^{2} r}{mg + \dfrac{W}{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $\omega^{2}$:

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{\left(mg + \dfrac{W}{2}\right)\tan\alpha}{m \cdot r}$$

**Step 5: Include sleeve friction.** When the sleeve is moving *up* (engine speed rising), friction $F$ opposes the motion, so the effective sleeve load is $(W + F)$. When moving *down* (speed falling), the effective load is $(W - F)$.

**Upper limit (sleeve rising):**

$$\omega_{1}^{2} = \frac{\left(mg + \dfrac{W+F}{2}\right)\tan\alpha}{m \cdot r}$$

**Lower limit (sleeve falling):**

$$\omega_{2}^{2} = \frac{\left(mg + \dfrac{W-F}{2}\right)\tan\alpha}{m \cdot r}$$

$$\boxed{N_{mean} = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2}, \quad S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N_{mean}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cycloidal Motion — Velocity and Acceleration

The **cycloidal motion** is the most refined of the four standard follower motions because it has **finite velocity and acceleration at the start and end of the stroke** (unlike uniform velocity, which has infinite acceleration at the boundaries).

### Step 1: Displacement Function
For the outstroke ($\theta$ from $0$ to $\beta_o$), the displacement is:

$$y = h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta_o} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

### Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $\theta$ to get the velocity

$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = h\left(\frac{1}{\beta_o} - \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\beta_o} \cdot \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

$$\frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h}{\beta_o}\left(1 - \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

This is the angular velocity $v_{\theta}$ of the follower *per radian* of cam rotation. The actual velocity (in m/s, assuming cam rotates at $\omega$ rad/s) is:

$$v = \omega \cdot \frac{dy}{d\theta} = \frac{h\,\omega}{\beta_o}\left(1 - \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

**At $\theta = 0$:** $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta_o}(1 - 1) = 0$ ✅ (no jerk at start)
**At $\theta = \beta_o/2$:** $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta_o}(1 - \cos\pi) = \dfrac{2h\omega}{\beta_o}$ ✅ (max velocity)
**At $\theta = \beta_o$:** $v = \dfrac{h\omega}{\beta_o}(1 - \cos 2\pi) = 0$ ✅ (no jerk at end)

### Step 3: Differentiate again to get acceleration

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} = \frac{h}{\beta_o} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\beta_o} \cdot \sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o} = \frac{2\pi h}{\beta_o^{2}}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}$$

The actual acceleration (m/s²):

$$a = \omega^{2} \cdot \frac{d^{2}y}{d\theta^{2}} = \frac{2\pi h \omega^{2}}{\beta_o^{2}}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}$$

**At $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \beta_o$:** $a = 0$ ✅ (continuous)
**At $\theta = \beta_o/4$ and $3\beta_o/4$:** $|a| = \dfrac{2\pi h \omega^{2}}{\beta_o^{2}}$ (max acceleration)

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cam Profile for Knife-Edge Follower with Offset

### Setup
- The cam rotates counter-clockwise about point $O$.
- The knife-edge follower has its line of stroke *offset* from $O$ by a distance $e$ to the *right* of the cam axis.
- The follower is *translating* (not rotating) along the offset axis.
- The cam must push the follower *up* by an amount equal to the displacement $y$ at each instant.

### Construction (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1:** Draw the **base circle** of radius $R$ (smallest circle tangent to the cam profile) centred at $O$.

**Step 2:** Draw the **offset line** — a line at perpendicular distance $e$ from $O$ on the right side. This is the line of stroke of the follower.

**Step 3:** Mark the **trace point** $T$ on the offset line, at distance $R$ from $O$, i.e., on the base circle. This is the *initial* position of the follower tip.

**Step 4:** Divide the outstroke cam rotation $\beta_o$ and return stroke $\beta_r$ into equal angular intervals (typically 6 or 12). The total cam rotation is $2\pi$ rad (one full revolution), with $\beta_d$ = dwell (if any) included.

**Step 5:** On the displacement diagram, mark the displacements $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ at each angular division.

**Step 6:** On the cam drawing, draw radial lines from $O$ at the same angular divisions. For each radial line, measure a distance $R + y_i$ **along the offset line** on the *same side* as the trace point. The position of the trace point at angle $\theta_i$ is found by:

- Mark the angle $\theta_i$ on the base circle.
- The trace point is offset by $y_i$ from the base circle *measured parallel to the line of stroke*.

**Step 7:** The **cam profile** is the smooth curve passing through the trace points.

### Why the Offset Matters
For an offset follower, the follower is *not* radial. As the cam rotates, the velocity vector of the contact point has a component *perpendicular* to the line of stroke, which contributes to the **side thrust** on the follower. This side thrust is:

$$F_{side} = F_{axial} \cdot \tan\alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the **pressure angle**. For an offset follower:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\dfrac{dy}{d\theta} \mp e}{R + y}$$

The **− sign** is taken when the offset is in the direction of motion (so the cam pushes the follower *into* the offset line of stroke, *reducing* the effective side thrust and hence the pressure angle). The **+ sign** is taken when the offset is in the opposite direction (which would *increase* the pressure angle — usually undesirable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule (KTU):** For minimum pressure angle and side thrust, the offset should be in the *direction of follower motion* (i.e., offset such that the cam pushes the follower *toward* the offset line during outstroke).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Four Cam Motions

> [!TIP]
> **The most-tested topic in Part A.** Memorize this table; it appears in nearly every KTU module test.

| Property | Uniform Velocity | SHM | Uniform Accel. | Cycloidal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max velocity | $\dfrac{h}{\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{\pi h}{2\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{\beta_o}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{\beta_o}$ |
| Max acceleration | **Infinite** at stroke ends | $\dfrac{\pi^{2} h}{2\beta_o^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{4h}{\beta_o^{2}}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi h}{\beta_o^{2}}$ (lowest) |
| Acceleration at start | **Infinite** | Finite ($\dfrac{\pi^{2}h}{2\beta_o^{2}}$) | Finite ($\dfrac{4h}{\beta_o^{2}}$) | **Zero** (best) |
| Jerk (rate of change of accel.) | **Infinite** at stroke ends | **Infinite** at stroke ends | **Infinite** at mid-stroke | **Zero** (smoothest) |
| Practical use | Low-speed simple machines | Low-speed pumps | Punch presses | High-speed IC engines |

> [!NOTE]
> **Jerk** is the time-derivative of acceleration — a measure of "smoothness" of the motion. Cycloidal motion has **bounded velocity, acceleration, and jerk** — making it the smoothest. This is why it's preferred for high-speed cam design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Architecture:** This diagram shows how the *governor* and the *cam* work together in a real engine. The **governor** controls the *amount* of fuel (a continuous, slow control loop), while the **cam** controls the *timing* of the valves (a discrete, high-speed control loop). They are complementary, not redundant.
