# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a Porter governor and a Hartnell governor. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Porter Governor | Hartnell Governor |
|---|---|---|
| Controlling force | Sleeve weight (gravity) | Spring force |
| Sleeve friction | Must be considered | Negligible (no sliding) |
| Speed range | Low to medium | High speed |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Very high |
| Adjustability | Difficult to adjust sensitivity | Sensitivity adjustable by lever ratio |
| **Advantage** | Simple, robust, low cost | Compact, precise, low wear |
| **Disadvantage** | Lower sensitivity, friction causes hunting | Requires careful spring design, expensive |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**A cam rotating at 300 rpm imparts Simple Harmonic Motion to a follower. The lift is 40 mm and the outstroke angle is 90°. Find the maximum velocity and maximum acceleration during the outstroke.**

**Model Solution (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 300$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416$ rad/s
- $h = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m
- $\beta_o = 90° = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad $= 1.5708$ rad

**Maximum velocity (SHM outstroke):**

$$v_{max} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta_o} = \frac{\pi \times 0.04 \times 31.416}{2 \times 1.5708}$$

$$v_{max} = \frac{3.9486}{3.1416} = 1.257\ \text{m/s}$$

**Maximum acceleration (SHM outstroke):**

$$a_{max} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta_o^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 0.04 \times (31.416)^{2}}{2 \times (1.5708)^{2}}$$

$$a_{max} = \frac{1240.0}{4.9348} = 251.3\ \text{m/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} \approx 1.26\ \text{m/s}, \quad a_{max} \approx 251.3\ \text{m/s}^{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Examiner Pitfall (3-mark question):** Students often forget to **convert the outstroke angle to radians** before applying the formula. Always do this conversion as the *first* step — losing this costs you 1 mark instantly.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a)** A Porter governor has all four arms of length 300 mm. The upper and lower arms are at 30° and 40° to the vertical, respectively, at the equilibrium position. Each ball has a mass of 5 kg, and the central load on the sleeve is 50 N. Determine:
> 1. The radius of rotation of the balls at the equilibrium position.
> 2. The equilibrium speed in rpm.
> 3. If the friction on the sleeve is 10 N, find the range of speed for the given radius.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of a **Hartnell governor**. Derive the expression for the **sensitiveness** of a Hartnell governor. (7 marks)

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]**

> **(a)** A cam rotates at 600 rpm and operates a knife-edge follower with an offset of 12 mm. The follower has a lift of 30 mm and is to move with **uniform acceleration and retardation** during the outstroke of 120° and **simple harmonic motion** during the return stroke of 150°. The remaining angle is a dwell. Determine:
> 1. The maximum velocity during the outstroke and return stroke.
> 2. The maximum acceleration during the outstroke and return stroke.
> 3. The time taken for the outstroke and return stroke.
>
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a displacement diagram, derive the **velocity and acceleration equations for Cycloidal motion** of a cam follower. State **two advantages** of cycloidal motion over uniform velocity motion. (7 marks)

---

## SOLUTIONS

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Porter Governor (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Arm length $L = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m
- Upper arm angle $\alpha = 30°$
- Lower arm angle $\beta = 40°$
- Ball mass $m = 5$ kg
- Sleeve load $W = 50$ N
- Friction $F = 10$ N

**Step 1: Radius of rotation of balls**

The radius $r$ is the horizontal projection of the arm from the spindle axis. For the *upper* arm (which determines the ball height above the pivot):

$$r = L \sin\alpha = 0.3 \times \sin 30° = 0.3 \times 0.5 = 0.15\ \text{m} = 150\ \text{mm}$$

(We use the upper arm because the ball is at the joint between upper and lower arms in a standard Porter governor.)

**Step 2: Equilibrium speed (no friction)**

For a Porter governor with unequal arm angles, the force balance gives:

$$T_1 \cos\alpha + T_2 \cos\beta = mg + W$$
$$T_1 \sin\alpha = T_2 \sin\beta + m\omega^{2} r$$

For *symmetric* load sharing and standard simplification:

$$(W + 2mg)\tan\alpha = 2 m \omega^{2} r \cdot \cos\alpha$$

Solving for $\omega^{2}$ (using the *upper* arm convention):

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{(W + 2mg)\tan\alpha}{2 m r \cos\alpha} = \frac{(50 + 2 \times 5 \times 9.81)\tan 30°}{2 \times 5 \times 0.15 \times \cos 30°}$$

Numerator: $(50 + 98.1) \times 0.5774 = 148.1 \times 0.5774 = 85.51$

Denominator: $2 \times 5 \times 0.15 \times 0.8660 = 1.299$

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{85.51}{1.299} = 65.83\ \text{rad}^{2}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$\omega = 8.114\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$N = \frac{\omega \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{8.114 \times 60}{6.2832} = 77.5\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step 3: With friction**

The friction force $F = 10$ N modifies the sleeve load:
- Upper limit (sleeve rising): $W + F = 60$ N
- Lower limit (sleeve falling): $W - F = 40$ N

**Upper limit:**

$$\omega_1^{2} = \frac{(60 + 98.1) \times 0.5774}{1.299} = \frac{91.28}{1.299} = 70.27$$

$$\omega_1 = 8.383\ \text{rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_1 = 80.0\ \text{rpm}$$

**Lower limit:**

$$\omega_2^{2} = \frac{(40 + 98.1) \times 0.5774}{1.299} = \frac{79.74}{1.299} = 61.39$$

$$\omega_2 = 7.835\ \text{rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_2 = 74.8\ \text{rpm}$$

**Range of speed:**

$$\boxed{\Delta N = N_1 - N_2 \approx 5.2\ \text{rpm}, \quad N_{mean} \approx 77.4\ \text{rpm}}$$

#### Part (b) — Hartnell Governor Construction and Sensitiveness (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
Refer to the architecture block diagram in Section 4.1. Describe:
- Central spindle rotating about a vertical axis.
- Two bell-crank levers pivoted at fixed fulcrums on the spindle.
- Fly-ball at one end of each lever.
- Roller at the other end of each lever, pressing against a flanged sleeve.
- Compression spring on the spindle, pushing the sleeve against the rollers.

**Working (2 marks):**
As the spindle speed increases, the fly-balls fly outward, the bell-crank levers rotate, the rollers push the sleeve *upward* against the spring, and the sleeve movement operates the throttle valve to *reduce* the fuel supply. The reverse happens on load increase.

**Derivation of Sensitiveness (2 marks):**

For one bell crank (lever ratio $a/b$):

$$F_c \cdot a = S \cdot b$$

Summing for both cranks (with centrifugal force per ball $F_{c1}, F_{c2}$):

$$(F_{c1} + F_{c2}) \cdot a = S \cdot b$$

$$2m\omega^{2}R \cdot a = S \cdot b$$

At the **upper** position ($R = R_2$, $S = S_0 + k(R_2 - R_0)$):

$$\omega_2^{2} = \frac{b(S_0 + k(R_2 - R_0))}{2 m a R_2}$$

At the **lower** position ($R = R_1$):

$$\omega_1^{2} = \frac{b \cdot S_0}{2 m a R_1}$$

Mean speed $\omega_{mean} = \dfrac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$

Sensitiveness:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_{mean}} = 2 \cdot \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_2 + \omega_1}}$$

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Cam Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N = 600$ rpm → $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.83$ rad/s
- $h = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- Outstroke $\beta_o = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ rad $= 2.094$ rad
- Return stroke $\beta_r = 150° = \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ rad $= 2.618$ rad
- Offset $e = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m

**1. Maximum velocity**

For **uniform acceleration** outstroke:

$$v_{max,\,out} = \frac{2h \cdot \omega}{\beta_o} = \frac{2 \times 0.030 \times 62.83}{2.094} = 1.80\ \text{m/s}$$

For **SHM** return stroke:

$$v_{max,\,ret} = \frac{\pi h \omega}{2 \beta_r} = \frac{\pi \times 0.030 \times 62.83}{2 \times 2.618} = 1.131\ \text{m/s}$$

**2. Maximum acceleration**

For uniform acceleration outstroke:

$$a_{max,\,out} = \frac{4h \cdot \omega^{2}}{\beta_o^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.030 \times (62.83)^{2}}{(2.094)^{2}} = \frac{473.7}{4.385} = 108.0\ \text{m/s}^{2}$$

For SHM return stroke:

$$a_{max,\,ret} = \frac{\pi^{2} h \omega^{2}}{2 \beta_r^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 0.030 \times 3947.8}{2 \times 6.854} = \frac{1168.0}{13.71} = 85.2\ \text{m/s}^{2}$$

**3. Time for each stroke**

Time for outstroke:

$$t_{out} = \frac{\beta_o}{\omega} = \frac{2.094}{62.83} = 0.0333\ \text{s} = 33.3\ \text{ms}$$

Time for return stroke:

$$t_{ret} = \frac{\beta_r}{\omega} = \frac{2.618}{62.83} = 0.0417\ \text{s} = 41.7\ \text{ms}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A, 7-mark question):** Many students mix up the formulas between *outstroke* and *return stroke*. The outstroke formula uses $\beta_o$, the return stroke uses $\beta_r$. *Both* use the *same* $h$ and $\omega$. The offset $e$ does **not** affect the velocity/acceleration — it affects only the **pressure angle** and the **cam profile geometry**.

#### Part (b) — Cycloidal Motion Derivation (7 marks)

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.3** of these notes.

**Final Equations (for outstroke):**

Displacement:
$$y = h\left(\frac{\theta}{\beta_o} - \frac{1}{2\pi}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

Velocity:
$$v = \frac{h\omega}{\beta_o}\left(1 - \cos\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}\right)$$

Acceleration:
$$a = \frac{2\pi h \omega^{2}}{\beta_o^{2}}\sin\frac{2\pi\theta}{\beta_o}$$

**Two Advantages of Cycloidal Motion over Uniform Velocity (2 marks):**

1. **Bounded (finite) velocity, acceleration, AND jerk at all points** — including at the start and end of the stroke. In contrast, uniform velocity has *infinite* acceleration at the stroke boundaries, causing mechanical shock and vibration.

2. **No sudden change in motion direction** — the velocity and acceleration curves are *continuous* and *smooth*, which is critical for high-speed cam applications (e.g., IC engine valves) where jerky motion would cause follower jump and noise.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks on this module:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the arms in Porter/Proell** — the ball may be at the *joint* (Porter) or at the *upper end* (Proell). Get this wrong, and your force balance is wrong.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the friction sign in Porter** — the friction force is **+F** when sleeve is rising (upper limit) and **−F** when sleeve is falling (lower limit). Mixing these gives wrong $N_1$ and $N_2$.
>
> 3. **Using degrees instead of radians** in cam formulas. The $\beta_o$ in all SHM, cycloidal, and uniform-acceleration formulas is in **radians**. Forgetting this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
>
> 4. **Confusing displacement diagram coordinates with cam profile** — the displacement diagram has displacement on the y-axis, but the cam profile has the *trace point* on the *base circle* offset by $y$ along the *offset line*. Don't transpose these.
>
> 5. **Not stating units** for final answers. KTU examiners are strict: every numerical answer must end with the correct unit (e.g., m/s² for acceleration, rpm for speed, m/s for velocity).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### 🎯 Governors — Must-Know

- [ ] **Watt:** Simplest, no sleeve, $h = 894/N^2$ (N in rpm)
- [ ] **Porter:** Sleeve weight + friction considered; $W \pm F = 2T\sin\alpha$
- [ ] **Proell:** Balls on upper arms; $F_c \tan\alpha = W/2$ — *more sensitive than Porter*
- [ ] **Hartnell:** Bell-crank + spring; $F_c \cdot a = S \cdot b$; spring force $S = S_0 + k(R-R_0)$
- [ ] **Hartung:** Two bell cranks per ball, intermediate ball pivot, very high speed
- [ ] **Inertia Governor (Pickering):** Uses inertia of a flyweight to detect *changes* in speed
- [ ] **Sensitiveness:** $S = (N_{max} - N_{min}) / N_{mean}$
- [ ] **Stability** ≠ **Isochronism** — a governor can be stable but not isochronous
- [ ] **Hunting** is *caused* by excessive sensitivity, friction, or both

### 🎯 Cams — Must-Know

- [ ] **Base circle** is the smallest circle tangent to the cam profile
- [ ] **Pitch curve** is for roller followers; **cam profile** is for knife-edge followers
- [ ] **Pressure angle** $\alpha$ measures side thrust on the follower
- [ ] **Four motions** in order of smoothness: Uniform Velocity < SHM < Uniform Accel. < **Cycloidal**
- [ ] **Cycloidal motion** has the lowest max acceleration ($2\pi h / \beta_o^2$)
- [ ] **SHM** has the most familiar displacement curve: $y = (h/2)(1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta_o))$
- [ ] **Uniform velocity** has *infinite* acceleration at stroke ends — *not* used for high-speed cams
- [ ] **Offset follower** reduces pressure angle when offset is in the *direction of motion*
- [ ] **Pressure angle formula with offset:** $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{(dy/d\theta) \mp e}{R + y}$
- [ ] **Roller follower** is the most common in IC engine valve trains

### 🎯 Formulas at a Glance

| Topic | Must-Memorize Formula |
|---|---|
| Watt height | $h = 894/N^{2}$ (m) |
| Porter friction | $W \pm F = 2T\sin\alpha$ |
| Proell | $\omega^{2} = W/(2mr\tan\alpha)$ |
| Hartnell | $F_c \cdot a = S \cdot b$ |
| Sensitiveness | $S = (N_{max} - N_{min})/N_{mean}$ |
| SHM velocity max | $v_{max} = \pi h\omega/(2\beta_o)$ |
| Cycloidal accel max | $a_{max} = 2\pi h\omega^{2}/\beta_o^{2}$ |
| Pressure angle (offset) | $\tan\alpha = (dy/d\theta \mp e)/(R + y)$ |

### 🎯 Engineering Significance

- **Governors** are the *feedback controllers* of pre-electronic engines — they keep speed steady without electronics.
- **Cams** are the *programmed actuators* of internal combustion engines — they time valve opening and closing for optimal breathing.
- Together, they make every piston engine, steam engine, and gas turbine *work reliably* under varying load.
- **Modern cars** use electronic governors and electronic cam-phasing (VVT, VVL), but the *principles* remain identical to those in this module.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** If a question asks "which motion is best for high-speed cams?", the answer is **always cycloidal** — both the maximum and minimum velocities/accelerations are bounded, and there is no jerk discontinuity. Memorize this; it appears in nearly every KTU module test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *gear* is a rotating, machine element possessing **cut teeth** (or the equivalent) on its rim, which intermesh with another toothed member (or a rack) to transmit motion and power between rotating shafts, while maintaining a definite speed ratio.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Theory of Machines, Module 3)**, gears are classified based on several primary engineering criteria:
1. **Position of Axes of Rotation** (Parallel, Intersecting, Non-intersecting/Skew)
2. **Peripheral Profile / Tooth Shape** (Involute, Cycloidal, Novikov/Wildhaber)
3. **Type of Motion Transmitted** (Uniform velocity, Variable velocity)
4. **Direction of Motion** (External, Internal)
5. **Peripheral Speed** (Low speed < 3 m/s, Medium 3–15 m/s, High > 15 m/s)

The fundamental kinematic purpose of a gear pair is the transformation of:
- **Rotational speed (N)**, measured in **rev/min (rpm)**
- **Torque (T)**, measured in **N·m**
- **Power (P)**, measured in **Watts (W)**
…while preserving a strict, deterministic kinematic ratio (the **velocity ratio, VR**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bicycle Gear System**
> Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a steep hill. You shift the chain onto a **larger rear sprocket** and a **smaller front chainring**. Your legs still pedal at the same comfortable cadence, but the rear wheel rotates **slower** but with much **greater turning force (torque)**. Conversely, on a flat road, you use a **larger front chainring and a smaller rear sprocket** to achieve higher wheel speed with reduced pedaling effort. **Gears are the mechanical equivalent of a transformer for rotational motion** — converting speed into torque (and vice versa) in a precise, non-slip manner.

The **black standard** metric governing all gear design is:
- **Module (m)** = Pitch circle diameter (D) / Number of teeth (T) → **m = D / T (in mm)**
- This is the universally standardized tooth-size parameter recognized in the **AGMA (American Gear Manufacturers Association)** and **IS (Indian Standards)**.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters in KTU Context

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 evaluations, students frequently lose marks by stating *types* of gears without contextualizing them to the **classification criterion**. For example, "spur gear" is incomplete — the proper phrasing is "spur gear — classified under *Parallel Axes* with *External Mesh*."

**Key Industrial Applications Snapshot:**
- **Automotive differentials** → Bevel gears
- **Watches and clocks** → Fine-pitch spur gears
- **Conveyor drives** → Helical gears
- **Marine propulsion** → Worm and worm wheel
- **Heavy machinery gearboxes** → Herringbone / double helical gears
- **Power plants (wind turbines)** → Hypoid / spiral bevel
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification Logic

A KTU 2024 examiner expects the classification to be presented in a **structured decision-tree format** based on the *position of shafts* — this is the most academically accepted top-level classification.

#### **A. Gears for Parallel Shafts**
- **Spur Gear** — Straight teeth parallel to the axis of rotation. Simplest, most economical, used for low-to-medium speeds.
- **Helical Gear** — Teeth cut at a helix angle (α). Provides smoother, quieter meshing and higher load capacity than spur gears. Introduces **axial thrust load**.
- **Double Helical (Herringbone) Gear** — Two helical gears of opposite hand placed side-by-side. **Axial thrusts cancel out.** Used in heavy-duty industrial gearboxes.
- **Rack and Pinion** — A spur gear (pinion) meshing with a linear toothed bar (rack). Converts **rotational → linear** motion.

#### **B. Gears for Intersecting (Non-Parallel) Shafts**
- **Straight Bevel Gear** — Conical pitch surfaces; straight tapered teeth. Used for 90° (or other) intersecting shafts.
- **Spiral Bevel Gear** — Curved, oblique teeth — analogous to helical in bevel form. Quieter and stronger.
- **Mitre Gear** — Special bevel gear pair with equal number of teeth and shafts at 90°. Velocity ratio = **1:1**.
- **Angular Bevel Gear** — Shafts at angles other than 90°.

#### **C. Gears for Non-Intersecting, Non-Parallel (Skew) Shafts**
- **Worm and Worm Wheel** — Power transmission between shafts at 90° (typically) but non-intersecting. Provides very high reduction ratios (up to 100:1 in single stage). Self-locking capability.
- **Hypoid Gear** — Used in automotive rear axles. Offset between gear axes; resembles spiral bevel but with hypoid offset.
- **Spiral (Crossed Helical) Gear** — Two helical gears with non-intersecting, non-parallel axes. Used for light-duty skew shafts.

### 2.2 Auxiliary Classification (Critical for KTU 2-Mark Questions)

| Sub-Classification Type | Description | KTU Quick Fact |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Gear** | Teeth cut on the *inner* periphery of an annular ring. | Meshes with an external gear concentric with it. |
| **External Gear** | Teeth cut on the *outer* periphery. | The default gear type. |
| **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear** | Gears rotating about a common carrier. | Module 4 topic in KTU, but classified under "compound." |
| **Stub-Tooth Gear** | Reduced addendum (e.g., addendum = 0.8m). | Higher strength, used in automotive transmissions. |
| **Novikov (Wildhaber) Gear** | Circular-arc tooth profile. | High-load capacity; developed in the USSR. |
| **Involute Gear** | Most common, ease of manufacture. | Standard **20°** full-depth pressure angle. |
| **Cycloidal Gear** | Used in clocks and instruments. | No interference, but sensitive to center-distance variation. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas form the **foundational kinematic backbone** of all gear analysis under Module 3 of PCAUT502.**

| # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity Ratio (VR) | $VR = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ | dimensionless | Gear pair basic kinematics |
| 2 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $D = m \cdot T$ | mm | Given T and m |
| 3 | Module | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | mm | Standard IS values |
| 4 | Circular Pitch (p) | $p = \pi \cdot m$ | mm | Tooth spacing on P.C.D. |
| 5 | Diametral Pitch (P) | $P = \dfrac{T}{D}$ | teeth/inch | Used in imperial (USA) |
| 6 | Center Distance (C) | $C = \dfrac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \dfrac{m(T_1+T_2)}{2}$ | mm | Parallel shaft gears |
| 7 | Linear Velocity (v) | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | Pitch-line velocity |
| 8 | Bevel Gear Cone Distance | $R = \dfrac{D_2}{2 \sin \gamma}$ | mm | Bevel gear geometry |
| 9 | Worm Gear Velocity Ratio | $VR = \dfrac{N_{worm}}{N_{wheel}} = \dfrac{T_{wheel}}{T_{worm}}$ | dimensionless | Worm analysis |
| 10 | Helical Gear Virtual Teeth | $T_v = \dfrac{T}{\cos^3 \alpha}$ | dimensionless | Strength calc. (Lewis) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conversion Safeguard for KTU board papers:** If circular pitch is given, **always use** $m = p/\pi$ — students frequently write $m = p \cdot \pi$, losing **1–2 marks** in valuation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of gears is **not a theoretical taxonomy** — it is a design decision matrix:
- **Helical vs Spur** decision = trade-off between smoothness and axial thrust.
- **Worm vs Bevel** decision = trade-off between high reduction ratio and efficiency.
- **Spur vs Herringbone** decision = cost of manufacturing vs need for thrust cancellation.
- **Involute vs Cycloidal** decision = modern standard (involute) vs legacy precision instruments (cycloidal).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Velocity Ratio of a Simple Gear Pair

**Problem Statement:** Two spur gears with teeth $T_1$ and $T_2$ are in external mesh. Show that the velocity ratio is given by $VR = T_1/T_2$.

**Step 1 — Establish the Rolling Constraint:**
When two gears mesh, the linear velocity at the pitch circle must be **identical** (the no-slip meshing condition). The pitch circle linear velocity of gear 1 is:

$$
v_1 = \pi \cdot D_1 \cdot N_1
$$

where $D_1$ is the pitch circle diameter of gear 1 (in meters or mm) and $N_1$ is its rotational speed (rev/sec).

**Step 2 — Same Condition for Gear 2:**
By the same logic for gear 2:

$$
v_2 = \pi \cdot D_2 \cdot N_2
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the No-Slip Condition:**
At the pitch point, $v_1 = v_2$ (otherwise the teeth would shear or slip). Therefore:

$$
\pi \cdot D_1 \cdot N_1 = \pi \cdot D_2 \cdot N_2
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Common Term:**
The $\pi$ cancels on both sides:

$$
D_1 \cdot N_1 = D_2 \cdot N_2
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for the Velocity Ratio:**
Solving for the ratio $N_2/N_1$:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute D = m·T:**
Using the relation $D = m \cdot T$ (where $m$ is the module — same for both gears in mesh), we get:

$$
\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{m \cdot T_1}{m \cdot T_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Form:**

$$
\boxed{VR = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the ratio is *inverted* in the velocity ratio definition:** In standard KTU 2024 textbook convention, **VR is defined as driven speed / driver speed** ($N_2/N_1$). Because driver has fewer teeth (smaller gear → spins faster), $T_1 < T_2$, so VR is a *fraction less than 1*. If the question asks for *speed ratio (SR)* in the KTU sheet, it is the inverse of VR.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pitch Line Velocity of a Spur Gear

**Step 1 — Define the circumference traversed per revolution:**
The pitch circle of a gear of diameter $D$ has circumference $\pi D$.

**Step 2 — Compute linear distance per unit time:**
If the gear rotates at $N$ rev/min, then in 1 minute it covers $\pi D \cdot N$ units of linear distance.

**Step 3 — Convert to per-second basis:**

$$
v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \left[\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right]
$$

> *(Note: When D is in meters, the result is in m/s directly. When D is in mm, divide the numerator by 1000.)*

**Step 4 — Substitute D = m·T:**

$$
v = \frac{\pi m T N}{60}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** If the question is on **medium/high speed classification**, the numerical value of $v$ must be computed. If $v < 3$ m/s → **Low speed**, $3 \leq v < 15$ m/s → **Medium**, $v \geq 15$ m/s → **High speed** (which dictates material choice — e.g., hardened alloy steel for high-speed gears).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Center Distance for a Spur Gear Pair

**Step 1 — Visualize the Setup:**
Two meshing spur gears with pitch circle diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$ rotate about parallel centers separated by distance $C$.

**Step 2 — Geometric Relation:**
The line connecting the two centers passes through the pitch point. Thus:

$$
C = \frac{D_1}{2} + \frac{D_2}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out and Simplify:**

$$
C = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of module and teeth:**

$$
C = \frac{m \cdot T_1 + m \cdot T_2}{2} = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Boxed Form:**

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2} \;\; \text{mm}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Bevel Gear Velocity Ratio (Shaft Angle δ)

**Step 1 — Pitch Cone Geometry:**
For a pair of bevel gears, the pitch cones roll on each other. The half-cone angles are $\gamma$ (pinion) and $\Sigma$ (gear) with $\gamma + \Sigma = \delta$ (shaft angle).

**Step 2 — Pitch Circle Diameters on Cone Surface:**

$$
D_1 = 2 R \sin \gamma, \quad D_2 = 2 R \sin \Sigma
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Velocity Ratio:**

$$
VR = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\sin \gamma}{\sin \Sigma}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The sinusoidal form** is the signature of bevel gear kinematics. For $\delta = 90°$ (shaft angle), $\Sigma = 90° - \gamma$, so $VR = \tan \gamma$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Gear Types

| Gear Type | Axes | VR (typical) | Efficiency | Axial Thrust | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spur | Parallel | up to 8:1 | **98–99%** | None | Machine tools, simple drives |
| Helical | Parallel | up to 10:1 | **97–99%** | **Yes** | Conveyors, automobile gearboxes |
| Double Helical | Parallel | up to 15:1 | **97–99%** | **Cancels out** | Heavy turbines, rolling mills |
| Straight Bevel | Intersecting | up to 6:1 | **97–98%** | Radial + axial | Hand drills, differentials |
| Spiral Bevel | Intersecting | up to 10:1 | **98–99%** | **Yes** | Automotive rear axles |
| Worm & Wheel | Skew (90°) | up to 100:1 | **40–90%** (variable) | **Yes** | Lifts, conveyors, small drives |
| Hypoid | Skew (offset) | up to 10:1 | **85–95%** | **Yes** | Car rear axles (offset for clearance) |
| Rack & Pinion | Parallel/linear | — | **95–98%** | None | Steering, machine tool slides |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)`**
> **Classify gears based on the position of the axes of the shafts. Give one example of each type.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):**
Gears are classified based on the position of the shafts of the meshing gears into three main categories:
1. **Parallel Shaft Gears** — Axes are parallel. *Example:* Spur gear, Helical gear, Herringbone gear. (1 Mark)
2. **Intersecting Shaft Gears** — Axes meet at a point (intersect). *Example:* Straight bevel gear, Spiral bevel gear, Mitre gear. (1 Mark)
3. **Non-Intersecting / Skew Shaft Gears** — Axes are neither parallel nor intersecting. *Example:* Worm and worm wheel, Hypoid gear, Crossed helical gear. (1 Mark)

> **`Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)`**
> **Differentiate between spur gears and helical gears. State one advantage and one disadvantage of helical gears.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):**
- **Spur gears** have teeth that are straight and parallel to the axis of rotation, while **helical gears** have teeth cut at an angle (helix angle, typically 15°–25°) to the axis. (1 Mark)
- **Advantage of helical gears:** Quieter and smoother operation at high speeds due to gradual tooth engagement. (1 Mark)
- **Disadvantage of helical gears:** Generates axial thrust force, requiring the use of thrust bearings. (1 Mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **`Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)`**

### **Option A:**

> **(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the classification of gears based on the position of axes of rotation. State at least four types with their applications.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
The classification of gears based on the position of axes of rotation is as follows: **(0.5 Mark for diagram, 6.5 Marks for explanation)**

**1. Spur Gears (Parallel Axes):**
The simplest and most common type. Teeth are straight and parallel to the axis. Used in clocks, washing machines, simple gear reducers.

**2. Helical Gears (Parallel Axes):**
Teeth are cut at a helix angle α. Provide smoother, quieter, and stronger meshing. The helix angle creates an axial thrust component.
- *Application:* Automotive transmissions, conveyor drives.

**3. Double Helical / Herringbone Gears (Parallel Axes):**
Two helical gears of opposite hand mounted on the same shaft. The axial thrusts from each helix cancel each other.
- *Application:* Heavy machinery, marine gearboxes, rolling mills.

**4. Bevel Gears (Intersecting Axes):**
Conical pitch surfaces. Used to transmit motion between shafts intersecting at an angle (commonly 90°).
- *Sub-types:* Straight bevel, spiral bevel, mitre gear.
- *Application:* Hand drills, automotive differentials.

**5. Worm and Worm Wheel (Skew Axes):**
Worm (screw-like) meshes with worm wheel. Used for high reduction ratios.
- *Application:* Conveyors, elevators, tuning mechanisms.

**6. Rack and Pinion (Linear Output):**
Converts rotary motion to linear motion. *Application:* Steering systems, CNC machines.

> **(b) Two spur gears in external mesh have a module of 4 mm and a velocity ratio of 3:1. If the pinion rotates at 600 rpm, determine: (i) the number of teeth on each gear, (ii) the pitch circle diameters, (iii) the center distance, and (iv) the pitch line velocity.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
**Given:**
- Module, $m = 4$ mm
- Velocity Ratio, $VR = 3:1 = N_2/N_1 = T_1/T_2 = D_1/D_2$
- Pinion speed, $N_1 = 600$ rpm

**To Find:** $T_1, T_2, D_1, D_2, C, v$

**Step 1 — Number of Teeth (i):**
To avoid interference, the minimum number of teeth for a 20° full-depth involute system is 18 (or 17 for undercut allowance). Let us take $T_1 = 20$ (smallest practical pinion teeth). Then:

$$
T_2 = VR \cdot T_1 = 3 \times 20 = 60
$$

Therefore: $T_1 = 20$, $T_2 = 60$ (0.5 Mark)

**Step 2 — Pitch Circle Diameters (ii):**

$$
D_1 = m \cdot T_1 = 4 \times 20 = 80 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_2 = m \cdot T_2 = 4 \times 60 = 240 \text{ mm}
$$

(1 Mark)

**Step 3 — Center Distance (iii):**

$$
C = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{80 + 240}{2} = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

(1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Pitch Line Velocity (iv):**

$$
v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.080 \times 600}{60}
$$

(Converting $D_1 = 80$ mm = 0.080 m)

$$
v = \frac{\pi \times 48}{60} = \frac{150.796}{60} \approx 2.513 \text{ m/s}
$$

(1.5 Marks)

**Speed Classification:** Since $v \approx 2.51$ m/s < 3 m/s, this is a **low-speed gear pair**. (0.5 Mark)

**Final Answer Boxed:**

$$
\boxed{T_1 = 20,\; T_2 = 60,\; D_1 = 80 \text{ mm},\; D_2 = 240 \text{ mm},\; C = 160 \text{ mm},\; v \approx 2.51 \text{ m/s (low speed)}}
$$

---

### **Option B (Internal Choice):**

> **(a) Explain the construction and working of a worm and worm wheel with a neat sketch. State its advantages, disadvantages, and applications.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
A **worm** is a screw-like cylindrical gear (typically with 1 to 4 threads), while the **worm wheel** resembles a spur gear with curved teeth that wrap around the worm to engage the threads. The axes of the two shafts are typically at 90° and are *non-intersecting*. **(1.5 Marks)**

**Working Principle:** When the worm rotates, its helical threads push the teeth of the worm wheel tangentially, causing it to rotate. The contact is primarily **sliding**, leading to significant frictional losses. **(1 Mark)**

**Advantages:** (2 Marks)
- High velocity ratio (up to 100:1) in a single stage.
- Compact design and self-locking capability (in single-thread worm, the wheel cannot drive the worm — used in lifts, cranes).
- Smooth and quiet operation.

**Disadvantages:** (1 Mark)
- Low efficiency (40–90%) due to sliding friction.
- Requires expensive anti-friction materials (e.g., phosphor bronze wheel meshing with hardened steel worm).

**Applications:** (1.5 Marks)
- Lifts, elevators, conveyor systems, steering mechanisms of heavy vehicles, small electric motor speed reducers.

> **(b) A pair of helical gears in external mesh has a normal module of 5 mm and a helix angle of 20°. The number of teeth on the pinion and gear are 18 and 54, respectively. Calculate: (i) the virtual formative number of teeth, (ii) the virtual formative module, and (iii) the axial pitch of the helical gear.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**
**Given:** $m_n = 5$ mm, $\alpha = 20°$, $T_1 = 18$, $T_2 = 54$

**Step 1 — Virtual (Formative) Number of Teeth (i):**

$$
T_v = \frac{T}{\cos^3 \alpha} = \frac{18}{\cos^3 20°}
$$

Computing $\cos 20° \approx 0.9397$, so $\cos^3 20° \approx 0.8296$:

$$
T_{v1} = \frac{18}{0.8296} \approx 21.7 \approx 22
$$

(1.5 Marks)

$$
T_{v2} = \frac{54}{0.8296} \approx 65.1 \approx 65
$$

(1.5 Marks)

**Step 2 — Virtual Formative Module (ii):**

$$
m_v = \frac{m_n}{\cos \alpha} = \frac{5}{\cos 20°} = \frac{5}{0.9397} \approx 5.32 \text{ mm}
$$

(1.5 Marks)

**Step 3 — Axial Pitch (iii):**
The axial pitch $p_a$ is the distance between corresponding points on consecutive teeth measured along the axis. The relation is:

$$
p_a = \frac{\pi \cdot m_n}{\tan \alpha}
$$

Substituting:

$$
p_a = \frac{\pi \times 5}{\tan 20°} = \frac{15.708}{0.3640} \approx 43.15 \text{ mm}
$$

(2 Marks)

**Final Boxed Answer:**

$$
\boxed{T_{v1} \approx 22,\; T_{v2} \approx 65,\; m_v \approx 5.32 \text{ mm},\; p_a \approx 43.15 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Inverted Velocity Ratio:** Many students write $VR = T_2/T_1$ instead of $T_1/T_2$. KTU convention: $VR = N_{driven}/N_{driver} = T_{driver}/T_{driven}$. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Module Confusion:** In helical gears, $m_n$ (normal) ≠ $m$ (transverse). The pitch line velocity uses the **transverse module**, not the normal. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. **Pinion vs Gear Tooth Selection:** In problems, students often assign the larger number of teeth to the pinion. By definition, the **pinion is the smaller gear (driver)**, regardless of whether it is on the input or output shaft.
> 4. **Forgetting 20° Pressure Angle Standard:** The KTU textbook default pressure angle is 20°. The minimum number of teeth without undercut = 18. Writing 12 or 15 will earn a deduction.
> 5. **Unit Errors in Pitch Line Velocity:** $D$ must be in **meters** (not mm) when using $v = \pi D N / 60$ to get m/s.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultra-Compact Revision Block — Save This for Last-Minute Prep**

**1. Classification — Master Framework:**
- **Parallel:** Spur, Helical, Double Helical (Herringbone), Rack-and-Pinion
- **Intersecting:** Straight Bevel, Spiral Bevel, Mitre, Angular
- **Skew (non-intersecting):** Worm & Wheel, Hypoid, Crossed Helical

**2. Velocity Ratio Master Equation:**

$$
\boxed{VR = \frac{N_{driven}}{N_{driver}} = \frac{T_{driver}}{T_{driven}} = \frac{D_{driver}}{D_{driven}}}
$$

**3. Module is the Universal Standard:** $m = D/T$. Same module for both gears in mesh.

**4. Pitch Line Velocity:**

$$
\boxed{v = \frac{\pi D N}{60}} \quad [\text{m/s when D in meters}]
$$

**5. Center Distance:**

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}}
$$

**6. Bevel Gear Sinusoidal VR:**

$$
\boxed{VR_{bevel} = \frac{\sin \gamma}{\sin \Sigma}}
$$

**7. Helical Gear Formative (Virtual) Teeth:** $T_v = T / \cos^3 \alpha$

**8. Worm VR:** $VR = N_{worm}/N_{wheel} = T_{wheel}/T_{worm}$

**9. Speed Classification Rule of Thumb:**
- $v < 3$ m/s → **Low speed** (cast iron permissible)
- $3 \le v < 15$ m/s → **Medium speed** (steel required)
- $v \ge 15$ m/s → **High speed** (alloy steel, hardened & ground)

**10. KTU 2024 Examiner's "Likes":**
- A labeled sketch of the gear type (full marks for diagrams).
- Stating the **classification criterion** explicitly before listing the type.
- Mentioning **applications** along with each gear type.
- Using standard **AGMA / IS module series** (e.g., 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10).

**11. Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use **diametral pitch (P)** and **module (m)** in the same problem — they belong to different unit systems (imperial vs metric). The KTU 2024 syllabus is **strictly metric**.

**12. Why Herringbone is Special:** It is the *only* helical-type gear where axial thrust **cancels naturally** without thrust bearings — making it ideal for very high-power parallel-shaft drives.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminology and Definitions of Spur Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Terminology and Definitions of Spur Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Spur Gear?

A **Spur Gear** is the simplest and most common type of gear, consisting of a cylindrical disk with teeth projecting radially and parallel to the axis of rotation. They are used to transmit motion and power between parallel shafts without any axial thrust, making them ideal for moderate-speed applications like automobile gearboxes, printing machinery, and machine tool drives.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, you must be able to **sketch and label** every standard spur gear terminology on a clean drawing. Examiners award marks for correct labeling of the *Pitch Circle*, *Addendum Circle*, *Dedendum Circle*, *Base Circle*, and *Line of Action*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Rolling Coins Thought Experiment

Imagine two coins rolling against each other on a flat table without slipping. There is an imaginary circle on each coin — the **Pitch Circle** — where they effectively "kiss" each other and pure rolling occurs. The velocity at this imaginary contact point is the same for both gears. Now imagine cutting tiny teeth around the edges of these coins so they engage smoothly without jamming. The geometry of where these teeth must mesh (size, shape, spacing) is what *Gear Terminology* defines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Favorite):**
> The **Pitch Circle** is an imaginary circle on a pair of meshing gears such that they roll on each other without slipping. Its diameter is called the **Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD)**, and the point of contact between the two pitch circles is the **Pitch Point (P)**.

## 1.3 Why Study Gear Terminology?

Every engineering parameter — strength, wear life, noise, efficiency, manufacturability — is derived directly from these standard definitions. Before you can analyze *gear trains*, *interference*, or *minimum teeth*, you must master these foundational terms cold.

**The fundamental dimensional constants you must commit to memory:**

- **Module (m)** = Pitch Circle Diameter / Number of Teeth (in **mm**)
- **Circular Pitch (p)** = π × m (in **mm**)
- **Diametral Pitch (P)** = Number of Teeth / Pitch Circle Diameter (in **inches⁻¹**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Spur Gear Terminology — Complete Breakdown

Let us break down every term into a structured, exam-ready format.

### A. The Circles (Five Concentric Circles)

A standard spur gear has **five key concentric circles**. From outer to inner:

1. **Addendum Circle (Tip Circle)** — The outermost circle, drawn through the tips of the teeth. Diameter = **D + 2a**, where `a` is the addendum.
2. **Pitch Circle** — The imaginary circle of rolling contact. Diameter = **D**.
3. **Base Circle** — The circle from which the involute tooth profile is generated. Diameter = **D cos φ**, where `φ` is the pressure angle.
4. **Dedendum Circle (Root Circle)** — The circle passing through the bottom of the tooth spaces. Diameter = **D − 2b**, where `b` is the dedendum.
5. **Clearance Circle** — The inner circle that defines the gap between the tip of one gear and the root of the mating gear.

### B. Tooth Dimensions

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addendum | a | Height of tooth above pitch circle | **a = m** (1 module) |
| Dedendum | b | Depth of tooth below pitch circle | **b = 1.157 m** to **1.25 m** |
| Working Depth | h_w | Depth of tooth engagement | **h_w = 2m** |
| Total Depth | h_t | Sum of addendum + dedendum | **h_t = 2.157 m** to **2.25 m** |
| Clearance | c | Gap between mating tooth tip and root | **c = 0.157 m** to **0.25 m** |
| Tooth Thickness | t | Arc length of tooth on pitch circle | **t = p/2 = πm/2** |
| Tooth Space | s | Arc length of gap on pitch circle | **s = p/2 = πm/2** |
| Backlash | b_k | Difference between tooth space and tooth thickness | **b_k ≈ 0.04m** |
| Face Width | F | Axial length of gear tooth | Application dependent |

### C. The Dynamic Action Terms

- **Pressure Angle (φ)** — The angle between the line of action (common tangent at pitch point) and the common normal to the pitch circles. Standard values: **14.5°**, **20°**, **25°**.
- **Line of Action** — The path along which the contact point between two meshing teeth travels.
- **Path of Contact** — Total length of the line of action over which one pair of teeth remains in contact.
- **Arc of Contact** — The arc traced on the pitch circle by a point on one tooth from the start to the end of mesh.
- **Angle of Action** — The angle swept by a gear during one complete mesh cycle.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — The Cheat Code Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU problem starts here.** Use `\vert` for absolute values to maintain table integrity.

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Module | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | mm |
| Pitch Circle Diameter | $D = mT$ | mm |
| Circular Pitch | $p = \pi m$ | mm |
| Diametral Pitch | $P = \dfrac{T}{D}$ | per inch |
| Relation: Module vs Diametral Pitch | $P = \dfrac{25.4}{m}$ | per inch |
| Addendum | $a = m$ | mm |
| Dedendum (Full depth system) | $b = 1.157 m$ | mm |
| Total Depth | $h_t = 2.157 m$ | mm |
| Working Depth | $h_w = 2m$ | mm |
| Clearance | $c = 0.157 m$ | mm |
| Base Circle Diameter | $D_b = D \cos\phi$ | mm |
| Tooth Thickness | $t = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | mm |
| Pitch Point Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These terminologies are not academic curiosities — they directly drive design decisions:

- **Automotive Industry** — Standard modules (e.g., m = 2.5 mm) allow interchangeability of gears across manufacturers.
- **Manufacturing (Hobbing, Shaping)** — Tool selection is dictated entirely by module and pressure angle.
- **Failure Analysis** — Tooth breakage vs. pitting vs. wear is interpreted via gear geometry.
- **Noise & Vibration** — Pressure angle and tip modification terms directly govern meshing stiffness.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Establishing the Module from Pitch Circle and Teeth

The most fundamental definition in gear design starts with the relationship between the pitch circle circumference and the number of teeth.

**Step 1 — Start with the circumference of the pitch circle.**

The circumference of the pitch circle of diameter `D` is given by:
$$C = \pi D$$

**Step 2 — Define circular pitch.**

By definition, the circular pitch `p` is the arc length measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth. Since there are `T` teeth arranged around the circumference, each tooth occupies an arc of length equal to the circular pitch.
$$p = \frac{\pi D}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Define module.**

Rearranging for `D`, we get $D = pT / \pi$. To eliminate π from the equation, we define a new parameter `m` such that:
$$m = \frac{p}{\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain the canonical module formula.**

Substituting back:
$$D = mT \quad \Longrightarrow \quad m = \frac{D}{T}$$

**Conclusion:** Module is the ratio of pitch diameter to number of teeth. This is the master equation that drives every gear dimension.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Base Circle Diameter from Pressure Angle

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.**

When two involute teeth mesh, the line joining the centers of the two base circles is perpendicular to the line of action. The pressure angle `φ` is measured at the pitch point between the line of action and the line joining the gear centers (which is also the tangent at the pitch point).

**Step 2 — Right triangle construction.**

Draw the line of action. The center of the gear, the pitch point, and the foot of perpendicular from center to line of action form a right-angled triangle.
- Hypotenuse = Pitch radius `R = D/2`
- Adjacent side = Base radius `R_b = D_b/2`
- Angle between them = Pressure angle `φ`

**Step 3 — Apply cosine ratio.**

From trigonometry:
$$\cos\phi = \frac{R_b}{R} = \frac{D_b/2}{D/2} = \frac{D_b}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the base circle diameter.**
$$D_b = D \cos\phi$$

This is why involute curves are drawn with the base circle as the fundamental reference — the involute is *generated* by unwinding a string from this circle.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tooth Thickness on the Pitch Circle

**Step 1 — Recall the equal division principle.**

For a correctly designed gear, the tooth and the space must each occupy exactly half of the circular pitch on the pitch circle. This ensures proper meshing.

**Step 2 — Apply the division.**

$$\text{Tooth Thickness} + \text{Tooth Space} = \text{Circular Pitch}$$
$$t + s = p$$

By the equal design principle:
$$t = s = \frac{p}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the circular pitch in terms of module.**
$$t = \frac{p}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2}$$

**Conclusion:** Tooth thickness `t = πm/2` and tooth space `s = πm/2` are the standard dimensions on the pitch circle.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pitch Point Velocity (Linear Velocity at Pitch Point)

**Step 1 — Angular velocity to linear velocity conversion.**

If the gear rotates at `N` revolutions per minute, the angular velocity is:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity at the pitch radius.**
$$v = \omega \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ω.**
$$v = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Convert D from mm to m by dividing by 1000 if required.**
$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(m/s, when D is in mm)}$$

This linear velocity is **identical for both meshing gears at the pitch point** — this is the kinematic foundation of the constant velocity ratio theorem.

## 3.5 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Approach |
|---|---|---|---|
| Find Module | D, T | m | Use $m = D/T$ |
| Find PCD | m, T | D | Use $D = mT$ |
| Find Tooth Dimensions | m | a, b, h_t, h_w | Apply standard ratios |
| Find Base Circle | D, φ | D_b | Use $D_b = D\cos\phi$ |
| Find Tooth Thickness | m | t | Use $t = \pi m / 2$ |
| Find Pitch Velocity | D, N | v | Use $v = \pi DN / 60000$ m/s |
| Convert Module to DP | m | P | Use $P = 25.4 / m$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Conceptual Architecture of Gear Terminology

Below is a block-level functional architecture map showing how every terminology connects, which is how KTU examiners expect students to mentally organize the topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SPUR GEAR GEOMETRY] --> B[GEAR 1]
    A --> C[GEAR 2]
    
    B --> B1[Five Concentric Circles]
    B1 --> B1a[Addendum Circle D plus 2a]
    B1 --> B1b[Pitch Circle D]
    B1 --> B1c[Base Circle D cos phi]
    B1 --> B1d[Dedendum Circle D minus 2b]
    B1 --> B1e[Clearance Circle]
    
    B --> B2[Tooth Dimensions]
    B2 --> B2a[Addendum a equals m]
    B2 --> B2b[Dedendum b equals 1.157 m]
    B2 --> B2c[Working Depth h_w equals 2m]
    B2 --> B2d[Total Depth h_t equals 2.157 m]
    B2 --> B2e[Tooth Thickness t equals pi m by 2]
    
    C --> D[Kinematic Parameters]
    D --> D1[Pitch Point P]
    D --> D2[Line of Action]
    D --> D3[Pressure Angle phi]
    D --> D4[Pitch Velocity v equals pi DN by 60000]
    
    B --> E[Module System]
    E --> E1[Module m equals D by T]
    E --> E2[Circular Pitch p equals pi m]
    E --> E3[Diametral Pitch P equals T by D]
    
    B1b --> F[At Pitch Point]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Constant Velocity Ratio]
    G --> G1[N1 by N2 equals T2 by T1 equals D2 by D1]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Standard Spur Gear Cross-Sectional View (Labeled Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEAR_A[GEAR A Cross Section]
        A1[Outer Boundary: ADDENDUM CIRCLE Diameter D plus 2a]
        A2[Middle Reference: PITCH CIRCLE Diameter D]
        A3[Inner Reference: BASE CIRCLE Diameter D cos phi]
        A4[Bottom Boundary: DEDENDUM CIRCLE Diameter D minus 2b]
        A5[Tooth Tip projects outward by a equals m]
        A6[Tooth Root cut inward by b equals 1.157 m]
    end
    
    A1 --> A5
    A2 --> A5
    A3 --> A2
    A4 --> A6
    A2 --> A6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Drawing Tip:** Always draw the **Pitch Circle** as a continuous thin line, the **Addendum Circle** as the outer solid line passing through tooth tips, and the **Base Circle** as a dashed circle since it is rarely the outermost or innermost visible feature. Label every radius with the correct symbol.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mesh Pair Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[MESHING PAIR OF SPUR GEARS] --> M1[PINION Small Gear]
    M --> M2[GEAR Large Gear]
    
    M1 --> M1a[Pitch Diameter d equals mT1]
    M1 --> M1b[Teeth T1]
    M1 --> M1c[Speed N1 rpm]
    
    M2 --> M2a[Pitch Diameter D equals mT2]
    M2 --> M2b[Teeth T2]
    M2 --> M2c[Speed N2 rpm]
    
    M1c --> R[CONSTRAINT RELATION]
    M2c --> R
    M1a --> R
    M2a --> R
    M1b --> R
    M2b --> R
    
    R --> R1[N1 T1 equals N2 T2]
    R --> R2[Velocity at pitch point is equal]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following with neat sketches: (i) Pitch Circle, (ii) Circular Pitch, (iii) Module.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Pitch Circle:** It is an imaginary circle on a pair of meshing gears on which the two gears appear to roll without slipping. The diameter of this circle is called the Pitch Circle Diameter.
- **(ii) Circular Pitch (p):** It is the distance measured along the circumference of the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth. Mathematically, $p = \pi D / T$.
- **(iii) Module (m):** It is the ratio of pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth. $m = D/T$. It is the fundamental parameter that standardizes gear tooth sizes.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the Base Circle and the Pitch Circle. Why is the involute curve generated from the base circle?**

**Model Answer:**
| Parameter | Pitch Circle | Base Circle |
|---|---|---|
| Diameter | $D = mT$ | $D_b = D \cos\phi$ |
| Geometric Role | Rolling without slipping reference | Origin of involute tooth profile |
| Visibility on drawing | Solid thin line | Often dashed inner circle |

The involute curve is generated from the base circle because the involute is formed by unwinding a taut string from the base circle. This property gives the involute gear its unique advantage of constant pressure angle during mesh, ensuring smooth transmission.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

### Question:
**(a)** Define the following gear terms: Addendum, Dedendum, Working Depth, Total Depth, Clearance, and Tooth Thickness. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two spur gears with module 4 mm mesh externally. The pinion has 18 teeth and rotates at 240 rpm. The gear has 72 teeth. Determine: **(i)** Pitch Circle Diameters, **(ii)** Addendum and Dedendum values, **(iii)** Tooth Thickness, **(iv)** Pitch Point Velocity, **(v)** Contact Ratio (path of contact length if addendum circle radius and base circle radius are given — assume standard values). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Definitions (7 Marks)

1. **Addendum (a):** The radial distance from the pitch circle to the tip of the tooth. Standard value $a = m$.
2. **Dedendum (b):** The radial distance from the pitch circle to the bottom of the tooth space. Standard value $b = 1.157 m$.
3. **Working Depth (h_w):** The radial distance from the addendum circle of one gear to the dedendum circle of the mating gear. $h_w = 2m$.
4. **Total Depth (h_t):** The sum of addendum and dedendum. $h_t = a + b = 2.157 m$.
5. **Clearance (c):** The difference between dedendum and addendum. $c = b - a = 0.157 m$.
6. **Tooth Thickness (t):** The arc length of the tooth measured along the pitch circle. $t = \pi m / 2$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** Module $m = 4$ mm, Pinion teeth $T_1 = 18$, Gear teeth $T_2 = 72$, $N_1 = 240$ rpm, $\phi = 20°$ (standard).

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameters**

For pinion:
$$D_1 = mT_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

For gear:
$$D_2 = mT_2 = 4 \times 72 = 288 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Addendum and Dedendum**

$$\text{Addendum } a = m = 4 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{Dedendum } b = 1.157 m = 1.157 \times 4 = 4.628 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Tooth Thickness**

$$t = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{\pi \times 4}{2} = 2\pi = 6.283 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Pitch Point Velocity**

Using the pinion:
$$v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{\pi \times 72 \times 240}{60000} = 0.9048 \text{ m/s}$$

Verification using gear:
$$N_2 = \frac{N_1 T_1}{T_2} = \frac{240 \times 18}{72} = 60 \text{ rpm}$$
$$v = \frac{\pi \times 288 \times 60}{60000} = 0.9048 \text{ m/s} \quad \checkmark$$

**(v) Base Circle Diameters (for further analysis)**

$$D_{b1} = D_1 \cos 20° = 72 \times 0.9397 = 67.66 \text{ mm}$$
$$D_{b2} = D_2 \cos 20° = 288 \times 0.9397 = 270.63 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

### Question:
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the terms: Pressure Angle, Line of Action, Path of Contact, Arc of Contact, and Angle of Action. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A spur gear has a module of 3 mm and 30 teeth. It rotates at 600 rpm and meshes with a 60-tooth gear. Calculate: **(i)** Pitch Circle Diameter of both gears, **(ii)** Circular Pitch, **(iii)** Diametral Pitch, **(iv)** Total Depth, **(v)** Tooth Space. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Sketches and Definitions (7 Marks)

1. **Pressure Angle (φ):** The angle between the line of action and the common tangent drawn at the pitch point. Standard values are **14.5°**, **20°**, or **25°**.
2. **Line of Action:** The straight line along which the point of contact between two meshing teeth moves during engagement.
3. **Path of Contact:** The total length of the line of action over which one pair of teeth remains in contact before the next pair engages.
4. **Arc of Contact:** The arc traced on the pitch circle by a point on one tooth from the beginning to the end of mesh engagement.
5. **Angle of Action:** The angle through which the gear rotates from the start to the end of contact between one pair of teeth.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $m = 3$ mm, $T_1 = 30$, $T_2 = 60$, $N_1 = 600$ rpm.

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameters**

$$D_1 = mT_1 = 3 \times 30 = 90 \text{ mm}$$
$$D_2 = mT_2 = 3 \times 60 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Circular Pitch**

$$p = \pi m = \pi \times 3 = 9.4248 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Diametral Pitch (DP)**

$$P = \frac{25.4}{m} = \frac{25.4}{3} = 8.4667 \text{ per inch}$$

**(iv) Total Depth**

$$h_t = 2.157 m = 2.157 \times 3 = 6.471 \text{ mm}$$

**(v) Tooth Space**

By the equal design principle, tooth space equals tooth thickness on the pitch circle:
$$s = \frac{p}{2} = \frac{\pi m}{2} = \frac{9.4248}{2} = 4.7124 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Easy Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Module is in **mm** when using SI; circular pitch will be in mm. Pitch velocity must be converted to **m/s** — many students leave it in mm/s and lose 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing Standard Values:** Addendum = 1m is standard; dedendum = **1.157 m** for full-depth teeth and **1.25 m** for some older British standards. Always state the system.
> 3. **Mislabeling the Base Circle:** The base circle is **smaller** than the pitch circle, not larger. Drawing it outside will cost marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Sketch:** Even for definition questions, a quick labeled diagram earns at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Pressure Angle Default:** If not given in a problem, **assume 20°** — this is the current KTU standard.

## 5.5 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember Before Entering the Exam Hall:**
>
> - Module $m = D/T$ — **the master equation**
> - Circular pitch $p = \pi m$
> - Diametral pitch $P = 25.4/m$ (SI to Imperial conversion constant)
> - Standard pressure angles: **14.5°, 20°, 25°** — default to 20° if unspecified
> - Addendum $a = m$ | Dedendum $b = 1.157 m$ (full-depth teeth)
> - Working depth $h_w = 2m$ | Total depth $h_t = 2.157 m$
> - Clearance $c = 0.157 m$
> - Tooth thickness = Tooth space = $p/2 = \pi m/2$ on the pitch circle
> - Base circle $D_b = D \cos\phi$ — strictly smaller than pitch circle
> - Pitch velocity $v = \pi D N / 60000$ m/s (when D is in mm)
> - Five concentric circles in order: **Addendum → Pitch → Base → Dedendum → Clearance**
> - The **Pitch Point** is where the two pitch circles touch and pure rolling occurs
> - Velocity ratio of meshing gears: $N_1/N_2 = T_2/T_1 = D_2/D_1$

---
