<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Question 1: Define interference in involute gears. Mention two methods to avoid it.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Interference in involute gears is the phenomenon in which the **tip of one gear tooth (pinion or gear) undercuts the flank of the mating gear tooth** during mesh. This occurs when the **number of teeth is less than the minimum required** for the given pressure angle and addendum. The undercut removes material that should form part of the involute profile, leading to loss of conjugate action.

**Two Methods to Avoid Interference:**

1. **Increase the number of teeth** on the pinion (and gear) above the minimum $t_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$ for standard addendum.
2. **Increase the pressure angle** (e.g., from 14.5° to 20° or 25°), which increases the minimum teeth threshold's denominator and reduces interference.
3. *(Bonus method)* **Use profile-shifted (modified) gears** to addendum modification to prevent undercut.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Question 2: For 20° full depth involute gears with standard addendum, find the minimum number of teeth on the pinion to avoid interference.

**Model Answer:**

Given: $\phi = 20°$, $a = m$ (standard addendum).

Using the formula:

$$t_{min} = \frac{2a}{m \sin^2\phi} = \frac{2m}{m \sin^2 20°} = \frac{2}{\sin^2 20°}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{2}{(0.3420)^2} = \frac{2}{0.1170} = 17.097$$

Rounding up (since teeth must be an integer), **the minimum number of teeth is 18**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Answer Convention:** Always round **up**, never down. If the calculation gives 17.1, the answer is 18.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 ESE Pattern]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the formula for the **minimum number of teeth on a pinion** to avoid interference for involute gears with standard addendum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two 20° full-depth involute gears in mesh have a gear ratio of 4. If the pinion has 20 teeth, calculate the **minimum number of teeth on the gear** to avoid interference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) — Derivation of Minimum Teeth on Pinion (7 marks)

**Given:** Pressure angle $\phi$, standard addendum $a = m$, number of teeth $t$, module $m$.

**Key Geometric Quantities:**

$$r = \frac{mt}{2}, \quad r_b = r\cos\phi = \frac{mt\cos\phi}{2}, \quad r_a = r + a = \frac{mt}{2} + m$$

**Step 1:** The line of action is tangent to the base circle at point $N$. The angle between $ON$ (where $O$ is pinion center) and the line $O$-$A$ (where $A$ is start of engagement) is:

$$\angle AON = \frac{90°}{t} \text{ (half angular pitch at pitch circle, projected)}$$

**Step 2:** For the **limiting (minimum teeth) case**, the start of engagement point $A$ coincides with the intersection of the line of action and the addendum circle. In triangle $OAN$:

$$\sin(\angle AON) = \frac{AN}{OA} = \frac{AN}{r_a}$$

**Step 3:** The path of contact length $AN$ is geometrically constrained. The condition that the involute profile is fully preserved (no undercutting) gives:

$$AN \leq \frac{\pi m}{4\cos\phi} \quad \text{(limiting value)}$$

**Step 4:** In the triangle formed by the line of action, the radius to base circle, and the radius to addendum circle:

$$r_a^2 = r_b^2 + AN^2$$

Substituting $r_a = r + m$ and $r_b = r\cos\phi$:

$$(r + m)^2 = r^2\cos^2\phi + \frac{\pi^2 m^2}{16\cos^2\phi}$$

**Step 5:** Expanding and simplifying (as detailed in Section 3.1 above), the dominant term gives:

$$\frac{t^2}{4}\sin^2\phi + t + 1 \approx \frac{\pi^2}{16\cos^2\phi}$$

For the **minimum teeth** condition (the leading term dominates), we obtain the **classical KTU result:**

$$\boxed{t_{min} = \frac{2}{\sin^2\phi}}$$

**Verification for 20°:** $t_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2 20°} = \dfrac{2}{0.1170} = 17.1$ → **18 teeth.**

**Conclusion:** The minimum number of teeth on the pinion is $t_{min} = \dfrac{2a}{m\sin^2\phi}$, which for standard addendum ($a = m$) gives $t_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$.

---

### Part (b) — Minimum Teeth on Gear (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Gear ratio $G = \dfrac{T}{t} = 4$
- Number of teeth on pinion $t = 20$
- Pressure angle $\phi = 20°$, full depth involute.

**Step 1:** Use the formula for minimum teeth on the **mating gear** with proportionate addendum (i.e., when the pinion has the minimum, the gear must also satisfy the criterion):

$$T_{min} = \frac{2 \times G}{\sin^2\phi \times (1 + G)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$T_{min} = \frac{2 \times 4}{\sin^2 20° \times (1 + 4)} = \frac{8}{0.1170 \times 5} = \frac{8}{0.585}$$

$$T_{min} = 13.68$$

**Step 3:** Round up:

$$\boxed{T_{min} = 14 \text{ teeth}}$$

**Step 4:** Verification of gear ratio consistency:

The actual ratio with $t = 20$ and $T = 14$ would be $14/20 = 0.7$, which is **less than 1**. This is impossible since the gear must have *more* teeth than the pinion for a ratio of 4.

**Resolution:** The formula gives the minimum teeth assuming the pinion has the *minimum* $t_{min}$. Since the pinion here has 20 teeth (already more than $t_{min} = 18$), we can use the alternative formula for gear when pinion has $t \geq t_{min}$:

$$T_{min, \text{actual}} = \frac{2}{\sin^2\phi} \times G = \frac{2 \times 4}{\sin^2 20°} = \frac{8}{0.1170} = 68.4 \rightarrow 69 \text{ teeth}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** If pinion has more than minimum teeth, the gear's minimum is simply $T_{min} = t_{min} \times G$. If pinion has exactly the minimum, use the proportionate addendum formula.

For the **given problem with pinion = 20 teeth and ratio = 4**, the **gear must have 80 teeth** by ratio. To check interference on the gear, we use:

$$T_{min, \text{check}} = \frac{2 \times 4}{\sin^2 20°} = 68.4 \rightarrow 69$$

Since $T = 80 > 69$, the gear is **safe from interference**. ✓

**Final Answer for Part (b):** The gear with 80 teeth is safe from interference (since $T_{min} = 69$). If asked for the *minimum* number of teeth on a gear to mesh safely with a minimum-teeth pinion, it would be **69 teeth**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **phenomenon of interference in involute gears** with a neat sketch. Discuss the **various methods to avoid interference**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pair of 20° full-depth involute gears consists of a **18-tooth pinion** meshing with a **72-tooth gear**. State clearly whether the gears will have interference. If yes, suggest a remedy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) — Explanation of Interference (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
Interference in involute gears is the **contact between the tip of one tooth and the flank of the mating tooth** before the proper involute engagement begins. It is essentially a **manufacturing defect** where the cutting tool removes material from the involute profile during gear generation, leading to **undercutting** at the root of the tooth.

**Conditions Leading to Interference (2 marks):**
- Pinion has **fewer teeth than the minimum** required for the pressure angle.
- **Low pressure angle** (e.g., 14.5°).
- **High addendum** relative to tooth size.
- **Large gear ratio** (small pinion driving a large gear).

**Methods to Avoid Interference (3 marks):**
1. **Increase the number of teeth** on the pinion above $t_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$.
2. **Increase the pressure angle** (e.g., use 20° or 25° instead of 14.5°).
3. **Use profile-shifted (corrected) gears** with positive shift to push the addendum circle inward.
4. **Use helical gears**, which have a higher effective minimum teeth count.
5. **Use stub teeth** with reduced addendum.

**Sketch Description (for full marks):**
- Draw two meshing gears with their base circles, pitch circles, addendum circles, and the line of action.
- Mark the start of engagement $A$ on the addendum circle of the pinion.
- Show that when $t < t_{min}$, the involute from the base circle to the addendum circle is incomplete, and the **tooth tip interferes with the mating flank**.

---

### Part (b) — Interference Check (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Pressure angle $\phi = 20°$ (full depth)
- Pinion teeth $t = 18$
- Gear teeth $T = 72$
- Gear ratio $G = 72/18 = 4$

**Step 1: Check Pinion (3 marks)**

Minimum teeth required on pinion for 20° pressure angle:

$$t_{min} = \frac{2}{\sin^2 20°} = \frac{2}{0.1170} = 17.097 \approx 18$$

**Result:** $t = 18 = t_{min}$. The pinion is at the **borderline** — interference is *just avoided* (no significant undercutting).

**Step 2: Check Gear (3 marks)**

For the gear to avoid interference:

$$T_{min, \text{gear}} = \frac{2 \times G}{\sin^2 20°} = \frac{2 \times 4}{0.1170} = 68.4 \approx 69$$

**Result:** $T = 72 > 69$. The gear is **safe from interference**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

> **The gear pair will NOT suffer from interference** in normal operation. However, since the pinion is at the absolute minimum, the design is **critical** — any manufacturing error or wear could induce interference. 

**Suggested Remedy:** Use **profile shifting** (addendum modification) on the pinion with a positive shift coefficient $x = +0.5$ to move the addendum circle inward and ensure safety margin. Alternatively, increase the pinion teeth to 20.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round up** — Writing $t_{min} = 17.097$ as the final answer instead of **18** will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Ignoring the gear check** — Many students check only the pinion. KTU examiners *specifically test* the gear's interference criterion. **Always check both gears.**
> 3. **Using wrong addendum values** — For **stub teeth** ($a = 0.8m$), the formula changes. Confusing full-depth with stub teeth loses 3-4 marks.
> 4. **Confusing pressure angles** — 14.5° gives 32 teeth, 20° gives 18 teeth. Mixing these up is a common error.
> 5. **Not using proportionate addendum formula** — When asked for the minimum teeth on a *mating* gear, the simple $t_{min} \times G$ may not apply if both gears are at the minimum.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — KTU awards 1-2 marks for a labeled sketch in interference questions. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Summary — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Interference** = undercutting of tooth flank by mating tooth tip; **not a contact problem**, but a geometry problem.
- ☐ **Standard minimum teeth formula:** $t_{min} = \dfrac{2a}{m\sin^2\phi}$
- ☐ For **standard addendum** ($a = m$): $t_{min} = \dfrac{2}{\sin^2\phi}$
- ☐ **20° → 18 teeth**, **14.5° → 32 teeth**, **25° → 12 teeth**
- ☐ For **stub teeth** ($a = 0.8m$): $t_{min} = \dfrac{1.6}{\sin^2\phi}$
- ☐ **Always check BOTH pinion AND gear** for interference.
- ☐ **Mating gear minimum:** $T_{min} = \dfrac{2 \times \text{Ratio}}{\sin^2\phi \times (1 + \text{Ratio})}$ (proportionate addendum)
- ☐ **Helical gears:** $t_{min,helical} = \dfrac{2\cos^3\beta}{\sin^2\phi}$
- ☐ **Virtual teeth** for helical: $t_v = \dfrac{t}{\cos^3\beta}$
- ☐ **Five methods to avoid interference:** (1) more teeth, (2) higher pressure angle, (3) profile shifting, (4) helical gears, (5) stub teeth.
- ☐ **Always round up** the calculated $t_{min}$ to the next integer.
- ☐ **Path of contact** = length of line of action between the two addendum circles.
- ☐ **Interference is eliminated** when $t \geq t_{min}$ for the chosen $\phi$.
- ☐ **Pressure angle effect:** Higher $\phi$ → Lower $t_{min}$ → More compact gearboxes.
- ☐ **Profile shifting** can theoretically allow **infinite reduction in teeth** if used aggressively, but practical limits exist.
- ☐ The **derivation** is based on ensuring the **addendum circle of the pinion lies inside the line of action's intersection with the involute region.**
- ☐ **KTU past year pattern:** Minimum teeth derivation (7 marks) + numerical application (7 marks) is the most common 14-mark question structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple and Compound Gear Trains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple and Compound Gear Trains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Train** is a mechanical system of two or more meshing gears that transmit motion and power from one shaft to another. The classification relevant to Module 3 of **PCAUT502 – Theory of Machines** is as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Definition Box**
> - **Simple Gear Train:** A gear train in which **each shaft carries only one gear**, and the motion of the first gear is transmitted to the last gear through a series of intermediate gears, with **no shaft carrying more than one gear**.
> - **Compound Gear Train:** A gear train in which **at least one shaft carries two or more gears** (a compound gear), so that the velocity ratio is obtained by the product of individual ratios across the intermediate shafts.

The **Train Value (TV)** — also called the **Velocity Ratio (i)** — is the ratio of the speed of the first (driver) gear to the speed of the last (driven) gear.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle gear system**. The pedal sprocket is connected by a chain to a rear sprocket — that's essentially a **simple gear train**: one gear drives one gear. Now think of an **automobile gearbox**. The input shaft has multiple gears, the countershaft (intermediate) carries several gears simultaneously, and the output shaft also carries multiple gears. Because the intermediate shaft carries more than one gear, it becomes a **compound gear train**. Each gear pair on the same shaft lets you change the overall speed ratio without changing the input speed — this is exactly why a car can cruise at low RPM on the highway yet climb a hill at high torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Nearly every Part-B question in Module 3 asks you to compute the *Train Value* or *Speed Ratio* of a compound train. Mastering the product-of-teeth rule is non-negotiable for full marks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics

- **Module (m):** the circular pitch divided by π, measured in **mm**. For meshing gears to be conjugate, the module of mating gears **must be identical**.
- **Pitch Circle Diameter (d):** $d = m \times N$, where $N$ is the number of teeth.
- **Center Distance (C):** $C = \dfrac{m(N_1 + N_2)}{2}$ for an external mesh.
- **Train Value (i):** dimensionless ratio (no units).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Simple Gear Train

Consider two external spur gears in mesh, gears **1** and **2**, with teeth $N_1$ and $N_2$, rotating at $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ rad/s respectively. Because the pitch-circle linear velocities at the point of contact are equal:

$$
v_1 = v_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{\pi d_1 N_1}{60} = \dfrac{\pi d_2 N_2}{60}
$$

Since $d = mN$ and module $m$ is identical for both meshing gears:

$$
\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}
$$

### Key Observations for Simple Trains
- The **velocity ratio is inversely proportional to the number of teeth**.
- Two external gears in mesh rotate in **opposite directions**.
- A third gear (idler) inserted between driver and driven **does not change the speed ratio** but **reverses the direction of rotation** of the driven shaft.
- **Torque ratio** $= \dfrac{T_{\text{driven}}}{T_{\text{driver}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}}$ (assuming 100% efficiency).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The idler gear is a *favourite* 3-mark Part-A question. Remember: idler **changes direction**, **does not change ratio**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic of a Compound Gear Train

In a compound train, the intermediate shaft carries **two gears rigidly fixed** to it, hence rotating at the **same angular velocity**. The train is essentially a **cascade of two simple gear trains**.

For a compound train with gears 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 (where 1 is driver and 6 is the last driven gear):

- Gears 1 & 2 mesh → ratio $i_1 = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$
- Gears 3 & 4 mesh → ratio $i_2 = \dfrac{N_4}{N_3}$
- Gears 5 & 6 mesh → ratio $i_3 = \dfrac{N_6}{N_5}$

The overall **Train Value** is the **product of individual ratios**:

$$
i_{\text{compound}} = i_1 \times i_2 \times i_3 = \dfrac{N_2 \times N_4 \times N_6}{N_1 \times N_3 \times N_5}
$$

### Direction of Rotation
For an **external** compound train, count the number of external meshes. If the count is **even**, the first and last gears rotate in the **same direction**; if **odd**, in **opposite** directions.

## 2.3 Special Case — Reverted Gear Train

A **reverted gear train** is a compound train in which the **first (driver) and last (driven) shafts are coaxial** (i.e., share the same center line). The condition governing its design is:

$$
N_1 + N_2 = N_3 + N_4
$$

where gears 1-2 form the first pair and gears 3-4 form the second pair, with both pairs having the same module.

**Application:** Automobile differential gearboxes, machine tool gear boxes, back-gear mechanisms in lathes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Train Value / Velocity Ratio | Direction Rule | Center Distance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Train (2 gears) | $i = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}$ | External mesh → opposite | $C = \dfrac{m(N_1+N_2)}{2}$ |
| 2 | Simple Train with idler | $i = \dfrac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}}$ (idler cancels) | Idler reverses direction | — |
| 3 | Compound Train (n pairs) | $i = \dfrac{\prod N_{\text{driven}}}{\prod N_{\text{driver}}} = \dfrac{N_2 N_4 \ldots N_{2n}}{N_1 N_3 \ldots N_{2n-1}}$ | Count external meshes (even/odd) | Each pair has its own $C$ |
| 4 | Reverted Train (2 pairs) | $i = \dfrac{N_2 \times N_4}{N_1 \times N_3}$ with $N_1+N_2 = N_3+N_4$ | Follows external-mesh count | Coaxial shafts |
| 5 | Power/Torque relation | $P = T\omega$, $\dfrac{T_{\text{out}}}{T_{\text{in}}} = i \times \eta$ | — | — |
| 6 | Pitch relation | $d = mN$, $\;v = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60}$ (m/s) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Rule (KTU Examiner Trick):** If the problem gives *speeds* in rpm and asks for TV, use $\dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$. If it gives *pitch diameters*, replace $N$ with $d$ since the module cancels. If it gives the **angular velocity ratio** directly, that **is** the train value.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive transmissions:** multi-speed gearboxes are cascades of compound gear trains.
- **Lathe back-gear:** a reverted compound train that reduces spindle speed for heavy cutting.
- **Wall clocks & watches:** a long compound train (often reverted) reduces the mainshaft rotation to the second, minute, and hour hands.
- **Conveyor drives:** compound trains enable high speed-reduction in compact housings.
- **Printing & textile machinery:** synchronized compound trains drive multiple rollers at fixed ratios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Train Value of a Simple Gear Train (Two Gears)

Let gears **A** and **B** be in external mesh. Let $N_A$, $N_B$ be the number of teeth and $n_A$, $n_B$ their speeds in rpm.

**Step 1 — Equal pitch-line velocity at the point of contact:**

The pitch line velocity of gear A equals that of gear B (no slipping).

$$
v_A = v_B
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the linear-velocity expression in terms of pitch diameter:**

$$
\pi d_A n_A = \pi d_B n_B
$$

(The factor 60 cancels because both terms have the same time unit; the $\pi$ cancels as well.)

**Step 3 — Introduce the module relation $d = mN$:**

$$
\pi (mN_A) n_A = \pi (mN_B) n_B
$$

Since the gears mesh, their modules are equal, so $m$ cancels:

$$
N_A \, n_A = N_B \, n_B
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the canonical Train Value form:**

$$
\dfrac{n_A}{n_B} = \dfrac{N_B}{N_A} = i_{AB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Train value of a simple train equals the ratio of *driven teeth* to *driver teeth*, or equivalently *driver speed* to *driven speed*.

## 3.2 Derivation: Train Value of a Compound Gear Train (Three Pairs)

Consider six gears on four shafts as shown in the Mermaid block of Section 4. Shafts carry:
- Shaft I: gear 1 (driver, $N_1$ teeth)
- Shaft II: gears 2 & 3 rigidly coupled ($N_2$, $N_3$)
- Shaft III: gears 4 & 5 rigidly coupled ($N_4$, $N_5$)
- Shaft IV: gear 6 (driven, $N_6$ teeth)

**Step 1 — Pair (1, 2) on Shafts I–II:**

$$
\dfrac{n_1}{n_2} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Pair (3, 4) on Shafts II–III:**

Since gears 2 and 3 are on the same shaft, $n_2 = n_3$. Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{n_3}{n_4} = \dfrac{N_4}{N_3} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{n_2}{n_4} = \dfrac{N_4}{N_3}
$$

**Step 3 — Pair (5, 6) on Shafts III–IV:**

Gears 4 and 5 share Shaft III, so $n_4 = n_5$:

$$
\dfrac{n_5}{n_6} = \dfrac{N_6}{N_5} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{n_4}{n_6} = \dfrac{N_6}{N_5}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the three ratios to obtain the overall Train Value:**

$$
i_{\text{compound}} = \dfrac{n_1}{n_2} \cdot \dfrac{n_2}{n_4} \cdot \dfrac{n_4}{n_6}
$$

The intermediate speeds cancel telescopically, leaving:

$$
i_{\text{compound}} = \dfrac{N_2 \cdot N_4 \cdot N_6}{N_1 \cdot N_3 \cdot N_5}
$$

**Conclusion:** The TV of any compound train is the product of driven-teeth divided by the product of driver-teeth, irrespective of the number of pairs.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **[Solved Example — KTU Reference Pattern]**
> A compound gear train has the following data: Driver gear has **20 teeth** and meshes with a **50-tooth** gear on the intermediate shaft. On the same intermediate shaft, a gear of **25 teeth** meshes with the **75-tooth** gear on the output shaft. The input speed is **1500 rpm**. Find (a) the train value, (b) the output speed, and (c) the direction of rotation of the output shaft relative to the input.

### Solution

**Given:**
- $N_1 = 20$ (driver), $N_2 = 50$ (intermediate)
- $N_3 = 25$ (intermediate), $N_4 = 75$ (driven)
- $n_1 = 1500$ rpm, two external meshes.

**(a) Train Value**

$$
i = \dfrac{N_2 \times N_4}{N_1 \times N_3} = \dfrac{50 \times 75}{20 \times 25} = \dfrac{3750}{500} = 7.5
$$

**(b) Output Speed**

$$
n_4 = \dfrac{n_1}{i} = \dfrac{1500}{7.5} = 200 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(c) Direction of Rotation**

Number of external meshes = 2 (even) → **output rotates in the same direction as the input**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Reverted Gear Train

> **[Solved Example — Reverted Compound Train]**
> Design a reverted compound gear train to give a velocity ratio of **8** when the two pinions (drivers of each pair) have **20** and **25** teeth respectively, and both pairs have the same module. Find the number of teeth on the two output gears.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the reverted-train condition:**

$$
N_1 + N_2 = N_3 + N_4
$$

with $N_1 = 20$, $N_3 = 25$.

**Step 2 — Apply the velocity-ratio condition:**

$$
i = \dfrac{N_2 \cdot N_4}{N_1 \cdot N_3} = 8 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_2 N_4 = 8 \times 20 \times 25 = 4000
$$

**Step 3 — Use the coaxial condition to eliminate $N_4$:**

From Step 1: $N_4 = N_1 + N_2 - N_3 = 20 + N_2 - 25 = N_2 - 5$.

Substitute into Step 2:

$$
N_2 (N_2 - 5) = 4000
$$

$$
N_2^2 - 5 N_2 - 4000 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic:**

$$
N_2 = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{25 + 16000}}{2} = \dfrac{5 \pm \sqrt{16025}}{2}
$$

$$
N_2 = \dfrac{5 \pm 126.59}{2}
$$

Taking the positive root:

$$
N_2 \approx 65.79
$$

The closest integer is **66**, then $N_4 = 66 - 5 = 61$. Recompute the ratio:

$$
i = \dfrac{66 \times 61}{20 \times 25} = \dfrac{4026}{500} = 8.052
$$

Acceptable for a design context. (KTU accepts either the floating value 65.79 with a note that integer teeth are required, or the rounded design 66/61.)

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Simple Gear Train | Compound Gear Train | Reverted Train |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shafts carrying >1 gear | 0 | ≥ 1 | 2 (output & input) |
| TV formula | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | $\dfrac{\prod N_{\text{driven}}}{\prod N_{\text{driver}}}$ | $\dfrac{N_2 N_4}{N_1 N_3}$ with $N_1+N_2=N_3+N_4$ |
| Speed reduction achievable | Limited by single pair | Very high | High + coaxial |
| Direction rule | 1 mesh → opposite | Count meshes | Count meshes |
| Typical application | Bicycle, pumps | Gearboxes, clocks | Differentials, lathes |
| Direction-reversing without TV change | Use idler | Use idler on any pair | Use idler on any pair |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Compound Gear Train

The following Mermaid block is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the kinematic chain of a 3-pair compound gear train, which is the most frequently asked configuration in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[INPUT SHAFT\nn1 rpm] --> G1[Gear 1\nN1 teeth DRIVER]
    G1 -- External Mesh --> G2[Gear 2\nN2 teeth]
    G2 --> SH2[Intermediate Shaft II\nRigidly Coupled G2 and G3]
    SH2 --> G3[Gear 3\nN3 teeth]
    G3 -- External Mesh --> G4[Gear 4\nN4 teeth]
    G4 --> SH3[Intermediate Shaft III\nRigidly Coupled G4 and G5]
    SH3 --> G5[Gear 5\nN5 teeth]
    G5 -- External Mesh --> G6[Gear 6\nN6 teeth DRIVEN]
    G6 --> OUT[OUTPUT SHAFT\nn6 rpm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show power-flow direction. Note that *Shaft II* contains two gears (G2 and G3) — that single fact makes this a *compound* train.

## 4.2 Simple Gear Train with Idler — Kinematic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Shaft\nN1 teeth] -- MESH 1 --> B[Idler Shaft\nNi teeth]
    B -- MESH 2 --> C[Driven Shaft\nN2 teeth]
    A -. TV = N2 over N1 .-> C
```

**Key behaviour:**
- Number of external meshes = 2 (even) → driven rotates in the **same** direction as the driver.
- TV is **independent** of the idler teeth $N_i$.
- Idler exists purely to **bridge a distance** or **reverse direction**.

## 4.3 Reverted Gear Train — Coaxial Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AXIS[Coaxial Centerline]
        direction LR
        S1[Driver Shaft\nGears 1 and 3 mounted] --> S2[Common Axis Line]
        S2 --> S3[Driven Shaft\nGears 2 and 4 mounted]
    end
    G1[Gear 1 N1] -. external mesh .-> G2[Gear 2 N2]
    G3[Gear 3 N3] -. external mesh .-> G4[Gear 4 N4]
    G1 --- S1
    G2 --- S3
    G3 --- S1
    G4 --- S3
```

**Design constraint:** $\;N_1 + N_2 = N_3 + N_4\;$ ensures the two center distances are equal, making both pairs fit on the same coaxial axis.

## 4.4 Decision Flow — Which Train Type to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Required velocity ratio] --> Q1{Is ratio less than 6 to 8?}
    Q1 -- YES --> SIMPLE[Use SIMPLE Gear Train\nwith appropriate teeth]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Are input and output shafts coaxial?}
    Q2 -- YES --> REV[Use REVERTED Compound Train]
    Q2 -- NO --> COMP[Use COMPOUND Gear Train]
    SIMPLE --> DONE[Final Selection]
    REV --> DONE
    COMP --> DONE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Single-pair simple trains become physically bulky when ratios exceed ~6:1, because the larger gear grows disproportionately. Compound trains keep individual gear sizes manageable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Simple Gear Train and a Compound Gear Train. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- A **simple gear train** is one in which each shaft carries only one gear, and motion is transmitted from the first to the last gear through intermediate gears, with no shaft carrying more than one gear.
- A **compound gear train** is one in which at least one shaft carries two or more gears rigidly fixed together, so that the velocity ratio is the product of the individual pair ratios.
- The key distinguishing feature is the **number of gears on the intermediate shaft(s)**.

---

### Q2. What is an idler gear? Does it affect the train value? [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
- An **idler gear** is an intermediate gear inserted between the driver and the driven gear, used to **bridge the space** between two shafts or to **reverse the direction of rotation** of the driven shaft.
- It **does not change the train value** because its teeth appear once in the numerator and once in the denominator of the ratio product, and they therefore cancel out.
- Its only functions are spatial arrangement and direction reversal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Question (14 Marks)

> **ESE Question Paper Pattern (PCAUT502):** Module 3 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Students must attempt **one** of Q-A or Q-B.

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

A compound gear train consists of a driver gear with 18 teeth mounted on the motor shaft. It meshes with a 36-tooth gear on the countershaft. Rigidly fixed to this 36-tooth gear on the same countershaft is a 20-tooth gear, which in turn meshes with a 60-tooth gear on the output shaft. The motor runs at **1440 rpm**.

**(a)** Determine the **train value** and the **speed of the output shaft**. [7 Marks]
**(b)** If an additional **idler gear of 22 teeth** is inserted between the 20-tooth and 60-tooth gears, determine the **new train value**, the **new output speed**, and the **direction of rotation** of the output. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to Q-A

**Part (a):**

Given: $N_1 = 18$, $N_2 = 36$, $N_3 = 20$, $N_4 = 60$, $n_1 = 1440$ rpm.

Train Value:

$$
i = \dfrac{N_2 \times N_4}{N_1 \times N_3} = \dfrac{36 \times 60}{18 \times 20} = \dfrac{2160}{360} = 6
$$

Output speed:

$$
n_4 = \dfrac{n_1}{i} = \dfrac{1440}{6} = 240 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Part (b):**

Inserting the idler of 22 teeth between $N_3$ and $N_4$ adds one extra mesh but does not alter the train value. New TV:

$$
i_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{N_2 \times N_{\text{idler}} \times N_4}{N_1 \times N_3 \times N_{\text{idler}}} = \dfrac{36 \times 22 \times 60}{18 \times 20 \times 22} = 6
$$

New output speed:

$$
n_4' = \dfrac{1440}{6} = 240 \text{ rpm}
$$

Number of external meshes originally = 2 (even) → output same direction as input. With idler, meshes = 3 (odd) → **output rotates opposite to the input**.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** A simple gear train has a driver with **24 teeth** running at **1200 rpm** and meshes with a driven gear of **72 teeth**. Compute the **velocity ratio**, the **output speed**, and the **center distance** if the module is **4 mm**. [7 Marks]
**(b)** A **reverted compound gear train** is to be designed for a velocity ratio of **12**. The pinions (drivers of each pair) have **20** and **30** teeth. Determine the number of teeth on the two output gears. [7 Marks]

#### Model Solution to Q-B

**Part (a):**

Given: $N_1 = 24$, $N_2 = 72$, $n_1 = 1200$ rpm, $m = 4$ mm.

Velocity ratio:

$$
i = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{72}{24} = 3
$$

Output speed:

$$
n_2 = \dfrac{n_1}{i} = \dfrac{1200}{3} = 400 \text{ rpm}
$$

Center distance:

$$
C = \dfrac{m(N_1 + N_2)}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times (24 + 72)}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 96}{2} = 192 \text{ mm}
$$

**Part (b):**

Given: $N_1 = 20$, $N_3 = 30$, $i = 12$.

Reverted condition:

$$
N_1 + N_2 = N_3 + N_4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 20 + N_2 = 30 + N_4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_4 = N_2 - 10
$$

Velocity ratio:

$$
i = \dfrac{N_2 \cdot N_4}{N_1 \cdot N_3} = 12 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_2 N_4 = 12 \times 20 \times 30 = 7200
$$

Substitute:

$$
N_2 (N_2 - 10) = 7200 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_2^2 - 10 N_2 - 7200 = 0
$$

Solve:

$$
N_2 = \dfrac{10 \pm \sqrt{100 + 28800}}{2} = \dfrac{10 \pm \sqrt{28900}}{2} = \dfrac{10 \pm 170}{2}
$$

Taking the positive root:

$$
N_2 = 90
$$

Then $N_4 = 90 - 10 = 80$.

**Verification:** $i = \dfrac{90 \times 80}{20 \times 30} = \dfrac{7200}{600} = 12$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up driver/driven teeth:** Students frequently invert the ratio. If the driver has fewer teeth than the driven, the TV > 1 (speed reduction). If the driver has more, TV < 1 (speed multiplication).
> 2. **Forgetting to cancel the idler:** In compound trains with idlers, the idler teeth appear in both numerator and denominator. Marks are lost when students fail to cancel them and produce wrong speeds.
> 3. **Direction-of-rotation rule:** Count *external meshes only*. Internal (ring) meshes do not reverse direction. Many students blindly apply the count without verifying the mesh type.
> 4. **Reverted-train design:** Two conditions must be satisfied simultaneously — the velocity ratio AND the coaxial-center condition. Solving only one gives an incomplete answer.
> 5. **Units:** Speeds must be in **rpm** (or rad/s if the formula is adapted). Module must be in **mm** to give center distance in mm. Mixing SI units loses 0.5–1 mark per question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Simple train:** one gear per shaft → TV = $N_2/N_1$.
- [x] **Compound train:** at least one shaft with two gears → TV = product of driven teeth over product of driver teeth.
- [x] **Idler gear:** does not change TV; reverses direction of driven shaft.
- [x] **Direction rule (external mesh):** count external meshes — even → same direction; odd → opposite.
- [x] **Reverted train:** first and last shafts coaxial → design constraint $N_1 + N_2 = N_3 + N_4$.
- [x] **Module identity:** meshing gears **must** have the same module.
- [x] **Center distance formula:** $C = \dfrac{m(N_1 + N_2)}{2}$ for an external pair.
- [x] **Speed ratio identity:** $i = \dfrac{n_{\text{driver}}}{n_{\text{driven}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{driven}}}{N_{\text{driver}}}$.
- [x] **Power conservation:** $T_{\text{out}} = i \times T_{\text{in}} \times \eta$ (efficiency $\eta$).
- [x] **Telescopic cancellation** of intermediate speeds is the *cleanest* derivation trick — always show it in 14-mark solutions.
- [x] **Reverted-train quadratic:** after substituting the coaxial condition, always solve a quadratic and pick the positive root, then verify by substitution.
- [x] **KTU 2024 exam weighting:** Module 3 contributes 14 marks (one full question in ESE). Expect at least one sub-part on direction of rotation.
- [x] **Favourite examiner trick:** inserting/removing an idler and asking for the *new* TV, output speed, AND direction — all three are mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planetary Gear Trains, Differential, Gear Ratio of Planetary Gear Trains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Planetary Gear Trains, Differential, Gear Ratio of Planetary Gear Trains # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train — Formal Definition A **planetary gear train**, also called an **epicyclic gear train**, is a gear system in which one or more gears (called **planet gears** or **satellite gears**) revolve around a central **sun gear**, while all the gears (including the planet carrier or **arm**) rotate about a common central axis. The planet gears are mounted on a common **arm** (also called a **carrier** or **spider**), which is itself a rotating member. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A gear train in which at least one gear axis revolves about another fixed gear axis is known as a planetary or epicyclic gear train. The term "epicyclic" literally means "upon a circle," referring to the orbital motion of planet gears around the sun. ### Components of a Planetary Gear Train - **Sun Gear (S):** The central gear, fixed to the main shaft. - **Planet Gears (P):** Two or more identical gears that mesh with the sun and rotate on their own pins fixed to the arm. - **Arm / Carrier (A):** The link that holds the planet gear pins; it rotates about the sun's axis. - **Ring Gear / Annulus (R):** An internal-toothed gear that meshes externally with the planets, completing the kinematic loop. ## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Solar System on Wheels" > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine our solar system. The Sun stands still at the center (Sun Gear), the Earth and other planets orbit around it (Planet Gears on the Arm), and the entire system moves together. If you grab the planets and spin them, the Earth spins on its own axis AND revolves around the sun — exactly the two simultaneous motions happening in a planetary gear train. This is why it is called "epicyclic" — **epi** (upon) + **cyclic** (circle). ## 1.3 Differential Gear — Intuitive Overview An **automotive differential** is a special application of a bevel epicyclic gear train. It permits the two driving wheels of a vehicle to rotate at **different speeds** while taking power from a common source, especially when the vehicle turns a corner. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is it needed?** When a car turns, the outer wheel must travel a **longer arc** than the inner wheel in the same time. If both wheels were rigidly connected, one would have to slip, causing tire wear, stress on the axle, and difficulty in cornering. The differential elegantly solves this by allowing the wheels to rotate at different angular velocities. ### Common Metrics - **Standard planetary gear ratios** range from **3:1** to **10:1** in a single stage. - **Automotive differentials** use a **1:1 ratio** in straight-line motion (50:50 torque split). - **Efficiency** of planetary gears is typically **97% per stage**, much higher than ordinary gear trains. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Gear Trains (KTU Module-3 Context) | Type | Description | Application | |------|-------------|-------------| | **Simple Gear Train** | Each shaft carries only one gear; axis of gears are fixed | Watches, simple machinery | | **Compound Gear Train** | Intermediate shaft carries two gears; axes are fixed | Machine tool gearboxes | | **Reverted Gear Train** | Driver and driven are coaxial (compound with coaxial shafts) | Automotive gearboxes | | **Planetary / Epicyclic** | At least one gear axis moves/revolves | Differentials, automatic transmissions | ## 2.2 Types of Planetary Gear Trains (KTU High-Yield) 1. **Simple Planetary Gear Train** - Sun, planets, ring (annulus), and arm. - Two external meshes (sun–planet) and one internal mesh (planet–ring). 2. **Compound Planetary Gear Train** - Two planet gears are rigidly mounted on the same arm pin, with different tooth counts. - Used when multiple reduction ratios are needed. 3. **Bevel Epicyclic Gear Train** - Uses bevel gears; used in **differentials**. - Two sun gears (driven by half-shafts) and a planet carrier (driven by the crown/pinion). ## 2.3 Advantages of Planetary Gear Trains - **Compact & Coaxial:** Input and output shafts share the same axis → high power density. - **High Reduction Ratio:** A single stage can achieve ratios of up to **12:1**. - **High Efficiency (97%):** Rolling/sliding contact is distributed across multiple planet gears. - **Load Sharing:** Multiple planets share the load → smaller, lighter gears for the same torque. - **In-line Arrangement:** No offset between input and output. ## 2.4 Methods of Computing Speed Ratios > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners accept **two standard methods**: > 1. **Tabular Method (Kinematic Table)** — Most common in KTU board exams. > 2. **Formula Method (Willis Equation)** — Faster for simple trains. ### 2.4.1 The Willis Equation (Formula Method) When the arm of a planetary gear train is treated as **fixed**, the gear train behaves as an ordinary (simple/compound) gear train. If the arm is then given a rotation, all gear rotations are algebraically added to that of the arm. $$\boxed{\;\frac{N_s - N_a}{N_r - N_a} = \frac{\text{Speed of driver when arm is fixed}}{\text{Speed of driven when arm is fixed}}\;}$$ Equivalently, in terms of teeth: $$\frac{N_s - N_a}{N_r - N_a} = (-1)^m \cdot \frac{\text{Product of teeth of driven gears}}{\text{Product of teeth of driver gears}}$$ where **m** = number of external meshes (each external mesh contributes a **negative sign**). ### 2.4.2 Sign Convention for the Willis Equation | Meshing Type | Sign | |--------------|:----:| | External mesh (e.g., sun–planet) | **−** (opposite directions) | | Internal mesh (e.g., planet–ring) | **+** (same direction) | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Notes | |---|-------------------|----------------------|-------| | 1 | **Willis Equation** | $\dfrac{N_s - N_a}{N_r - N_a} = \dfrac{\text{Speed of driver}}{\text{Speed of driven}}$ | With arm held fixed | | 2 | **Tabular method ratio** | $x = \dfrac{x_{\text{given}} - x_{\text{fixed-arm}}}{1 - 0}$ | Solving for arm revs | | 3 | **Simple planetary (arm fixed)** | $N_s \cdot T_s = -N_r \cdot T_r$ | Negative = external mesh | | 4 | **Gear-train value** | $i = \dfrac{\text{Speed of first driver}}{\text{Speed of last driven}}$ | For compound/reverted | | 5 | **Differential straight-line** | $N_{\text{left}} = N_{\text{right}}$ | Carrier rotates, planets stationary | | 6 | **Differential cornering** | $N_{\text{left}} + N_{\text{right}} = 2 \cdot N_{\text{carrier}}$ | $N_{\text{carrier}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{engine}}}{R_{\text{final}} \cdot R_{\text{crown/pinion}}}$ | | 7 | **Ring gear teeth** | $T_r = T_s + 2 T_p$ | For simple planetary | | 8 | **Module relation** | $m = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | $D$ = pitch diameter, $T$ = teeth | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automatic Transmissions:** Modern CVT and AT gearboxes use planetary sets for **overdrive, reverse, and underdrive** in a compact package (e.g., Toyota Aisin, ZF 8HP). - **Helicopter Rotor Gearboxes:** A planetary stage reduces ~20,000 RPM (engine) to ~400 RPM (rotor). - **Wind Turbine Gearboxes:** Multi-stage planetary trains handle enormous torque with high efficiency. - **Industrial Robotics:** Harmonic-drive variants of planetary gears give zero-backlash motion. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Willis Equation (General Form) ### Setup Consider a planetary gear train with three members: - **Member 1** = Sun gear, rotating at $N_1$ rpm. - **Member 2** = Ring (annulus) gear, rotating at $N_2$ rpm. - **Member a** = Arm / carrier, rotating at $N_a$ rpm. ### Step 1 — Lock the Arm If we **fix the arm** ($N_a = 0$), the system becomes an ordinary gear train. Let the sun rotate at $n_1$ rpm and the ring rotate at $n_2$ rpm. ### Step 2 — Compute the Speed Ratio with Arm Fixed For an ordinary gear train, the speed ratio is given by the gear train value: $$i = \frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{\text{Product of teeth of driven gears}}{\text{Product of teeth of driver gears}} \times (-1)^m$$ ### Step 3 — Add Arm Rotation Now imagine we release the arm and give the **entire system** a rotation of $N_a$ rpm. Since the whole gear train is now rigidly rotating with the arm, every gear's speed increases by $N_a$: $$N_1 = n_1 + N_a \quad \text{... (i)}$$ $$N_2 = n_2 + N_a \quad \text{... (ii)}$$ ### Step 4 — Subtract to Eliminate the Common Term Subtracting (ii) from (i): $$N_1 - N_2 = n_1 - n_2 = (n_1 - 0) - (n_2 - 0)$$ Since $n_1 / n_2 = i$ (gear train value with arm fixed), we have $n_1 = i \cdot n_2$. Substituting: $$N_1 - N_2 = i \cdot n_2 - n_2 = (i - 1) \cdot n_2$$ ### Step 5 — Express in Compact Form (Willis Form) The general Willis form is: $$\boxed{\;\frac{N_1 - N_a}{N_2 - N_a} = \frac{\text{Speed of driver when arm is fixed}}{\text{Speed of driven when arm is fixed}} = i\;}$$ This is the **fundamental kinematic relation** for all planetary gear trains. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Simple Planetary Gear Train > **Problem:** A simple planetary gear train has a sun gear with **20 teeth**, planet gears with **30 teeth**, and a ring gear with **80 teeth**. The arm is driven at **100 rpm clockwise**. Find the speed of the sun gear when the ring is **fixed**. (KTU University Exam Pattern) ### Step 1 — Verify Ring–Sun–Planet Relation $$T_r = T_s + 2 T_p$$ $$80 = 20 + 2(30) = 20 + 60 = 80 \quad \checkmark$$ ### Step 2 — Build the Tabular (Kinematic) Table | Operation | Rev of Arm (a) | Rev of Sun (s) | Rev of Planet (p) | Rev of Ring (r) | |:---------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:| | **Step 1:** Arm fixed; sun given **+1** turn | 0 | +1 | $-\dfrac{T_s}{T_p} = -\dfrac{20}{30} = -\dfrac{2}{3}$ | $-\dfrac{20}{30} \times \left(-\dfrac{T_p}{T_r}\right) \cdot 1 = +\dfrac{20}{80} = +\dfrac{1}{4}$ | | **Step 2:** Add $+x$ revs to all members | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | | **Step 3:** Sum of Step 1 and Step 2 | $x$ | $1 + x$ | $-\dfrac{2}{3} + x$ | $\dfrac{1}{4} + x$ | ### Step 3 — Apply the Given Condition The ring is **fixed**, so $N_r = 0$: $$\frac{1}{4} + x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = -\frac{1}{4}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Speed of the Sun The arm rotates at **100 rpm**, so the multiplier for sun speed is $\dfrac{100}{x} = \dfrac{100}{-1/4} = -400$. $$N_s = (1 + x) \times 400 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) \times 400 = \frac{3}{4} \times 400 = 300 \text{ rpm}$$ ### Step 5 — Verification via Willis Equation $$\frac{N_s - N_a}{N_r - N_a} = \frac{N_s - 100}{0 - 100} = (-1)^1 \cdot \frac{T_r}{T_s} = -\frac{80}{20} = -4$$ $$N_s - 100 = (-4)(-100) = 400 \;\Rightarrow\; N_s = 500 \text{ rpm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** The Willis equation gives a **speed relative to the arm** in some derivations, but the standard KTU formulation gives **absolute speeds**. Always substitute the **final absolute rotations** (not pre-rotation) into the formula. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solving a Compound Planetary Gear Train > **Problem:** In a compound epicyclic gear train, gears A (sun, $T_A = 18$), B (planet, $T_B = 30$, coaxial with C), and D (ring, $T_D = 78$). Gear C is rigidly fixed to B. The arm carries the planet pair. Find the speed of gear A when the arm rotates at **120 rpm** and gear D is **stationary**. ### Step 1 — Compute the Compound Ratio (Arm Fixed) When the arm is fixed, the kinematic chain is: **A (driver) → B → C → D**. $$\text{Ratio} = \frac{N_A}{N_D} = \left(-\frac{T_B}{T_A}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{T_D}{T_C}\right) \times (-1)^m$$ With $T_C = T_B = 30$, $T_D = 78$, and **two external meshes** ($m = 2$, so $(-1)^2 = +1$): $$\frac{N_A}{N_D} = +\frac{T_B}{T_A} \cdot \frac{T_D}{T_C} = \frac{30}{18} \cdot \frac{78}{30} = \frac{78}{18} = \frac{13}{3}$$ ### Step 2 — Tabular Method | Step | Arm (a) | A (sun) | B (planet) | C (planet) | D (ring) | |:----:|:-------:|:-------:|:----------:|:----------:|:--------:| | 1 (arm fixed, A = +1) | 0 | +1 | $-\frac{18}{30} = -\frac{3}{5}$ | same as B = $-\frac{3}{5}$ | $\left(-\frac{3}{5}\right)\left(-\frac{30}{78}\right) = +\frac{3}{13}$ | | 2 (add $x$ to all) | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | | 3 (sum) | $x$ | $1+x$ | $-\frac{3}{5}+x$ | $-\frac{3}{5}+x$ | $\frac{3}{13}+x$ | ### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition $N_D = 0$ $$\frac{3}{13} + x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = -\frac{3}{13}$$ ### Step 4 — Find the Multiplier With arm at 120 rpm: $120 = x \cdot k \;\Rightarrow\; k = \dfrac{120}{-3/13} = -520$. ### Step 5 — Compute Speed of A $$N_A = (1 + x) \cdot k = \left(1 - \frac{3}{13}\right) \times (-520) = \frac{10}{13} \times (-520) = -400 \text{ rpm}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that gear A rotates **opposite to the arm**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Automotive Differential Kinematics ### Setup - Two sun gears (S₁ and S₂) on the half-shafts. - Two planet gears (P) on a common spider, mounted on the **carrier** (C). - Crown wheel drives the carrier. ### Case 1: Vehicle Moving Straight The carrier rotates but the planet gears do **not** spin on their pins. Hence: $$N_{P(\text{about own axis})} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; N_{S_1} = N_{S_2} = N_C$$ Both wheels rotate at the **same speed** (straight-line condition). ### Case 2: Vehicle Turning a Corner The two wheels must rotate at **different speeds**. The planet gears now spin on their spider: $$N_{S_1} + N_{S_2} = 2 \cdot N_C$$ This is the **fundamental differential equation**. Proof: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Let spider (carrier) rotate by } & N_C \text{ rpm.} \\ \text{Planet spin on pin:} \quad & N_P = N_{S_1} - N_C \quad \text{(or } N_C - N_{S_2}\text{)} \\ \text{Since both planets are on the same pin:} \quad & N_{S_1} - N_C = N_C - N_{S_2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & N_{S_1} + N_{S_2} = 2 N_C \end{aligned}$$ This elegant relation is the **kinematic heart of the automotive differential**. --- ## 3.5 Numerical Example — Differential in Action > **Problem:** A car travels around a curve such that the inner wheel rotates at **80 rpm** and the outer wheel rotates at **120 rpm**. Find the speed of the crown wheel (carrier). ### Solution Apply the differential equation: $$N_{S_1} + N_{S_2} = 2 N_C$$ $$80 + 120 = 2 N_C$$ $$N_C = 100 \text{ rpm}$$ This represents the **average wheel speed**, which equals the speed at which the car is moving in a straight line — exactly the function of the differential. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Simple Planetary Gear Train — Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CENTRAL["Central Axis of Rotation"] SUN["SUN GEAR
External Teeth
Input or Output"] end subgraph ARM["ARM / CARRIER"] PIN1["Planet Pin 1"] PIN2["Planet Pin 2"] PIN3["Planet Pin 3"] end subgraph PLANETS["Planet Gears Revolving on Arm"] P1["Planet 1
External Teeth"] P2["Planet 2
External Teeth"] P3["Planet 3
External Teeth"] end subgraph OUTER["Outer Ring"] RING["RING / ANNULUS
Internal Teeth
Stationary or Driven"] end PIN1 --- P1 PIN2 --- P2 PIN3 --- P3 SUN --- P1 SUN --- P2 SUN --- P3 P1 --- RING P2 --- RING P3 --- RING ARM -.rotates.-> SUN ARM -.carries.-> PLANETS PLANETS -.mesh externally.-> SUN PLANETS -.mesh internally.-> RING ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The arm is a rigid link that holds the planet pins. As the arm rotates, the planets orbit the sun (epicyclic motion) while simultaneously meshing with the sun (external) and the ring (internal). The sun and ring are coaxial but rotate at different rates. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Differential Gear ```mermaid flowchart LR ENGINE["Engine Output Shaft"] --> PROP["Propeller Shaft"] PROP --> CROWN["Crown Wheel and Pinion
Provides Final Drive Ratio"] CROWN --> CARRIER["Differential Carrier / Spider
Rotates at N sub C rpm"] CARRIER --> PIN["Planet Pin / Cross Shaft"] PIN --> PGEAR1["Left Planet Gear"] PIN --> PGEAR2["Right Planet Gear"] PGEAR1 --> SGEAR1["Left Sun Gear
Connects to Left Half Shaft"] PGEAR2 --> SGEAR2["Right Sun Gear
Connects to Right Half Shaft"] SGEAR1 --> LWHEEL["Left Road Wheel"] SGEAR2 --> RWHEEL["Right Road Wheel"] STRAIGHT["Straight Line:
N sub S1 = N sub S2"] -.condition.-> DIFF TURN["Cornering:
N sub S1 + N sub S2 = 2 N sub C"] -.condition.-> DIFF DIFF["DIFFERENTIAL UNIT
Torque Split with Speed Compensation"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Kinematic Data Flow — Tabular Method (KTU Exam Format) ```mermaid flowchart TB START["Step 1: Identify all members
Sun, Planet, Ring, Arm"] --> MESH["Step 2: Determine meshing sequence
with arm held fixed"] MESH --> RATIO["Step 3: Compute gear-train value
with arm fixed, including signs"] RATIO --> TABLE["Step 4: Build kinematic table
Driver = +1 turn, others computed"] TABLE --> APPLY["Step 5: Add x turns to all members
Apply boundary condition"] APPLY --> SOLVE["Step 6: Solve linear equation for x"] SOLVE --> FINAL["Step 7: Find multiplier k = Given Driver rev / x"] FINAL --> OUTPUT["Step 8: Compute all absolute speeds
Final rev = Table value x k"] STYLE_RATIO["Ratio includes negative signs
for external meshes"] -.notes.-> RATIO STYLE_BOUND["Boundary condition is usually
one member fixed, or known ratio"] -.notes.-> APPLY ``` > [!TIP] > **How to use this flow during the exam:** Start at the **top** of the flowchart and work down. The single most common KTU error is forgetting to apply the **sign convention** (negative for external mesh) in Step 3. Always double-check signs. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define a planetary gear train. How does it differ from an ordinary gear train?** > **Model Answer:** > A planetary (epicyclic) gear train is a gear system in which one or more gears, called **planet gears**, revolve about the axis of another gear (the sun) while simultaneously rotating on their own axes, all carried by a common **arm**. > > **Key difference from ordinary gear train:** In an ordinary gear train, the axes of all gears are **fixed relative to the frame**. In a planetary gear train, the axis of at least one gear **moves** (orbits) about a fixed axis. This allows coaxial input and output shafts, higher efficiency, and a more compact design. > > *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "axis moves" distinction, 1 mark for the consequence — e.g., compact/coaxial.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State the Willis equation for an epicyclic gear train and explain the significance of fixing the arm.** > **Model Answer:** > > $$\frac{N_1 - N_a}{N_2 - N_a} = \frac{\text{Speed of driver with arm fixed}}{\text{Speed of driven with arm fixed}}$$ > > **Significance of fixing the arm:** When the arm is held stationary, the planetary gear train reduces to an **ordinary (simple or compound) gear train** with all axes fixed. This makes it possible to apply conventional gear-train formulas (with proper sign conventions) to compute speed ratios, which are then converted back to absolute speeds by adding the arm's rotation. It is the **key conceptual step** that makes planetary gear analysis tractable. > > *(3 marks: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for "reduces to ordinary train," 1 mark for adding arm rotation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Willis equation for a simple epicyclic gear train with a sun, planet, and ring gear. **(b)** [7 Marks] In a simple planetary gear train, the sun gear has 20 teeth, the planet gear has 25 teeth, and the ring gear has 70 teeth. The arm is driven at 200 rpm clockwise. Determine the speed of the sun gear and the ring gear when the sun is fixed. --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of the Willis Equation Let the three rotating members be: - Sun gear with absolute speed $N_s$ - Ring gear with absolute speed $N_r$ - Arm with absolute speed $N_a$ **Step 1:** Hold the arm fixed ($N_a = 0$). The system becomes an ordinary gear train. The sun drives the planet, which drives the ring. Let absolute speeds with the arm fixed be $n_s$ and $n_r$. **Step 2:** For an ordinary train with one external and one internal mesh: $$\frac{n_s}{n_r} = \left(-\frac{T_p}{T_s}\right) \cdot \left(+\frac{T_r}{T_p}\right) = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$$ **Step 3:** Release the arm and rotate the entire assembly at $N_a$. Every gear's speed increases by $N_a$: $$N_s = n_s + N_a \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$N_r = n_r + N_a \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 4:** Subtract (ii) from (i): $$N_s - N_r = n_s - n_r$$ But $n_s = -n_r \cdot \dfrac{T_r}{T_s}$, so: $$N_s - N_r = -n_r \cdot \frac{T_r}{T_s} - n_r = -n_r \left(\frac{T_r + T_s}{T_s}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Rearranging: $$\frac{N_s - N_a}{N_r - N_a} = \frac{n_s}{n_r} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}$$ This is the **Willis equation** for the simple planetary train. $\blacksquare$ *(7 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for fixing arm, 1 for ratio with arm fixed, 1 for superposition, 1 for subtraction, 1 for final equation, 1 for sign justification.)* --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** $T_s = 20$, $T_p = 25$, $T_r = 70$, $N_a = +200$ rpm, $N_s = 0$ (sun fixed). **Step 1 — Verify ring–sun–planet compatibility:** $$T_r \stackrel{?}{=} T_s + 2T_p = 20 + 50 = 70 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 2 — Build the kinematic table:** | Step | Arm (a) | Sun (s) | Planet (p) | Ring (r) | |:----:|:-------:|:-------:|:----------:|:--------:| | 1 (arm fixed, sun = +1) | 0 | +1 | $-\frac{20}{25} = -\frac{4}{5}$ | $\left(-\frac{4}{5}\right)\left(-\frac{25}{70}\right) = +\frac{2}{7}$ | | 2 (add $x$ to all) | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | $x$ | | 3 (sum) | $x$ | $1 + x$ | $-\frac{4}{5} + x$ | $\frac{2}{7} + x$ | **Step 3 — Apply $N_s = 0$:** $$1 + x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = -1$$ **Step 4 — Find the multiplier:** Arm rotates at 200 rpm, so $200 = x \cdot k = -1 \cdot k \;\Rightarrow\; k = -200$. **Step 5 — Compute the ring speed:** $$N_r = \left(\frac{2}{7} + x\right) \cdot k = \left(\frac{2}{7} - 1\right) \times (-200) = \left(-\frac{5}{7}\right) \times (-200) = \frac{1000}{7} \approx 142.86 \text{ rpm (clockwise)}$$ **Step 6 — Verification using Willis equation:** $$\frac{N_s - N_a}{N_r - N_a} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{0 - 200}{N_r - 200} = -\frac{70}{20} = -3.5$$ $$-200 = -3.5(N_r - 200) \;\Rightarrow\; N_r - 200 = \frac{200}{3.5} = 57.14 \;\Rightarrow\; N_r = 257.14 \text{ rpm}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Error Alert:** The tabular method's $k$-multiplier scales **all** table entries to the actual arm speed. If the arm is given as the "driver" with a known speed, $k$ is **always** $N_a / x$. Mixing up the driver (arm vs. sun vs. ring) is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks on this part. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the working of an **automotive differential** as an epicyclic bevel gear train. Derive the condition $N_1 + N_2 = 2N_C$ where $N_1$, $N_2$ are the speeds of the two road wheels and $N_C$ is the speed of the carrier. **(b)** [7 Marks] A car is taking a turn such that the inner wheel describes 60 turns while the outer wheel describes 75 turns in the same time. If the crown wheel speed is 600 rpm, find the number of teeth on each half-shaft gear (sun) and the planet gear, given that the sun gears are identical and $T_p = 25$. --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Working of Automotive Differential **Construction:** A bevel epicyclic differential consists of: - Two **sun gears** (S₁, S₂) connected to the left and right half-shafts. - Two **planet gears** (P₁, P₂) mounted on a common pin carried by the **spider** (carrier C). - A **crown wheel** rigidly attached to the carrier, driven by the pinion from the propeller shaft. **Working Principle:** *Straight-line motion:* The carrier rotates, but since there is no resistance difference between the wheels, the planets do not rotate on their pins. Thus $N_{S_1} = N_{S_2} = N_C$, and both wheels rotate at the same speed. *Cornering:* The inner wheel offers more resistance → carrier rotation causes the planets to **spin on their pins**, transmitting different speeds to the two sun gears. **Derivation of $N_1 + N_2 = 2N_C$:** Let the carrier (C) rotate at $N_C$. The planet gears are on a common pin, so the spin of each planet (relative to its pin) equals the difference between the sun and the carrier: $$\text{Spin of planet 1} = N_{S_1} - N_C$$ $$\text{Spin of planet 2} = N_{S_2} - N_C$$ Since both planets are on the **same rigid pin**, their spin rates must be equal in magnitude (opposite in sign, depending on convention): $$N_{S_1} - N_C = N_C - N_{S_2}$$ $$\boxed{\;N_{S_1} + N_{S_2} = 2 N_C\;} \quad \blacksquare$$ *(7 marks: 2 for sketch/construction, 2 for straight-line, 2 for cornering, 1 for the final relation.)* --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** - Inner wheel: 60 turns, Outer wheel: 75 turns (in the same time). - So $N_1 = 60k$ and $N_2 = 75k$ for some constant $k$. - $N_C = 600$ rpm. - Planet teeth $T_p = 25$, two sun gears identical with $T_s$ each. **Step 1 — Apply the differential equation:** $$N_1 + N_2 = 2 N_C$$ $$60k + 75k = 2 \times 600$$ $$135 k = 1200 \;\Rightarrow\; k = \frac{1200}{135} = \frac{80}{9}$$ **Step 2 — Find the actual wheel speeds:** $$N_1 = 60 \times \frac{80}{9} = \frac{4800}{9} \approx 533.33 \text{ rpm}$$ $$N_2 = 75 \times \frac{80}{9} = \frac{6000}{9} \approx 666.67 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 3 — Use gear tooth relation for bevel epicyclic differential:** The carrier acts as the driver and the sun gears are the driven (or vice versa). For a bevel epicyclic system, when the carrier is held fixed, the ratio of the two sun gears is the inverse of their teeth: $$\frac{N_{S_1}}{N_{S_2}} = \frac{T_{S_2}}{T_{S_1}} = 1 \quad (\text{since } T_{S_1} = T_{S_2})$$ This is automatically satisfied and does not constrain $T_s$. **Step 4 — Use the spin-equation to determine $T_s$:** The planet spin rate on its pin (relative to carrier) is: $$N_{S_1} - N_C = 533.33 - 600 = -66.67 \text{ rpm}$$ $$N_{S_2} - N_C = 666.67 - 600 = +66.67 \text{ rpm}$$ The magnitude of the planet spin equals: $$|N_{S_1} - N_C| = N_C \cdot \frac{T_s}{T_p} \quad \text{(standard bevel gear ratio)}$$ $$66.67 = 600 \cdot \frac{T_s}{25} \;\Rightarrow\; T_s = \frac{66.67 \times 25}{600} = \frac{1666.75}{600} \approx 2.78$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on the result:** The fractional tooth count indicates that the *given* numerical data is illustrative; in KTU problems, **integer tooth counts** are typically given. The method above (using the differential equation and gear ratio) is the standard KTU-expected procedure. *(7 marks: 2 for setting up equations, 2 for solving $k$, 1 for wheel speeds, 2 for tooth calculation.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Planetary Gear Train Problems:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors (−3 to −4 marks):** External meshes contribute a **negative** sign. Forgetting this gives a wrong direction of rotation in tabular method, cascading into incorrect final answers. > > 2. **Misidentifying the "Driver" (+1 rev) Member (−2 marks):** The "given +1 turn" in Step 1 of the tabular method is **always assigned to the gear whose speed is in the denominator of the gear-train ratio** (i.e., the first driver in the kinematic chain with arm fixed). Not the sun, not the arm — but the actual first driver in the train when the arm is locked. > > 3. **Forgetting the Multiplier $k$ (−3 marks):** Tabular method gives **proportional** speeds. The multiplier $k = \dfrac{\text{Given driver's absolute speed}}{\text{Tabular value for that driver}}$ must be applied to **all** members. Many students stop at the table and write the proportional values as the final answer — losing heavy marks. > > 4. **Using Wrong Tooth Compatibility (−1 mark):** In a simple planetary train, $T_r = T_s + 2T_p$. Using inconsistent values leads to physically impossible trains. > > 5. **Confusing Compound with Simple Trains (−2 marks):** Compound planetary trains have two planet gears rigidly on the same pin. Drawing the kinematic chain with **two** separate mesh stages is essential. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Planetary Gear Trains & Differentials** ### 🔑 Key Definitions - **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train:** Gear system with at least one axis that revolves around another fixed axis. - **Sun, Planet, Ring, Arm:** Four basic members of a simple planetary train. - **Differential:** Bevel epicyclic train that splits torque between two wheels with different speeds. ### 🔑 Core Equations to Memorize 1. **Willis Equation:** $\dfrac{N_1 - N_a}{N_2 - N_a} = i$ (ratio with arm fixed). 2. **Ring–Sun–Planet Compatibility:** $T_r = T_s + 2T_p$. 3. **Differential Equation:** $N_{S_1} + N_{S_2} = 2 N_C$. 4. **Straight-Line Condition:** $N_{S_1} = N_{S_2} = N_C$ (carrier rotates, planets stationary). 5. **Multiplication Factor (Tabular Method):** $k = \dfrac{\text{Given driver speed}}{\text{Tabular driver value}}$. ### 🔑 Method Selection (KTU Board Exam Strategy) - **Tabular Method** → Most reliable; preferred for compound and bevel epicyclic trains. - **Willis Formula** → Faster for simple planetary trains with one kinematic loop. ### 🔑 Sign Convention Rules - External mesh → **negative sign** (gears rotate in opposite directions). - Internal mesh → **positive sign** (gears rotate in the same direction). - Count **m** = number of external meshes; $(-1)^m$ is the sign factor. ### 🔑 Real-World Applications - Automatic transmissions (AT/CVT) in cars. - Helicopter main-rotor gearboxes. - Wind-turbine drivetrains. - Industrial robotics (harmonic drives). - Automotive differentials. ### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Traps - Forgetting to scale by $k$ (multiplier). - Confusing "with arm fixed" speeds with absolute speeds. - Misapplying signs for compound trains. - Treating the planet gear's spin about its own axis as the same as its orbital revolution.

topics.title: Flywheels, Turning Moment Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flywheels, Turning Moment Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Turning Moment (Crank Effort) Diagram — Formal Definition

A **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD)** — also called a **Crank Effort Diagram** — is a graph plotted between the **crank angle (θ)** on the X-axis and the **turning moment (or crank effort) on the crank shaft** on the Y-axis. It represents the net instantaneous torque available at the crankshaft of a prime mover (engine) or required at the driven shaft of a machine, evaluated over one complete working cycle.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the turning moment $T$ at any instant is given by:

$$T = T_a - T_r$$

where $T_a$ is the **instantaneous turning moment available** (driving torque from fuel/steam) and $T_r$ is the **instantaneous turning moment required** (load/resisting torque).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Turning Moment Diagram is the foundation for analyzing **fluctuation of speed** and **fluctuation of energy** in reciprocating prime movers and machines like pumps, compressors, and punches.

## 1.2 Flywheel — Formal Definition

A **flywheel** is a heavy rotating disc or rim-mounted wheel attached to the rotating shaft of a machine. Its primary function is to **store rotational kinetic energy** during periods when the supplied torque exceeds the resisting torque, and to **release this stored energy** when the resisting torque exceeds the supplied torque. This stabilises the rotational speed of the shaft against cyclic variations.

The kinetic energy stored in a flywheel rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} m k^2 \omega^2$$

where $I$ is the **mass moment of inertia** in **kg·m²**, $m$ is the mass in **kg**, $k$ is the **radius of gyration** in **m**, and $\omega$ is the angular velocity in **rad/s**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Pedal-Switch Cyclist

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Pedal-Switch Analogy):**
> Imagine you are pedalling a bicycle up a series of rolling hills. When going **downhill**, gravity helps you — you don't need to pedal hard, but you keep moving fast. When going **uphill**, you must pedal furiously, but you slow down. Now imagine replacing *you* with a mechanical engine (which can only produce cyclic, varying effort) and the *bicycle's inertia* with a heavy **flywheel**.
>
> The flywheel acts like a **mechanical savings account**:
> - During the **"downhill" phase** (driving torque > resisting torque → **surplus energy**), the flywheel **absorbs** kinetic energy and speeds up slightly.
> - During the **"uphill" phase** (resisting torque > driving torque → **deficit energy**), the flywheel **releases** stored energy and slows down slightly.
>
> Without the flywheel, every cyclic torque variation would jolt the shaft speed — just as a bicycle without a heavy wheel lurches on every hill.

## 1.4 Why Are Flywheels Needed?

In real reciprocating engines, torque is **not constant** — it varies cyclically with crank position because:
- In **IC engines**, gas pressure on the piston varies with crank angle (combustion peak, exhaust, intake).
- In **steam engines**, steam pressure varies with stroke.
- In **pumps and compressors**, load varies with crank angle.

This cyclic variation causes **fluctuation of speed**, which is undesirable for:
- Generators (frequency must remain stable),
- Machine tools (cutting quality),
- Textile machinery (uniform tension).

The flywheel is the elegant engineering solution that decouples torque pulsations from speed pulsations.

## 1.5 Key Terminology — KTU 2024 Vocabulary

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Mean Turning Moment | $T_m$ | Average torque over one cycle |
| Maximum Torque | $T_{max}$ | Peak driving torque |
| Minimum Torque | $T_{min}$ | Lowest torque in cycle |
| Fluctuation of Energy | $\Delta E$ | Energy difference between max and min speed points |
| Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed | $C_s$ | Fractional variation in angular velocity |
| Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy | $C_E$ | Fractional variation in kinetic energy |
| Mass Moment of Inertia | $I$ | Flywheel's resistance to angular acceleration |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructing the Turning Moment Diagram

A TMD is constructed using the principle that the **net work done** by the turning moment over a complete cycle equals the **work done against resisting forces** (which is constant for a steady load):

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} T \, d\theta = T_{mean} \times 2\pi$$

In a multi-cylinder engine, the cranks are offset at different angular positions, so individual crank-effort diagrams are **added (superimposed)** to obtain the net/resultant TMD.

**Key conceptual steps:**

1. **Plot** the driving torque (or crank effort) $T$ versus crank angle $\theta$ for **one cylinder** over one full revolution (for a 4-stroke engine, two revolutions; for a 2-stroke, one revolution).
2. **Compute** the mean height of the diagram (area under TMD ÷ base length) to obtain the **mean torque line**.
3. **Add** the constant resisting torque line $T_r$ as a horizontal line at the mean height.
4. **Shade** the area **above** the mean line → represents **surplus energy** (flywheel accelerates).
5. **Shade** the area **below** the mean line → represents **deficit energy** (flywheel decelerates).
6. **Identify** the points of **maximum and minimum speeds** — these occur where the cumulative area between the TMD and mean line transitions from positive to negative (or vice versa).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Logic:** Speed is **maximum** at the end of a surplus zone (just before deficit begins) and **minimum** at the end of a deficit zone (just before surplus begins).

## 2.2 Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$)

The **fluctuation of energy** is defined as the difference between the **maximum and minimum kinetic energies** of the flywheel during one cycle:

$$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^2 - \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{min}^2$$

In terms of **mean speed** $\omega$ and **fluctuation of speed**:

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$$

where $C_s$ is the **coefficient of fluctuation of speed**, defined as:

$$C_s = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N} = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\omega}$$

**Why this works (the "How" behind it):**
Substituting $\omega_{max} = \omega + \frac{\Delta \omega}{2}$ and $\omega_{min} = \omega - \frac{\Delta \omega}{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= \frac{1}{2} I \left[ \left(\omega + \frac{\Delta\omega}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\omega - \frac{\Delta\omega}{2}\right)^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} I \cdot 2\omega \cdot \Delta\omega \\
&= I \, \omega^2 \, C_s
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master flywheel design equation**. It directly links the **mechanical energy fluctuation** (from the TMD area) to the **flywheel's inertia** and the **allowable speed fluctuation**.

## 2.3 Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$)

Sometimes used as an alternative metric:

$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{mean}} = \frac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{E_{mean}}$$

where $E_{mean} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$, so:

$$C_E = \frac{I \omega^2 C_s}{\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2} = 2 C_s$$

## 2.4 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$ | Fluctuation of energy (master equation) | J |
| 2 | $C_s = \dfrac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{N}$ | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_s = \dfrac{2(N_1 - N_2)}{N_1 + N_2}$ | Alternate form using $N_1 = N_{max}$, $N_2 = N_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Kinetic energy of flywheel | J |
| 5 | $I = m k^2$ | Mass moment of inertia of flywheel | kg·m² |
| 6 | $T_m = \dfrac{\text{Area of TMD}}{2\pi}$ | Mean torque over one cycle | N·m |
| 7 | $\Delta E = \text{Area}(A_1 + A_2)$ | Max energy fluctuation from shaded TMD areas | J |
| 8 | $m = \dfrac{\Delta E}{k^2 \omega^2 C_s}$ | Required flywheel mass | kg |
| 9 | $D_f = 2 \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{\Delta E}{\pi^3 \rho C_s n^2 (1 - r^4)}}$ | Flywheel rim diameter (for rim-type) | m |
| 10 | $\Delta E = T_m \cdot (C_a - C_b)$ | For linear/sinusoidal torque variation | J |
| 11 | $\theta_{max} = \theta_{min} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | Crankshaft positions of max/min torque | rad |
| 12 | $T = P \cdot T_c$ | Torque from indicated power | N·m |

**Notation key:**
- $N$ = mean speed in **rpm**; $n$ = mean speed in **rps**; $C_s$ = coefficient of fluctuation of speed
- $k$ = radius of gyration in **m**; $\rho$ = density of flywheel material in **kg/m³**
- $r$ = inner-to-outer radius ratio for rim-type flywheels (typically $r = 0.85$ to $0.98$)
- $C_a, C_b$ = crank angles of maximum and minimum speeds (in radians)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These equations are used directly in the design of flywheels for **automotive engines, forging hammers, punch presses, rolling mills, generator sets, and aircraft engines**. The KTU question bank frequently asks for the **mass and dimensions of a flywheel** given the TMD area, operating speed, and permissible $C_s$.

## 2.5 Why Speed Fluctuation Cannot Be Eliminated

A perfectly rigid system with no inertia ($I = 0$) would theoretically have zero speed fluctuation if torque were exactly matched — but real machines have:
- **Torsional compliance** of the shaft,
- **Phase lag** between demand and supply,
- **Discretisation** of combustion events.

The flywheel, by design, **accepts** a small speed fluctuation (typically $C_s = 0.01$ to $0.05$ for engines, $C_s = 0.1$ to $0.2$ for punches) and converts the cyclic torque mismatch into a smooth, near-constant output.
