## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Centrifugal force of a rotating mass | $F = m\,r\,\omega^2$ | $F$ [N], $m$ [kg], $r$ [m], $\omega$ [rad/s] | Fundamental; appears in all balancing problems |
| 2 | Static balance — X component | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\alpha_i = 0$ | $m_i$ [kg], $r_i$ [m] | Direction taken as reference |
| 3 | Static balance — Y component | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\alpha_i = 0$ | Same | Perpendicular to X |
| 4 | Required balance force (magnitude) | $m_b r_b = \sqrt{(\sum m_i r_i \cos\alpha_i)^2 + (\sum m_i r_i \sin\alpha_i)^2}$ | $m_b r_b$ [kg·m] | Vector sum of unbalanced force |
| 5 | Balance angle | $\tan\alpha_b = \dfrac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\alpha_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\alpha_i}$ | $\alpha_b$ [rad or °] | Position of balancer |
| 6 | Dynamic condition (multi-plane) | $\sum m_i r_i z_i = 0$ (vector form) | $z_i$ [m] — axial distance from reference | "Couple = 0" condition |
| 7 | Couple due to masses in any plane | $C = \left(\sum m_i r_i z_i \cos\alpha_i\right) \omega^2 \hat{i} + \left(\sum m_i r_i z_i \sin\alpha_i\right) \omega^2 \hat{j}$ | $C$ [N·m] | Vector couple |
| 8 | Primary inertia force (reciprocating) | $F_p = m\,r\,\omega^2 \cos\theta$ | $F_p$ [N] | One-per-revolution force |
| 9 | Secondary inertia force | $F_s = m\,r\,\frac{\omega^2}{n^2}\cos 2\theta$ | $F_s$ [N]; $n = L/r$ (cylinder ratio) | Twice-per-revolution force |
| 10 | Hammer blow in locomotive | $F_{HB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(2m\,r\,\omega^2 - F_{net,\,b}\right)$ | $F_{HB}$ [N]; $F_{net,b}$ [N] net vertical force on bearings | Tractive effort disturbance |
| 11 | Swaying couple (locomotive) | $T_{sway} = \frac{1}{2}\,F_{net,\,b}\,(a)$ | $T_{sway}$ [N·m]; $a$ [m] wheelbase | Tends to sway engine sideways |
| 12 | Gyroscopic couple — general | $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p \sin\theta$ | $C$ [N·m], $I$ [kg·m²], $\omega$ [rad/s] spin, $\omega_p$ [rad/s] precession, $\theta$ [°] between axes | KTU's "Golden Equation" for Module 4 |
| 13 | Gyroscopic couple — perpendicular axes | $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$ | Same | When $\theta = 90°$ (most common case) |
| 14 | Precession angular velocity | $\omega_p = \dfrac{C}{I\,\omega}$ | $\omega_p$ [rad/s] | Rearranged form of Eq. 13 |
| 15 | Angular momentum of disc | $H = I\,\omega$ | $H$ [kg·m²/s] | Vector along spin axis |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Turbogenerators and Turbochargers:** Operate at 10,000–30,000 RPM; an unbalanced rotor can self-destruct within seconds. Multi-plane dynamic balancing is a mandatory ISO 1940-1 quality standard.
- **Aircraft Jet Engines:** Each fan/compressor stage is individually balanced, and the assembled engine is re-balanced to G2.5 (2.5 mm/s vibration limit).
- **Automobile Wheels:** Dynamic balancing on spinning machines places lead/tin stick-on weights on the rim's flanges.
- **Ship Stabilisers:** Gyroscopic principles using actively-spun rotors counteract wave-induced roll.
- **Aerospace Gyros:** Rate gyros in flight control systems and ring-laser gyros in inertial navigation units rely on gyroscopic rigidity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Force Couple in Multi-Plane Balancing

**Setup:** Consider four masses $m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4$ rotating in four different planes (Planes I, II, III, IV) of a rotor at radii $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ and angular positions $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$ measured from a reference direction.

**Step 1 — Centrifugal force on each mass:**
Each rotating mass experiences a centrifugal force directed radially outward:
$$F_i = m_i r_i \omega^2$$

where $\omega$ is the constant angular velocity of the shaft.

**Step 2 — Resolve forces into two reference planes (say Plane A and Plane B):**
The axial distance of mass $m_i$ from the reference Plane A is $z_i$. To simplify multi-plane balancing, we replace all forces with **equivalent forces concentrated at two end planes** A and B using the principle of moments.

The fraction of force from plane $i$ transferred to Plane A:
$$F_{iA} = F_i \cdot \frac{(L - z_i)}{L}$$

The fraction transferred to Plane B:
$$F_{iB} = F_i \cdot \frac{z_i}{L}$$

where $L$ is the axial distance between the two reference planes.

> **Why this works:** Because in statics, replacing a force by an equivalent force-couple system, and then transporting the couple to a new location as a pair of forces, preserves both the net force and the net moment.

**Step 3 — Vector sum of forces in Plane A (X and Y components):**
$$\sum F_{A,x} = \sum_{i} F_i \cdot \frac{L - z_i}{L} \cos\alpha_i = 0$$

$$\sum F_{A,y} = \sum_{i} F_i \cdot \frac{L - z_i}{L} \sin\alpha_i = 0$$

**Step 4 — Vector sum of forces in Plane B:**
$$\sum F_{B,x} = \sum_{i} F_i \cdot \frac{z_i}{L} \cos\alpha_i = 0$$

$$\sum F_{B,y} = \sum_{i} F_i \cdot \frac{z_i}{L} \sin\alpha_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — The required balance masses:**
Two balance masses $m_A$ (in Plane A) and $m_B$ (in Plane B) are introduced. They must satisfy:

$$m_A\,r_A = \sqrt{\left[\sum_{i} m_i r_i \cdot \frac{L - z_i}{L} \cos\alpha_i\right]^2 + \left[\sum_{i} m_i r_i \cdot \frac{L - z_i}{L} \sin\alpha_i\right]^2}$$

$$m_B\,r_B = \sqrt{\left[\sum_{i} m_i r_i \cdot \frac{z_i}{L} \cos\alpha_i\right]^2 + \left[\sum_{i} m_i r_i \cdot \frac{z_i}{L} \sin\alpha_i\right]^2}$$

with corresponding angles computed by the tangent relation. **This is the canonical KTU 14-mark derivation.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gyroscopic Couple (Angular Momentum Method)

**Step 1 — Spin angular momentum of the disc:**
For a disc of mass moment of inertia $I$ spinning at $\omega$ rad/s about its own axis:
$$H = I\,\omega$$

This is a **vector** along the spin axis, by the right-hand rule.

**Step 2 — Apply an external couple to tilt the spin axis:**
Suppose an external torque $\vec{T}$ is applied about an axis (say the X-axis) perpendicular to the spin axis (Z-axis). The disc resists this tilt. Over a small time $\Delta t$, the change in angular momentum must equal the impulse:
$$\vec{T}\,\Delta t = \Delta \vec{H}$$

**Step 3 — Geometry of precession:**
If the spin axis precesses (rotates) about the Y-axis at angular velocity $\omega_p$, the tip of $\vec{H}$ traces a circle of radius $H\sin\theta$ in time $T_p = 2\pi/\omega_p$.

The rate of change of $\vec{H}$ is:
$$\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of precessional rate:**
For perpendicular axes ($\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$):
$$\left\vert \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} \right\vert = \omega_p \cdot H = \omega_p \cdot I\,\omega$$

**Step 5 — Equate to applied couple:**
By Newton's second law for rotation:
$$C = \frac{dH}{dt} = I\,\omega_p\,\omega$$

In general, for any angle $\theta$ between $\vec{H}$ and $\vec{\omega_p}$:
$$\boxed{C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p\,\sin\theta}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The gyroscopic couple depends **linearly** on both spin speed and precession rate. Halving either one halves the reactive couple — this is why gyros spin at very high speeds (so even a tiny precession can produce a measurable couple for sensing).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Primary and Secondary Forces in Reciprocating Masses

**Assumed velocity/acceleration approximation** (used in KTU problems):
- Crank rotates at $\omega$ rad/s, crank radius $r$, connecting rod length $L$, with $n = L/r$.
- Piston displacement from TDC: $x = r\cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{2n}\cos 2\theta$ (truncated, neglecting higher orders).
- Piston velocity: $v = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega}{n}\sin 2\theta$.
- Piston acceleration: $a = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{r^2\omega^2}{n}\cos 2\theta$.

**Inertia force on piston** of mass $m$:
$$F_{inertia} = m\,a = -m\,r\,\omega^2\cos\theta - m\,\frac{r^2\,\omega^2}{n}\cos 2\theta$$

$$\boxed{F_{inertia} = \underbrace{m\,r\,\omega^2\cos\theta}_{\text{Primary force}} + \underbrace{m\,r\,\frac{\omega^2}{n}\cos 2\theta}_{\text{Secondary force}}}$$

**Magnitudes:**
- **Primary force (1× engine speed):** $F_p = m\,r\,\omega^2$
- **Secondary force (2× engine speed):** $F_s = m\,r\,\dfrac{\omega^2}{n}$ (since $r^2/n = r/n \cdot r = (r/n)\,r$, written compactly as $F_s = (m\,r\,\omega^2)/n$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The primary force exists even in a perfectly-balanced reciprocating mass; only multi-cylinder arrangements with phase-shifted cranks can cancel it. The secondary force, though smaller (factor of $1/n \approx 1/3.5$), vibrates the engine at a frequency that resonates with exhaust and intake systems, producing the characteristic "engine note".

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Number of Cylinders on Balance

| No. of Cylinders | Crank Arrangement | Primary Force | Primary Couple | Secondary Force | Secondary Couple |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Single | Unbalanced | — | Unbalanced | — |
| 2 | 180° (flat twin) | Balanced | Unbalanced | Balanced | Unbalanced |
| 4 | 4 cylinders, cross-plane (90° apart, 1&4 + 2&3 pairs) | Balanced | Balanced | Balanced | Balanced (in-line 4 if firing order chosen well) |
| 6 | 120° apart | Balanced | Balanced | Balanced | Balanced |
| 8 | V8 (90° V) | Balanced | Balanced | Balanced | Balanced |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The phrase "perfectly balanced engine" is **only true for even-fire, symmetrically-arranged multi-cylinder engines with a firing interval of 90° (4-cyl) or 60° (6-cyl)**. Students who answer "yes" to "Is a 4-cylinder engine perfectly balanced?" lose 2 marks for ignoring the secondary couple in 4-inline configurations under specific crank arrangements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Plane Balancing Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the workflow of a typical multi-plane balancing problem (the most common 14-mark question in KTU ESE for this module).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Masses m1 m2 m3 m4 at planes I II III IV] --> B[Identify radii r1 r2 r3 r4 and angles alpha1 alpha2 alpha3 alpha4]
    B --> C[Choose two reference planes Plane A and Plane B at distance L apart]
    C --> D[Compute centrifugal force F equals m times r times omega squared for each mass]
    D --> E[Resolve F of each mass into X and Y components]
    E --> F[Transfer force from original plane to reference planes using moment principle]
    F --> G[Compute sum of F at Plane A: Sigma F Ax and Sigma F Ay]
    G --> H[Compute sum of F at Plane B: Sigma F Bx and Sigma F By]
    H --> I[Apply static balance at Plane A: mA times rA equals resultant of sums]
    I --> J[Apply static balance at Plane B: mB times rB equals resultant of sums]
    J --> K[Find angle of mA using tan inverse of Fy over Fx]
    K --> L[Find angle of mB using tan inverse of Fy over Fx]
    L --> M[OUTPUT: Balance masses mA and mB with their angles and radii]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Gyroscopic Couple Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Disc spinning at omega about Z axis] --> B[External torque T applied about X axis]
    B --> C[Disc resists tilting due to angular momentum H equals I times omega]
    C --> D[Spin axis precesses about Y axis at omega p]
    D --> E[Rate of change of H equals omega p cross H]
    E --> F[Gyroscopic couple C equals I times omega times omega p times sin theta]
    F --> G{Is the spin axis perpendicular to precession axis}
    G --> H[YES: sin theta equals 1, C equals I times omega times omega p]
    G --> I[NO: Use C equals I times omega times omega p times sin theta]
    H --> J[OUTPUT: Magnitude of reactive gyroscopic couple]
    I --> J[OUTPUT: Magnitude of reactive gyroscopic couple]
```

## 4.3 ASCII Reference Sketch — Multi-Plane Masses

```
   z-axis (along shaft)
       ↑
       |        Plane I        Plane II       Plane III      Plane IV
       |       (z=z1)          (z=z2)          (z=z3)         (z=z4)
       |     ●m1,r1,α1       ●m2,r2,α2      ●m3,r3,α3      ●m4,r4,α4
       |     |               |               |              |
       |     |               |               |              |
       +-----+-------+-------+-------+-------+--------------+----→ reference
             |       |               |              |
         Plane A ←——————————— L ———————————————→ Plane B
       (z=0)                                     (z=L)
       ●mA,rA,αA                              ●mB,rB,αB
```

> **Reading the sketch:** The four original masses are relocated as two equivalent masses $m_A$ and $m_B$ in reference planes A and B, separated by distance $L$. The balance calculations are now solved in 2-D in each plane.

## 4.4 ASCII Reference Sketch — Gyroscopic Precession

```
                     Spin axis (Z)
                        ↑
                        |      ●  (Disc spinning at ω)
                        |     /|
                        |    / |
                        |   /  |
                        |  /   |
                        | /    |
                        |/     |
   Precession axis (Y) ──+──────+──→ X-axis (applied torque axis)
                        |      ωp (precessional angular velocity)
                        |
                        ↓
                    Reactive couple C = Iωωp
```

> **Right-Hand Rule:** Fingers curl in direction of spin; thumb points along $\vec{H}$. The reactive gyroscopic couple acts about the precession axis according to $d\vec{H}/dt = \vec{T}_{ext}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Define static and dynamic balancing. Why is dynamic balancing of a multi-plane rotor not guaranteed by static balance alone?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Static Balancing:** A rotor is in static balance if the **resultant of the centrifugal forces** of all its rotating masses is zero ($\sum m_i \vec{r}_i = 0$). This requires only that the mass centre lies on the axis of rotation.

> **Dynamic Balancing:** A rotor is in complete (dynamic) balance when, in addition to the static condition, the **resultant couple** due to the centrifugal forces is also zero ($\sum m_i \vec{r}_i z_i = 0$).

> **Why static ≠ dynamic:** Two equal masses placed on **opposite sides of the shaft** (180° apart) at the **same radius** can be statically balanced because their force vectors cancel. However, if they lie in **different axial planes**, they create a **couple** that rocks the shaft. This couple is invisible to a static (gravity) test but causes severe vibration at operating speed. Therefore, two reference balancing planes are required to fix both the net force and the net couple — this is the essence of dynamic balancing.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is gyroscopic precession? State the formula for the gyroscopic couple and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Gyroscopic Precession:** When a spinning disc is subjected to an external torque about an axis perpendicular to its spin axis, the disc axis itself rotates (precesses) about a **third mutually perpendicular axis** at angular velocity $\omega_p$, rather than tilting in the direction of the applied torque.

> **Gyroscopic Couple Formula:**
> $$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p \sin\theta$$

> where $C$ = gyroscopic couple [N·m], $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the disc about its spin axis [kg·m²], $\omega$ = spin angular velocity [rad/s], $\omega_p$ = precessional angular velocity [rad/s], and $\theta$ = angle between spin axis and precession axis. For most engineering applications the axes are perpendicular, so $\sin\theta = 1$ and $C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In multi-plane balancing:** Students frequently forget to **divide by $L$** when transferring forces to reference planes. Each step that requires division by $L$ costs **1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Sign convention:** If $\alpha_i$ is measured counter-clockwise, $\cos$ and $\sin$ signs must be carried carefully. Mixing clockwise vs. counter-clockwise angles silently corrupts the final answer — examiners deduct **2 marks** for a sign error.
> 3. **For gyroscope problems:** Always state the **direction of precession** explicitly (CW or CCW) and the **axis** (pitching, rolling, yawing) — merely writing the formula without the kinematic interpretation costs **2 marks** in ESE.
> 4. **Reciprocating mass problems:** Distinguish between **primary** ($\cos\theta$, frequency $\omega$) and **secondary** ($\cos 2\theta$, frequency $2\omega$) — confusing them is the most common single-cause of zero marks in 14-mark problems.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**A rotor carries four masses in four different planes as detailed below. The balancing is to be done in planes A and B located 100 mm apart.**

| Mass | Magnitude (kg) | Radius (mm) | Plane location $z_i$ (mm) | Angular position $\alpha_i$ (°) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $m_1$ | 6 | 80 | 0 (Plane A) | 0 |
| $m_2$ | 4 | 100 | 30 | 90 |
| $m_3$ | 5 | 90 | 70 | 180 |
| $m_4$ | 3 | 120 | 100 (Plane B) | 270 |

**Find:** **(a)** The magnitude and angular position of the balance mass in Plane A. **(b)** The magnitude and angular position of the balance mass in Plane B. **Assume the balance radii as 100 mm in both planes.**

#### **Solution to Q-A(a): Forces at Plane A**

**Step 1:** Compute $m_i r_i$ for each mass:
- $m_1 r_1 = 6 \times 80 = 480$ kg·mm
- $m_2 r_2 = 4 \times 100 = 400$ kg·mm
- $m_3 r_3 = 5 \times 90 = 450$ kg·mm
- $m_4 r_4 = 3 \times 120 = 360$ kg·mm

**Step 2:** Transfer factor to Plane A: $(L - z_i)/L$ with $L = 100$ mm.

| Mass | $z_i$ (mm) | $(L - z_i)/L$ | $m_i r_i \cdot (L-z_i)/L$ (kg·mm) | $\cos\alpha_i$ | $\sin\alpha_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $m_1$ | 0 | 1.00 | 480 | 1 | 0 |
| $m_2$ | 30 | 0.70 | 280 | 0 | 1 |
| $m_3$ | 70 | 0.30 | 135 | -1 | 0 |
| $m_4$ | 100 | 0.00 | 0 | 0 | -1 |

**Step 3:** Sum X-components at Plane A:
$$\sum F_{A,x} = 480(1) + 280(0) + 135(-1) + 0 = 345 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Step 4:** Sum Y-components at Plane A:
$$\sum F_{A,y} = 480(0) + 280(1) + 135(0) + 0 = 280 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude of balance mass moment in Plane A:
$$m_A r_A = \sqrt{345^2 + 280^2} = \sqrt{119025 + 78400} = \sqrt{197425} \approx 444.3 \text{ kg·mm}$$

With $r_A = 100$ mm: $\quad m_A = 444.3/100 = 4.443$ kg

**Step 6:** Angular position:
$$\alpha_A = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{280}{345}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.8116) \approx 39.05°$$

> **Answer (a):** $m_A \approx 4.44$ kg at $\alpha_A \approx 39°$ (counter-clockwise from reference).

#### **Solution to Q-A(b): Forces at Plane B**

**Step 1:** Transfer factor to Plane B: $z_i/L$.

| Mass | $z_i$ (mm) | $z_i/L$ | $m_i r_i \cdot z_i/L$ (kg·mm) | $\cos\alpha_i$ | $\sin\alpha_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $m_1$ | 0 | 0.00 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| $m_2$ | 30 | 0.30 | 120 | 0 | 1 |
| $m_3$ | 70 | 0.70 | 315 | -1 | 0 |
| $m_4$ | 100 | 1.00 | 360 | 0 | -1 |

**Step 2:** Sum X-components at Plane B:
$$\sum F_{B,x} = 0 + 0 + 315(-1) + 0 = -315 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Step 3:** Sum Y-components at Plane B:
$$\sum F_{B,y} = 0 + 120(1) + 0 + 360(-1) = -240 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude of balance mass moment in Plane B:
$$m_B r_B = \sqrt{(-315)^2 + (-240)^2} = \sqrt{99225 + 57600} = \sqrt{156825} \approx 396.0 \text{ kg·mm}$$

With $r_B = 100$ mm: $\quad m_B = 396.0/100 = 3.96$ kg

**Step 5:** Angular position:
$$\alpha_B = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-240}{-315}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.7619) \approx 37.3°$$

Since both X and Y components are negative, the resultant lies in the **third quadrant**:
$$\alpha_B = 180° + 37.3° = 217.3°$$

> **Answer (b):** $m_B \approx 3.96$ kg at $\alpha_B \approx 217.3°$ (counter-clockwise from reference).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the gyroscopic couple experienced by a four-wheeled vehicle taking a turn, and explain its effect on the vehicle.

**(b)** An aeroplane makes a complete turn in 50 seconds at a constant speed of 720 km/h, banking at 30° to the horizontal. The moment of inertia of the propeller is 50 kg·m² and the engine rotates at 2400 RPM clockwise when viewed from the rear. Find the magnitude and direction of the gyroscopic couple on the aircraft.

#### **Solution to Q-B(a): Derivation**

**Setup:** A four-wheeled vehicle of mass $m$ takes a turn of radius $R$ at velocity $V$. The wheels are rotating at angular speed $\omega_w = V/r_w$ (where $r_w$ is wheel radius) about a horizontal axis. The vehicle itself rotates (turns) about a vertical axis with angular velocity $\omega_p = V/R$.

**Step 1 — Angular momentum of the wheels:**
$$H_{wheels} = I_w \cdot \omega_w \quad \text{(along horizontal axis, along the wheel axle)}$$

For two pairs of wheels (front and rear) with combined moment of inertia $I_w$:
$$\vec{H}_{wheels} = I_w \cdot \omega_w \cdot \hat{j}$$
(where $\hat{j}$ is along the axle direction, e.g., along the vehicle's width).

**Step 2 — Precession of the vehicle:**
The vehicle yaws (turns) about the vertical axis at $\omega_p$. The horizontal $\vec{H}$ vector therefore precesses about the vertical axis.

**Step 3 — Reactive gyroscopic couple:**
$$C = I_w \cdot \omega_w \cdot \omega_p = I_w \cdot \frac{V}{r_w} \cdot \frac{V}{R} = \frac{I_w \cdot V^2}{r_w \cdot R}$$

**Step 4 — Effect of the couple:**
- The reactive couple tends to **pitch the vehicle** — inner wheels tend to lift while outer wheels tend to press down (or vice versa, depending on the direction of turn and wheel rotation).
- This causes **uneven load distribution** on the wheels during a turn, especially noticeable in two-wheelers and high-speed cars.
- In two-wheelers, this is the principle behind the **counter-steering technique** used by professional riders.

> **Sub-answer (a) complete:** $C = \dfrac{I_w V^2}{r_w R}$ N·m, producing a pitching moment.

#### **Solution to Q-B(b): Numerical Gyroscopic Couple**

**Given:**
- Turn time $T = 50$ s → precession rate $\omega_p = 2\pi/T = 2\pi/50 = 0.1257$ rad/s
- Spin speed of propeller $N = 2400$ RPM → $\omega = 2\pi N/60 = 2\pi(2400)/60 = 251.33$ rad/s
- Moment of inertia $I = 50$ kg·m²
- Banking angle 30° (used for finding the component of weight, but for **gyroscopic couple** only spin × precession × $I$ matters; banking does not enter the couple formula directly — it affects the **resultant** moment in the airplane's frame, which is a second-order effect).

**Step 1 — Magnitude of gyroscopic couple:**
$$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p = 50 \times 251.33 \times 0.1257$$
$$C = 50 \times 31.59 = 1579.5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 1580 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 2 — Direction:**
- Propeller spins **clockwise** (viewed from rear) → angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ points **rearward** (away from the pilot, into the tail).
- Aircraft turns — the pitching plane (longitudinal-vertical plane) changes. $\vec{H}$ precesses, generating a reactive couple.

**Applying the right-hand rule for active precession:** If the aircraft is pitching nose-down (nose rotating downward), $\vec{H}$ rotates in a vertical plane. The reactive gyroscopic couple is given by $\vec{C} = d\vec{H}/dt$, which acts **perpendicular** to the pitch motion.

**For a left turn with engine rotating CW (rear view):**
- $\vec{H}$ points backward.
- Yawing (turning) left means $\vec{H}$ rotates toward the left wing.
- Reactive couple acts about the **pitch axis**, tending to **pitch the nose upward** (i.e., the aircraft tends to rise on turn-entry).

> **Sub-answer (b) complete:** $C \approx 1580$ N·m, acting about the pitching axis, tending to **raise the nose** on a left turn (or **dip the nose** on a right turn), with the propeller spinning clockwise viewed from the tail.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (KTU PCAUT502):**

- [x] **Static balance condition:** $\sum m_i r_i \cos\alpha_i = 0$ AND $\sum m_i r_i \sin\alpha_i = 0$. Two equations, two unknowns (one balancing mass: magnitude and angle).
- [x] **Dynamic balance condition:** Add $\sum m_i r_i z_i = 0$ (couple condition). Two balancing planes needed to provide 4 unknowns (2 magnitudes + 2 angles) for the 3 conditions.
- [x] **Reference plane technique:** Forces from intermediate planes are transferred to two end planes A and B using the moment rule — multiply by $(L - z_i)/L$ for Plane A and $z_i/L$ for Plane B.
- [x] **Centrifugal force:** Always $F = m r \omega^2$ directed **radially outward** from the rotation axis. $\omega^2$ cancels in all balance problems because every mass shares the same $\omega$.
- [x] **Primary force (reciprocating):** $F_p = m r \omega^2$ at frequency $\omega$.
- [x] **Secondary force (reciprocating):** $F_s = m r \omega^2 / n$ at frequency $2\omega$, where $n = L/r$.
- [x] **Complete balance of reciprocating masses** in a single cylinder is **impossible** without an auxiliary counter-rotating mass.
- [x] **Multi-cylinder engines** achieve balance by phase-shifting the primary/secondary forces between cylinders.
- [x] **Gyroscopic couple:** $C = I \omega \omega_p \sin\theta$. For perpendicular axes, $\sin\theta = 1$.
- [x] **Gyroscopic rigidity:** A spinning disc resists change in orientation of its spin axis. This is the basis of all inertial navigation systems.
- [x] **Precession direction:** Determined by the right-hand rule applied to $\vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$ — the reactive couple is along the direction of precession, but the *applied* couple is opposite to it.
- [x] **Real-world applications to remember:** (i) Ship rolling/turning — gyroscopic couple tilts the ship; (ii) Aeroplane pitching/yawing during turns; (iii) Two-wheeler on a curve — gyroscopic couple causes a steering moment; (iv) Locomotive hammer-blow and swaying couple are reciprocating-mass effects.
- [x] **Units to be careful with:** Convert RPM → rad/s by multiplying by $2\pi/60$. Convert km/h → m/s by multiplying by $5/18$. Mass-radius products ($mr$) must use **consistent units** (kg·m or kg·mm).
- [x] **Sign convention:** Always define one direction as positive; state the convention explicitly at the start of the solution. KTU examiners will not award method marks if the sign convention is ambiguous.
- [x] **Vector form is preferred** in KTU 2024 Scheme for the 14-mark multi-plane question — write the final answer in both Cartesian and polar form for full marks.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — KTU PCAUT502 | Theory of Machines | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Angular Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Angular Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Angular Acceleration ($\alpha$)** is the time rate of change of **angular velocity ($\omega$)** of a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis. Mathematically, it is the second time derivative of the **angular displacement ($\theta$)**.

In vectorial form, angular acceleration is represented as:

$$
\vec{\alpha} \;=\; \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d^{2}\vec{\theta}}{dt^{2}}
$$

The **SI unit** of angular acceleration is **radians per second squared (rad/s²)** or simply **s⁻²**. The **CGS unit** is **rad/s²**, and in some engineering contexts, it is expressed in **revolutions per minute per second (rpm/s)** or **revolutions per second squared (rev/s²)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCAUT502 / Module 4**
> In Module 4 (Dynamics of Machines / Force Analysis), angular acceleration is a **primary kinematic input** for solving problems using Newton's laws in rotational form ($T = I\alpha$) and the **Work–Energy principle for rotating bodies** ($W = \frac{1}{2}I\omega_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}I\omega_1^2$). Board questions in this module consistently test whether students can correctly relate $\alpha$ to torque, inertia, and flywheel sizing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting on a playground **merry-go-round** with a friend pushing it. Initially it rotates slowly. As your friend pushes harder, the merry-go-round speeds up — its **angular velocity** is increasing. The rate at which this rotational speed is changing is the **angular acceleration**.

A more relatable real-world analogy is the **accelerator pedal of a car**:

- Pressing the pedal harder increases the engine's **rotational speed** (RPM) → the **engine crank** experiences positive **angular acceleration**.
- Releasing the pedal or braking reduces RPM → the crank experiences **deceleration** (negative angular acceleration).
- Cruising at a constant speed → **angular acceleration is zero**, even though the crank is still rotating at high speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Velocity vs. Acceleration**
> A rotating shaft can have a **large angular velocity** (e.g., 3000 rpm) but **zero angular acceleration** (constant speed). Conversely, a slowly rotating shaft can have a **large angular acceleration** (rapidly speeding up). This distinction is critical in KTU problems involving **flywheels**, where stored kinetic energy depends on $\omega$, but **torque demand** depends on $\alpha$.

### 1.3 Sign Convention and Vector Direction

The direction of the **angular acceleration vector** $\vec{\alpha}$ follows the **right-hand rule**:

- If $\omega$ is **increasing**, $\vec{\alpha}$ points in the **same direction** as $\vec{\omega}$ → **positive angular acceleration**.
- If $\omega$ is **decreasing**, $\vec{\alpha}$ points **opposite** to $\vec{\omega}$ → **negative angular acceleration** (also called **angular retardation** or **angular deceleration**).

The magnitude of angular acceleration is therefore a **signed scalar** in one-dimensional rotational problems, with the sign determined by whether the rotation is speeding up or slowing down.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Rotational Kinematic Quantities

The motion of a rigid body rotating about a **fixed axis** is described by three time-varying scalar quantities:

1. **Angular Displacement ($\theta$)** — measured in **radians (rad)**.
2. **Angular Velocity ($\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$)** — measured in **rad/s**.
3. **Angular Acceleration ($\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}$)** — measured in **rad/s²**.

These three quantities form a **hierarchy of derivatives** with respect to time, exactly analogous to the linear quantities $s$, $v$, and $a$.

### 2.2 Two Geometric Components of Linear Acceleration

When a point **P** on a rotating body is at a radial distance $r$ from the axis of rotation, its **total linear acceleration** $\vec{a}$ has **two orthogonal components**:

#### (a) Tangential Acceleration ($a_t$)

The tangential component arises **only when $\alpha \neq 0$**. It is directed **along the tangent** to the circular path of point P and is responsible for **changing the speed** of point P.

$$
a_t \;=\; r\,\alpha
$$

#### (b) Normal / Centripetal Acceleration ($a_n$ or $a_c$)

The normal component arises **whenever $\omega \neq 0$**, even if $\alpha = 0$. It is directed **radially inward** (toward the axis of rotation) and is responsible for **changing the direction** of velocity.

$$
a_n \;=\; r\,\omega^{2}
$$

#### (c) Total Linear Acceleration Magnitude

The total linear acceleration of point P is the **vector sum** of $a_t$ and $a_n$:

$$
a \;=\; \sqrt{a_t^{\,2} + a_n^{\,2}} \;=\; \sqrt{(r\alpha)^{2} + (r\omega^{2})^{2}} \;=\; r\,\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \omega^{4}}
$$

### 2.3 Equations of Rotational Motion (Constant $\alpha$ Case)

When angular acceleration is **constant** (uniform angular acceleration), the kinematic equations are perfectly analogous to the linear SUVAT equations:

- $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$
- $\theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$
- $\omega^{2} = \omega_0^{\,2} + 2\alpha\theta$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Constant $\alpha$" Matters in KTU**
> Most solved KTU problems on flywheels, brakes, and clutch engagement **assume constant $\alpha$** for simplicity. When $\alpha$ is variable (e.g., during one full engine cycle), **mean angular acceleration** is used: $\alpha_{mean} = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Angular acceleration is a foundational quantity in:

- **Flywheel design** — sizing the moment of inertia $I$ to limit $\alpha$ fluctuations.
- **Engine dynamics** — computing mean torque from $T = I\alpha$.
- **Brake and clutch analysis** — time to stop: $t = \dfrac{\omega_0}{\alpha} = \dfrac{I\omega_0}{T_{retarding}}$.
- **Robot arm control** — trajectory planning in joint space.
- **Turbogenerator transient analysis** — angular acceleration during grid disturbances.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Time rate of change of angular velocity | rad/s² |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}$ | Second derivative of angular displacement | rad/s² |
| 3 | $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$ | Final angular velocity (constant $\alpha$) | rad/s |
| 4 | $\theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$ | Angular displacement (constant $\alpha$) | rad |
| 5 | $\omega^{2} = \omega_0^{\,2} + 2\alpha\theta$ | Velocity–displacement relation | rad²/s² |
| 6 | $a_t = r\alpha$ | Tangential linear acceleration of a point at radius $r$ | m/s² |
| 7 | $a_n = r\omega^{2}$ | Normal (centripetal) linear acceleration | m/s² |
| 8 | $a = r\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \omega^{4}}$ | Total linear acceleration magnitude | m/s² |
| 9 | $\alpha_{avg} = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t}$ | Average angular acceleration | rad/s² |
| 10 | $T = I\alpha$ | Newton's 2nd law for rotation (torque–inertia–alpha) | N·m |
| 11 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{2\pi\,(N_2 - N_1)}{60\,t}$ | Angular acceleration from RPM change | rad/s² |
| 12 | $\alpha = \dfrac{2\pi\,N}{60}$ | Conversion: RPM → rad/s (velocity only) | rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Note**
> Whenever a problem provides speeds in **rpm (N)**, convert to rad/s using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ **before** computing $\alpha$, $a_t$, or $a_n$. KTU examiners frequently award 1 mark purely for correct unit conversion.

### 2.6 Why These Relationships Exist — The Logical Chain

- **Why does $a_t = r\alpha$?** Because a point at radius $r$ sweeps an arc of length $s = r\theta$. Differentiating twice: $v = r\omega$ and $a_t = r\alpha$. The geometric factor $r$ simply scales the rotational quantities to linear quantities at a specific point on the body.
- **Why does $a_n = r\omega^{2}$?** Because any object moving in a circle of radius $r$ at speed $v = r\omega$ must be continuously deflected toward the center, requiring a centripetal acceleration $v^{2}/r = r\omega^{2}$.
- **Why $T = I\alpha$?** Newton's second law for a particle ($F = ma$) generalizes to a rigid body: torque $T$ replaces force, and the mass $m$ is replaced by the moment of inertia $I$ about the axis of rotation. This is the single most important formula in Module 4 of PCAUT502.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Angular Displacement to Angular Acceleration

**Starting Point:** Define angular displacement $\theta$ as a function of time, $\theta = \theta(t)$.

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity (First Derivative):**
By definition, angular velocity is the time rate of change of angular displacement.

$$
\omega(t) \;=\; \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

*Logical reasoning:* If a body sweeps an angle $\Delta\theta$ in time $\Delta t$, the average angular velocity is $\Delta\theta/\Delta t$. Taking the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$ gives the instantaneous angular velocity.

**Step 2 — Angular Acceleration (Second Derivative):**
By definition, angular acceleration is the time rate of change of angular velocity.

$$
\alpha(t) \;=\; \frac{d\omega}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{dt}\right)
$$

Applying the chain rule of differentiation:

$$
\alpha(t) \;=\; \frac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}
$$

This proves the relationship $\alpha = d^{2}\theta/dt^{2}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Tangential Acceleration $a_t = r\alpha$

**Starting Point:** A point P at radial distance $r$ from the rotation axis traces a circular arc.

**Step 1 — Arc Length and Angular Displacement:**
The arc length swept by P is related to the angular displacement by the pure geometry of a circle:

$$
s \;=\; r\,\theta
$$

**Step 2 — First Time Derivative (Linear Velocity):**
Differentiating both sides with respect to time $t$ (and noting that $r$ is constant for a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis):

$$
\frac{ds}{dt} \;=\; r\,\frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

Since $v = ds/dt$ (tangential linear velocity) and $\omega = d\theta/dt$ (angular velocity):

$$
v \;=\; r\,\omega
$$

**Step 3 — Second Time Derivative (Tangential Acceleration):**
Differentiating again with respect to time:

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} \;=\; r\,\frac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

Since $a_t = dv/dt$ (tangential linear acceleration) and $\alpha = d\omega/dt$ (angular acceleration):

$$
\boxed{\,a_t \;=\; r\,\alpha\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Normal (Centripetal) Acceleration $a_n = r\omega^{2}$

**Starting Point:** Consider a particle moving with constant speed $v$ along a circular path of radius $r$.

**Step 1 — Velocity Vector Magnitude is Constant but Direction Changes:**
The velocity vector has magnitude $v$ and its direction is tangent to the circle. As the particle moves through an angle $\Delta\theta$, the velocity vector rotates by the same angle $\Delta\theta$.

**Step 2 — Change in Velocity Vector:**
The magnitude of the change in the velocity vector (using the small-angle chord approximation) is:

$$
\Delta v \;\approx\; v\,\Delta\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Rate of Change of Velocity:**

$$
a_n \;=\; \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} \;=\; v\,\lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{\Delta\theta}{\Delta t} \;=\; v\,\omega
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v = r\omega$:**

$$
a_n \;=\; v\,\omega \;=\; (r\omega)(\omega) \;=\; r\,\omega^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,a_n \;=\; r\,\omega^{2}\,}
$$

This acceleration is directed **radially inward** toward the center of rotation.

### 3.4 Derivation: Total Linear Acceleration Magnitude

**Step 1 — Orthogonality of Components:**
The tangential acceleration $a_t$ is **perpendicular** to the radial line, and the normal acceleration $a_n$ is **along** the radial line. Hence they are mutually perpendicular.

**Step 2 — Apply the Pythagorean Theorem:**
Since $\vec{a}_t \perp \vec{a}_n$, the magnitude of the total linear acceleration is:

$$
a \;=\; \sqrt{a_t^{\,2} \;+\; a_n^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Component Expressions:**

$$
a \;=\; \sqrt{(r\alpha)^{2} \;+\; (r\omega^{2})^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $r$:**

$$
a \;=\; \sqrt{r^{2}\alpha^{2} \;+\; r^{2}\omega^{4}} \;=\; r\,\sqrt{\alpha^{2} \;+\; \omega^{4}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,a \;=\; r\,\sqrt{\alpha^{2} \;+\; \omega^{4}}\,}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Angular Acceleration from RPM Data (KTU Standard Form)

**Starting Point:** Many KTU problems give initial and final speeds in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Average Angular Acceleration:**

$$
\alpha_{avg} \;=\; \frac{\omega_2 \;-\; \omega_1}{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Conversion Formula:**

$$
\alpha_{avg} \;=\; \frac{1}{t}\left(\frac{2\pi N_2}{60} \;-\; \frac{2\pi N_1}{60}\right) \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,(N_2 - N_1)}{60\,t}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** that examiners expect to see in flywheel and brake problems.

### 3.6 Worked Analytical Example (Verifying the Framework)

> **Problem:** A flywheel rotating at **240 rpm** is brought to rest in **20 seconds** by a constant braking torque. Find: (i) the angular retardation, (ii) the number of revolutions made before stopping, and (iii) the tangential acceleration at a point **0.5 m** from the axis.

**Step (i) — Angular Retardation:**

Convert initial speed to rad/s:

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} \;=\; 8\pi \;\approx\; 25.133 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Final speed $\omega = 0$ (at rest). Apply $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$:

$$
0 \;=\; 8\pi + \alpha (20) \quad\Rightarrow\quad \alpha \;=\; -\frac{8\pi}{20} \;=\; -0.4\pi \;\approx\; -1.257 \text{ rad/s}^{2}
$$

The negative sign indicates **angular retardation** (deceleration).

**Step (ii) — Number of Revolutions Before Stopping:**

Apply $\omega^{2} = \omega_0^{\,2} + 2\alpha\theta$:

$$
0 \;=\; (8\pi)^{2} + 2(-0.4\pi)\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta \;=\; \frac{64\pi^{2}}{0.8\pi} \;=\; 80\pi \text{ rad}
$$

Convert to revolutions:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{\theta}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{80\pi}{2\pi} \;=\; 40 \text{ revolutions}
$$

**Step (iii) — Tangential Acceleration at $r = 0.5$ m:**

$$
a_t \;=\; r\,\alpha \;=\; 0.5 \times (-0.4\pi) \;=\; -0.2\pi \;\approx\; -0.628 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

The negative sign confirms the tangential direction is **opposite** to the motion (deceleration).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — The Derivative Cascade

This Mermaid block illustrates the **hierarchical relationship** between angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration, mirroring the linear kinematic chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Angular Displacement θ rad"] -->|d/dt| B["Angular Velocity ω rad/s"]
    B -->|d/dt| C["Angular Acceleration α rad/s²"]
    C -->|r × α| D["Tangential Acceleration a_t m/s²"]
    B -->|r × ω²| E["Normal Centripetal a_n m/s²"]
    D --> F["Total Linear Acceleration a m/s²"]
    E --> F
    C -->|T = Iα| G["Required Net Torque T N·m"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rotational Force Analysis

This diagram maps how angular acceleration integrates with **torque** and **moment of inertia** within Module 4 force-analysis problems (brake, clutch, flywheel).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DATA"]
        A1["Initial Speed ω₀ rpm"]
        A2["Final Speed ω rpm"]
        A3["Time Interval t s"]
        A4["Radius r m"]
        A5["Mass Moment of Inertia I kg·m²"]
        A6["Frictional / Braking Force F N"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["COMPUTATIONAL LAYER"]
        B1["ω = 2πN / 60"]
        B2["α = ω − ω₀ / t"]
        B3["T = I × α"]
        B4["a_t = r × α"]
        B5["a_n = r × ω²"]
        B6["a = r × sqrt(α² + ω⁴)"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"]
        C1["Angular Retardation rad/s²"]
        C2["Braking Torque N·m"]
        C3["Number of Revolutions N"]
        C4["Tangential Acceleration m/s²"]
        C5["Total Linear Acceleration m/s²"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    A5 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C2
    B2 --> B4
    A4 --> B4
    B4 --> C4
    A4 --> B5
    B1 --> B5
    B4 --> B5
    B6 --> C5
    B1 --> C3
    B2 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Geometry of a Rotating Point — Tangential vs. Normal Components

This Mermaid block renders the **physical geometry** of a point P on a rotating rigid body, showing the orientation of the two linear acceleration components.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O["Axis of Rotation O"] ---|"Radius r"| P["Point P on Rim"]
    P -->|a_t = r·α| PT["Tangent Direction a_t"]
    P -->|a_n = r·ω²| PN["Radial Inward a_n"]
    PT --- ANNOT1["Changes SPEED of P"]
    PN --- ANNOT2["Changes DIRECTION of P"]
    O -->|ω| ROT["Angular Velocity Vector along axis"]
    O -->|α| ACC["Angular Acceleration Vector along axis"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define angular acceleration. State its SI unit and write its mathematical expression in terms of angular displacement.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Angular acceleration is the time rate of change of angular velocity of a body rotating about a fixed axis. It is a vector quantity that describes how rapidly the rotational speed of a body is increasing or decreasing.

**SI Unit:** rad/s² (radian per second squared) or s⁻².

**Mathematical Expressions:**

- In terms of angular velocity: $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$
- In terms of angular displacement: $\alpha = \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}$

The direction of $\vec{\alpha}$ is along the axis of rotation, following the right-hand rule. It is positive when $\omega$ is increasing and negative (angular retardation) when $\omega$ is decreasing.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for SI unit, 1 for expression]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A point on a rotating disc is at 0.4 m from the axis. If the disc has an angular velocity of 20 rad/s and angular acceleration of 5 rad/s², find the magnitudes of tangential and normal accelerations of the point.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $r = 0.4$ m, $\omega = 20$ rad/s, $\alpha = 5$ rad/s².

**Tangential acceleration:**

$$
a_t \;=\; r\,\alpha \;=\; 0.4 \times 5 \;=\; 2.0 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Normal (centripetal) acceleration:**

$$
a_n \;=\; r\,\omega^{2} \;=\; 0.4 \times (20)^{2} \;=\; 0.4 \times 400 \;=\; 160 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Result:** $a_t = 2.0$ m/s², $a_n = 160$ m/s². Since $a_n \gg a_t$, the point's acceleration is **almost entirely centripetal**, with only a small tangential component causing the speed to gradually increase.

**[3 Marks: 1 for formula + setup, 1 for $a_t$, 1 for $a_n$]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**A flywheel of mass 250 kg and radius of gyration 0.4 m is rotating at 300 rpm. It is uniformly accelerated for 10 seconds until it reaches 450 rpm.**

**(a)** Determine the **angular acceleration**, the **torque required**, and the **kinetic energy gained** by the flywheel.
**(b)** Find the **tangential and normal linear accelerations** of a point on the rim at the **instant** the flywheel reaches 450 rpm.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:**

$$
\omega_1 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} \;=\; 10\pi \;\approx\; 31.416 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_2 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 450}{60} \;=\; 15\pi \;\approx\; 47.124 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Acceleration:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{t} \;=\; \frac{15\pi - 10\pi}{10} \;=\; \frac{5\pi}{10} \;=\; 0.5\pi \;\approx\; 1.571 \text{ rad/s}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Moment of Inertia:**

$$
I \;=\; m\,k^{2} \;=\; 250 \times (0.4)^{2} \;=\; 250 \times 0.16 \;=\; 40 \text{ kg·m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Torque:**

$$
T \;=\; I\,\alpha \;=\; 40 \times 0.5\pi \;=\; 20\pi \;\approx\; 62.83 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Kinetic Energy Gained:**

$$
\Delta KE \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,I\left(\omega_2^{\,2} - \omega_1^{\,2}\right) \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times \left[(15\pi)^{2} - (10\pi)^{2}\right]
$$

$$
\Delta KE \;=\; 20 \times \pi^{2}\,(225 - 100) \;=\; 20 \times \pi^{2} \times 125 \;=\; 2500\,\pi^{2} \;\approx\; 24{,}674 \text{ J} \;\approx\; 24.67 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Result:** $\alpha \approx 1.571$ rad/s², $T \approx 62.83$ N·m, $\Delta KE \approx 24.67$ kJ.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given at the instant of 450 rpm:** $r = 0.4$ m, $\omega = 15\pi$ rad/s, $\alpha = 0.5\pi$ rad/s².

**Tangential Acceleration:**

$$
a_t \;=\; r\,\alpha \;=\; 0.4 \times 0.5\pi \;=\; 0.2\pi \;\approx\; 0.628 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Normal Acceleration:**

$$
a_n \;=\; r\,\omega^{2} \;=\; 0.4 \times (15\pi)^{2} \;=\; 0.4 \times 225\pi^{2} \;=\; 90\pi^{2} \;\approx\; 888.3 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Total Linear Acceleration Magnitude:**

$$
a \;=\; \sqrt{a_t^{\,2} + a_n^{\,2}} \;=\; \sqrt{(0.2\pi)^{2} + (90\pi^{2})^{2}} \;\approx\; 888.3 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

(Since $a_n \gg a_t$, $a \approx a_n$.)

**Direction of total acceleration** (angle $\phi$ from radial direction):

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \frac{a_t}{a_n} \;=\; \frac{0.2\pi}{90\pi^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.2}{90\pi} \;\approx\; 7.07 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\phi \;\approx\; 0.0405^{\circ} \text{ (almost purely radial)}
$$

**Result:** $a_t \approx 0.628$ m/s², $a_n \approx 888.3$ m/s², $a \approx 888.3$ m/s², almost entirely directed radially inward.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**A grinding wheel of mass 80 kg and radius of gyration 0.25 m rotates at 1200 rpm. A braking torque brings it uniformly to rest in 40 seconds.**

**(a)** Compute the **angular retardation**, the **number of revolutions** during braking, and the **heat dissipated** by the brake.
**(b)** For a point on the outer rim (radius 0.3 m), find the **tangential and normal components** of linear acceleration at the **start** of braking.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert initial speed:**

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} \;=\; 40\pi \;\approx\; 125.66 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Final speed $\omega = 0$ at $t = 40$ s.

**Step 2 — Angular Retardation:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \frac{\omega - \omega_0}{t} \;=\; \frac{0 - 40\pi}{40} \;=\; -\pi \;\approx\; -3.142 \text{ rad/s}^{2}
$$

Magnitude of angular retardation: $\vert\alpha\vert = \pi$ rad/s².

**Step 3 — Moment of Inertia:**

$$
I \;=\; m\,k^{2} \;=\; 80 \times (0.25)^{2} \;=\; 80 \times 0.0625 \;=\; 5.0 \text{ kg·m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Revolutions:**

Using $\omega^{2} = \omega_0^{\,2} + 2\alpha\theta$:

$$
0 \;=\; (40\pi)^{2} + 2(-\pi)\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta \;=\; \frac{1600\pi^{2}}{2\pi} \;=\; 800\pi \text{ rad}
$$

$$
N \;=\; \frac{\theta}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{800\pi}{2\pi} \;=\; 400 \text{ revolutions}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat Dissipated (Equal to Initial KE):**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,I\,\omega_0^{\,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \times 5.0 \times (40\pi)^{2} \;=\; 2.5 \times 1600\pi^{2} \;=\; 4000\pi^{2} \;\approx\; 39{,}478 \text{ J} \;\approx\; 39.48 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Result:** $\vert\alpha\vert = \pi$ rad/s², $N = 400$ rev, $Q \approx 39.48$ kJ.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given at start of braking:** $r = 0.3$ m, $\omega_0 = 40\pi$ rad/s, $\alpha = -\pi$ rad/s².

**Tangential Acceleration:**

$$
a_t \;=\; r\,\alpha \;=\; 0.3 \times (-\pi) \;=\; -0.3\pi \;\approx\; -0.942 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

The negative sign indicates the tangential acceleration is **opposite to the direction of motion** (deceleration along the rim).

**Normal Acceleration:**

$$
a_n \;=\; r\,\omega_0^{\,2} \;=\; 0.3 \times (40\pi)^{2} \;=\; 0.3 \times 1600\pi^{2} \;=\; 480\pi^{2} \;\approx\; 4737.4 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Total Linear Acceleration:**

$$
a \;=\; \sqrt{a_t^{\,2} + a_n^{\,2}} \;=\; \sqrt{(-0.3\pi)^{2} + (480\pi^{2})^{2}} \;\approx\; 4737.4 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Result:** $\vert a_t \vert \approx 0.942$ m/s² (decelerating), $a_n \approx 4737.4$ m/s² (centripetal). The centripetal component is roughly **5000 times larger** than the tangential component, demonstrating that even a slowly decelerating rim point still experiences enormous radial acceleration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rpm-to-rad/s conversion.** A 1-mark deduction is automatic if $\omega = N$ is used directly in rad/s² formulas. Always write $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Sign confusion in angular retardation.** Examiners expect students to either (a) carry the negative sign through and report $\alpha = -\pi$ rad/s², or (b) clearly state "retardation of $\pi$ rad/s²." Mixing the two styles loses marks.
> 3. **Wrong formula for $I$.** Moment of inertia of a **disc** about its own axis is $\tfrac{1}{2}mr^{2}$. Moment of inertia of a **disc** about a **diameter** is $\tfrac{1}{4}mr^{2}$. For a **flywheel ring** (mass concentrated at rim), $I = mr^{2}$. For a **solid cylinder**, $I = \tfrac{1}{2}mr^{2}$. Read the geometry in the question carefully.
> 4. **Using $a_t$ when $a_n$ is asked (or vice versa).** Examiners check whether the student wrote $r\alpha$ or $r\omega^{2}$. Mixing them up forfeits 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Neglecting the kinetic energy relation.** Heat dissipated by a brake = loss in KE of the rotating system. Many students incorrectly try to compute $Q = T \cdot \theta$ with wrong sign conventions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Angular acceleration $\alpha$** is the time derivative of angular velocity, with **SI unit rad/s²**.
- **Hierarchy of derivatives:** $\theta \to \omega \to \alpha$ mirrors the linear chain $s \to v \to a$.
- **Two linear acceleration components** exist for any point on a rotating body: $a_t = r\alpha$ (tangential) and $a_n = r\omega^{2}$ (normal/centripetal).
- **Total linear acceleration:** $a = r\sqrt{\alpha^{2} + \omega^{4}}$ (Pythagorean sum of orthogonal components).
- **Constant-$\alpha$ kinematic equations:** $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$, $\theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$, $\omega^{2} = \omega_0^{\,2} + 2\alpha\theta$.
- **Newton's 2nd law for rotation:** $T = I\alpha$ — the single most applied formula in Module 4 of PCAUT502.
- **Average angular acceleration** for variable $\alpha$: $\alpha_{avg} = (\omega_2 - \omega_1)/t$.
- **Mandatory unit conversion:** $\omega$ [rad/s] $= 2\pi N / 60$, where $N$ is in rpm.
- **Energy stored in a rotating body:** $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$; **heat dissipated by brake** = drop in KE.
- **Sign convention:** positive $\alpha$ = speeding up; negative $\alpha$ = angular retardation / deceleration.
- **Vector direction** of $\vec{\alpha}$ follows the **right-hand rule** along the axis of rotation.
- **Exam tip:** Always distinguish between **angular retardation** (magnitude with clear sign) and the **time** it takes to stop: $t_{stop} = \omega_0 / \vert\alpha\vert$.
- **Common KTU Module 4 applications:** flywheel sizing, clutch engagement, brake analysis, pulley dynamics, governor sensitivity, and gyroscopic effects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Airplanes and Ships, Stability of Automobile and Two-Wheel Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT502 - THEORY OF MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Airplanes and Ships, Stability of Automobile and Two-Wheel Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Gyroscopic Couple — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gyroscopic Couple:** When a body possessing **angular momentum** (such as a spinning rotor) is subjected to a **forced precession** (i.e., its axis of spin is tilted/twisted by an external moment), the body reacts by developing a **resisting couple** known as the **gyroscopic couple**. Its magnitude is the rate of change of angular momentum, and its direction is governed by the **right-hand rule**.

Mathematically, the **active gyroscopic couple** is expressed as:

$$C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$$

where:
- $I$ = Mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its own axis of spin (kg·m²)
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity of the rotor about its own axis (rad/s)
- $\omega_p$ = Angular velocity of precession (rad/s)
- $C$ = Gyroscopic couple (N·m)

The **reactive gyroscopic couple** is equal in magnitude, opposite in sense, and acts on the supporting frame (bearings, fuselage, hull, chassis).

## 1.2 The Three Axes of a Vehicle (Foundation Concept)

Before analyzing airplanes, ships, or vehicles, we must recognize **three mutually perpendicular axes** passing through the center of gravity:

| Axis | Name | Motion Described |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Longitudinal (Roll axis, $x$) | Pitching axis | Nodding (up/down) |
| Lateral (Yaw/Transverse axis, $y$) | Rolling axis | Banking (sideways tilt) |
| Vertical (Spin axis, $z$) | Yawing axis | Spinning (in place) |

> [!NOTE]
> In an **airplane**, the propeller/engine spins about the **longitudinal axis**.
> In a **ship**, the turbine shaft spins about the **longitudinal axis**.
> In an **automobile**, the engine/flywheel spins about the **vertical axis (transverse crankshaft)** or the longitudinal axis (longitudinal crankshaft).
> In a **two-wheeler**, the wheels spin about the **transverse axis**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Top

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine holding a spinning bicycle wheel by its axle.** When you try to twist the axle sideways (tilt the wheel), the wheel *resists* this twisting and instead swings *perpendicular* to the applied force. This perpendicular reactive motion is the **gyroscopic effect** in action. The same physics governs an airplane banking, a ship turning, and a motorcycle leaning into a curve.

## 1.4 Stability — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability of a Vehicle:** A vehicle is said to be in **stable equilibrium** if, when slightly displaced from its position of rest, it returns to its original position (or oscillates about it) under the action of gravity and inertia forces. In vehicle dynamics, we analyze:
> - **Directional stability** (ability to move in a straight line).
> - **Rollover / overturning stability** (ability to resist tipping).

## 1.5 Real-World Significance

The gyroscopic effect is **beneficial** in some cases (e.g., stabilizing a bicycle or a ship against rolling waves) and **harmful** in others (e.g., causing a ship to "swing" during sharp turns or an airplane to "nose-dive" during pitching maneuvers). Quantifying these effects is essential for safe vehicle design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on an Airplane

An airplane has **three angular motions**:
1. **Rolling** — rotation about the **longitudinal (front-to-back) axis**.
2. **Pitching** — rotation about the **lateral (wingtip-to-wingtip) axis**.
3. **Yawing** — rotation about the **vertical axis**.

The **propeller** (or jet engine rotor) spins about the **longitudinal axis** with angular velocity $\omega$. The gyroscopic couple therefore arises when the airplane executes rolling, pitching, or yawing motion.

### 2.1.1 Airplane Rolling (Banking Turn)

When the airplane takes a turn, the **lift** force tilts inward. The airplane executes **rolling** motion with angular velocity $\omega_p$. The gyroscopic couple resists this banking.

- Magnitude: $C = I\omega\omega_p$
- Effect: Produces **yawing tendency** (nose swings in or out of the turn).

### 2.1.2 Airplane Pitching (Take-off and Landing)

During take-off, the airplane **noses up** (rotates about the lateral axis) with angular velocity $\omega_p$. The propeller spins about the longitudinal axis with $\omega$.

- The gyroscopic couple has magnitude: $C = I\omega\omega_p$
- Its plane of action contains the **transverse axis** and **vertical axis**.
- **Effect:**
  - When nose is raised (taking off): the gyroscopic couple tends to **turn the airplane to the left** (assuming propeller rotates clockwise when viewed from the front).
  - When nose is lowered (landing): the couple tends to **turn the airplane to the right**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board frequently asks: *Why does an airplane yaw during take-off?* — The answer is the **gyroscopic reactive couple** caused by pitching.

### 2.1.3 Airplane Yawing

A yawing motion (turning left/right without banking) produces a gyroscopic couple that tends to **roll the airplane**. This is generally small and is countered by the ailerons.

## 2.2 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on a Ship

The **turbine rotor** of a ship spins about the **longitudinal (fore-aft) axis** with angular velocity $\omega$.

### 2.2.1 Steering of a Ship (Turning)

When the rudder is deflected, the ship **turns** (yaws) about the **vertical axis** with angular velocity $\omega_p$.

- Gyroscopic couple: $C = I\omega\omega_p$
- **Effect:** The reactive couple tends to **pitch the ship** (i.e., the bow dips and stern rises, or vice versa) about the transverse axis.

> [!NOTE]
> For a ship moving in the **Northern Hemisphere**, when it steers to the **left (port)**, the gyroscopic couple causes the **bow to dip and stern to rise**. When steering to the **right (starboard)**, the bow rises and stern dips.

### 2.2.2 Pitching of a Ship (Encountering Waves)

When waves hit the ship from the bow, the ship **pitches** (rotates about the transverse axis) with angular velocity $\omega_p$.

- Gyroscopic couple: $C = I\omega\omega_p$
- **Effect:** The reactive couple causes the ship to **roll** about the longitudinal axis.

### 2.2.3 Rolling of a Ship (Beam Waves)

When waves strike the ship from the side (beam sea), the ship **rolls** with angular velocity $\omega_p$.

- Gyroscopic couple: $C = I\omega\omega_p$
- **Effect:** Produces **yawing motion** (ship tends to swing its course to port or starboard).

## 2.3 Stability of an Automobile

An automobile has two stability concerns:
- **Stability on a straight, level path** (resistance to side-tipping due to uneven load or wind gusts).
- **Stability on a curved path** (resistance to overturning and skidding).

### 2.3.1 Stability on a Level Curved Path (No Banking)

Let:
- $m$ = mass of the vehicle
- $r$ = radius of the curve
- $v$ = velocity of the vehicle
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity
- $h$ = height of center of gravity above road
- $a$ = half the track width (wheelbase half)
- $\theta$ = angle of overturning (tilt from vertical)

For **overturning condition**, equating overturning moment to restoring moment:

$$\text{Tan}\,\theta = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}$$

For the vehicle to **just overturn**, the center of gravity must be vertically above the outer wheel:

$$\frac{v^2}{g \cdot r} = \frac{a}{h} \quad\Rightarrow\quad v_o = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot r \cdot a}{h}}$$

For the vehicle to **just skid**, the centrifugal force equals the lateral friction:

$$v_s = \sqrt{\mu \cdot g \cdot r}$$

**Stability rule:** $v_o > v_s$ for stability against skidding before overturning. If $\mu < a/h$, the vehicle skids before overturning.

### 2.3.2 Stability on a Banked Curved Path

Let $\alpha$ = angle of banking.

For **no side-thrust** on wheels (ideal banking):

$$\text{Tan}\,\alpha = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}$$

For the vehicle to **not overturn** (CG above inner wheel):

$$\text{Tan}\,\alpha = \frac{v^2 - g \cdot r \cdot (a/h)}{g \cdot r \pm v^2 \cdot (a/h)}$$

## 2.4 Stability of a Two-Wheel Vehicle (Motorcycle/Bicycle)

A two-wheeler is inherently **unstable** at rest but achieves **dynamic stability** while moving due to:
1. **Gyroscopic effect of the wheels.**
2. **Trail of the front fork (caster action).**
3. **Rider's balancing input.**

### Condition of Stability

For a two-wheeler negotiating a turn of radius $r$ at speed $v$, the **angle of lean** $\theta$ required for stability (so that the weight vector passes through the line of contact) is:

$$\text{Tan}\,\theta = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-wheeler stability rule:** A two-wheeler must always lean **inward** during a turn, at an angle $\theta$ such that $\tan\theta = v^2/(gr)$. Otherwise, the centrifugal couple will overturn it outward.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Scenario | Formula | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | General Gyroscopic Couple | $C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m |
| 2 | Airplane Pitching (nose up/down) | $C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m |
| 3 | Airplane Rolling (banking) | $C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m |
| 4 | Ship Steering (turning) | $C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m |
| 5 | Ship Pitching (waves) | $C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m |
| 6 | Ship Rolling (beam waves) | $C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$ | N·m |
| 7 | Automobile overturning speed (level road) | $v_o = \sqrt{(g \cdot r \cdot a)/h}$ | m/s |
| 8 | Automobile skidding speed (level road) | $v_s = \sqrt{\mu \cdot g \cdot r}$ | m/s |
| 9 | Banking angle for no side thrust | $\tan\alpha = v^2/(g \cdot r)$ | rad |
| 10 | Two-wheeler angle of lean | $\tan\theta = v^2/(g \cdot r)$ | rad |
| 11 | Condition: vehicle skids before overturning | $\mu < a/h$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Condition: vehicle overturns before skidding | $\mu > a/h$ | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry** uses gyroscopic precession data to design autopilots, gyrostabilizers, and inertial navigation systems.
- **Marine engineering** uses these principles to size the turbine flywheel mass to minimize uncomfortable pitching during turns.
- **Automotive engineering** uses stability formulas to set safe speeds on curves, design banked tracks, and configure anti-roll bars.
- **Two-wheeler design** uses lean-angle data to set ground clearance, footpeg position, and tire profile.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Gyroscopic Couple Formula

### Setup

Consider a rotor (disc) of mass moment of inertia $I$ spinning about its own axis with angular velocity $\omega$. The **angular momentum** vector is:

$$\vec{H} = I \cdot \vec{\omega}$$

Assume the spin axis lies along the $x$-axis. So initially:

$$\vec{H}_1 = I\omega \,\hat{i}$$

Now, suppose an external moment causes the spin axis to **precess** about the $y$-axis (transverse) with angular velocity $\omega_p$. After a small time $dt$, the angular momentum vector rotates by a small angle $d\phi = \omega_p \, dt$ in the $xz$-plane.

### Change in Angular Momentum

The new angular momentum vector is:

$$\vec{H}_2 = I\omega \,(\cos(\omega_p dt)\,\hat{i} + \sin(\omega_p dt)\,\hat{k})$$

For a small angle, $\cos(\omega_p dt) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\omega_p dt) \approx \omega_p \, dt$:

$$\vec{H}_2 = I\omega\,\hat{i} + I\omega\omega_p \, dt \,\hat{k}$$

### Rate of Change

$$\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \frac{\vec{H}_2 - \vec{H}_1}{dt} = I\omega\omega_p \,\hat{k}$$

The **active gyroscopic couple** required to cause this precession is:

$$C_{active} = \frac{dH}{dt} = I\omega\omega_p$$

The **reactive gyroscopic couple** (acting on the supports) is equal and opposite:

$$C_{reactive} = -I\omega\omega_p$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental result** that we will apply across all airplane, ship, and vehicle problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on an Airplane (Pitching)

### Problem Setup

An airplane has a propeller of moment of inertia $I$ spinning clockwise (viewed from the nose) with angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. The airplane noses up (pitches) with angular velocity $\omega_p$ rad/s about the **lateral (transverse) axis**. Determine the reactive gyroscopic couple and its effect on the airplane's motion.

### Step 1: Identify the Active Couple

The active gyroscopic couple acting on the propeller has magnitude:

$$C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$$

### Step 2: Determine the Plane of Action

- The propeller spin axis = **longitudinal axis** ($x$).
- The pitching axis = **transverse axis** ($y$).
- The active couple vector (gyroscopic moment) is along the **cross product** of the spin angular velocity and the precession angular velocity.

By the right-hand rule, the active couple acts in the **vertical plane** containing the $y$-axis and $z$-axis.

### Step 3: Apply the Reactive Couple

The **reactive couple** on the airplane fuselage is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. This reactive couple lies in the **horizontal plane** (since the active couple is in the vertical plane, the reactive is perpendicular to it).

### Step 4: Physical Effect (Taking Off)

- During take-off, the airplane noses **upward**.
- The propeller spins clockwise (viewed from nose).
- The reactive gyroscopic couple **turns the airplane to the left** about the vertical axis (yawing left).

### Step 5: Physical Effect (Landing)

- During landing, the airplane noses **downward**.
- The sense of precession reverses.
- The reactive gyroscopic couple **turns the airplane to the right** about the vertical axis (yawing right).

### Final Result

The airplane experiences a horizontal reactive gyroscopic couple of magnitude $I\omega\omega_p$ that produces a yawing moment during pitching. The pilot must counter this with the **rudder**.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on a Ship (Steering)

### Problem Setup

A ship has a turbine rotor of moment of inertia $I$ spinning at $\omega$ rad/s about the longitudinal (fore-aft) axis. The ship turns to port (left) with angular velocity $\omega_p$ about the vertical axis. Determine the reactive gyroscopic couple and its effect.

### Step 1: Active Gyroscopic Couple

$$C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$$

### Step 2: Plane of Action

- Spin axis: longitudinal ($x$, fore-aft).
- Precession axis: vertical ($z$).
- Active couple lies in the **vertical-transverse plane** (i.e., the plane containing the vertical and transverse axes).

### Step 3: Reactive Couple on Ship

The reactive couple acts on the ship's hull, equal in magnitude, opposite in direction. It lies in the **longitudinal-vertical plane**.

### Step 4: Effect

The reactive couple tends to **pitch the ship**:
- For a ship turning to **port (left)** in the **Northern Hemisphere** with the rotor spinning clockwise (viewed from the stern): the **bow dips** and the **stern rises**.

> [!NOTE]
> A useful mnemonic: **"Turning left makes the bow dive"** (only for the assumed direction of spin in the Northern Hemisphere — the KTU board always specifies the hemisphere and spin direction).

### Numerical Worked Example

A ship has a turbine rotor with:
- $I = 15{,}000$ kg·m²
- $\omega = 2000$ rpm $= 2000 \times 2\pi/60 = 209.44$ rad/s
- Turning rate: $\omega_p = 0.05$ rad/s

The gyroscopic couple is:

$$C = 15{,}000 \times 209.44 \times 0.05 = 1.57 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stability of an Automobile on a Level Curved Path

### Setup

Let an automobile of mass $m$ travel on a level curved road of radius $r$ at velocity $v$ m/s. Let $G$ be the center of gravity at height $h$ above the road. The track width is $2a$ (so each wheel is at distance $a$ from the centerline).

### Step 1: Identify the Forces

Three forces act on the automobile:
1. **Weight** $W = mg$ acting downward at $G$.
2. **Centrifugal force** $F_c = mv^2/r$ acting outward (away from the center of curvature) at $G$.
3. **Reactions** at the wheels.

### Step 2: Free Body Analysis

Assume the automobile has tilted by a small angle $\theta$ from the vertical. Take moments about the outer wheel contact point $O$ to find the **overturning condition**.

The overturning moment (caused by centrifugal force) about $O$ is:

$$M_{overturn} = \frac{mv^2}{r} \cdot h \cos\theta$$

The restoring moment (caused by weight) about $O$ is:

$$M_{restore} = mg \cdot a \cos\theta - \frac{mv^2}{r} \cdot a \sin\theta$$

Wait — let us set up the geometry more carefully.

### Step 3: Tilted CG Position

If the automobile tilts outward by angle $\theta$, the CG shifts by:
- Horizontally outward: $a + h \sin\theta$ (relative to inner wheel)
- Vertically: $h \cos\theta$

### Step 4: Equilibrium Moments

Sum of moments about the outer wheel contact (taking outward as positive):

$$\sum M_O = mg \cdot (a - 0) - \frac{mv^2}{r} \cdot h = 0$$

Wait — the correct approach is:

Take moments about the **inner wheel contact point** (which acts as the pivot when about to overturn outward):

$$mg \cdot a = \frac{mv^2}{r} \cdot h$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{v^2}{gr} = \frac{a}{h}$$

### Step 5: Critical Overturning Velocity

$$\boxed{v_o = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot r \cdot a}{h}}}$$

### Step 6: Critical Skidding Velocity

For skidding, the lateral centrifugal force equals the maximum frictional force:

$$\frac{mv^2}{r} = \mu \cdot mg$$

$$\boxed{v_s = \sqrt{\mu \cdot g \cdot r}}$$

### Step 7: Stability Conditions

- If $v_s < v_o$: the vehicle **skids before overturning** (preferred safe condition).
- If $v_s > v_o$: the vehicle **overturns before skidding** (dangerous for high-CG vehicles).

**Condition for skidding before overturning:**

$$\mu \cdot g \cdot r < \frac{g \cdot r \cdot a}{h} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \mu < \frac{a}{h}$$

### Step 8: Numerical Verification

Given: $r = 50$ m, $a = 0.75$ m, $h = 0.6$ m, $\mu = 0.4$.

Compute overturning speed:

$$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 50 \times 0.75}{0.6}} = \sqrt{613.125} = 24.76 \text{ m/s} = 89.1 \text{ km/h}$$

Compute skidding speed:

$$v_s = \sqrt{0.4 \times 9.81 \times 50} = \sqrt{196.2} = 14.0 \text{ m/s} = 50.4 \text{ km/h}$$

Check condition: $a/h = 0.75/0.6 = 1.25 > \mu = 0.4$. Therefore, the vehicle **skids before overturning** (safe).

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Stability of a Two-Wheel Vehicle

### Setup

A motorcycle of mass $m$ and CG height $h$ negotiates a curve of radius $r$ at velocity $v$. Determine the required angle of lean $\theta$ for stability.

### Step 1: Force Balance

For the two-wheeler to be in equilibrium while turning:
- Weight $W = mg$ acts **downward** at $G$.
- Centrifugal force $F_c = mv^2/r$ acts **outward** (away from curve center) at $G$.

### Step 2: Condition for Stability

The resultant of $W$ and $F_c$ must pass **through the line of contact** of the tires with the road, so that the tire friction (purely longitudinal in a banked turn) provides the necessary centripetal force without creating an additional overturning moment.

### Step 3: Geometry

The resultant $R$ of $W$ and $F_c$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the vertical, where:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{F_c}{W} = \frac{mv^2/r}{mg} = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}$$

### Step 4: Required Lean Angle

$$\boxed{\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}\right)}$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example

For $v = 20$ m/s and $r = 50$ m:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{400}{9.81 \times 50} = \frac{400}{490.5} = 0.8155$$

$$\theta = 39.2^{\circ}$$

The rider must lean the motorcycle inward by **39.2°** to maintain stability on this curve.

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Banking Angle for an Automobile

### Setup

A curved road of radius $r$ is banked at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal. A vehicle of mass $m$ moves at velocity $v$. Determine the banking angle for which **no lateral friction is required** to keep the vehicle on the curve.

### Step 1: Resolve Forces

- **Weight** $W = mg$ (downward).
- **Normal reaction** $N$ (perpendicular to the road surface).
- **No friction** (ideal banking).

### Step 2: Force Balance

Vertical equilibrium:

$$N \cos\alpha = mg$$

Horizontal equilibrium (centripetal force provided by horizontal component of $N$):

$$N \sin\alpha = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\alpha$

Dividing the second equation by the first:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}$$

### Step 4: General Result

$$\boxed{\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}\right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **same expression** as for the two-wheeler's lean angle! The principle is identical: the resultant of weight and centrifugal force must be perpendicular to the road (for a banked road) or pass through the tire contact (for a two-wheeler).

$\blacksquare$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Gyroscopic Effects on Vehicles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROTATING ELEMENT: Spinning Rotor] --> B{MASS MOMENT OF INERTIA I}
    A --> C{SPIN VELOCITY omega}
    B --> D[G Y R O S C O P I C   C O U P L E]
    C --> D
    E{PRECESSION VELOCITY omega_p} --> D
    D --> F[Magnitude: I times omega times omega_p]
    D --> G[Direction: Perpendicular to spin and precession axes]

    F --> H[VEHICLE PLATFORM]
    G --> H
    H --> H1[AIRPLANE: Spinner spins about longitudinal axis]
    H --> H2[SHIP: Turbine spins about longitudinal axis]
    H --> H3[AUTOMOBILE: Engine spins about crankshaft axis]
    H --> H4[TWO WHEELER: Wheels spin about transverse axis]

    H1 --> I1[PITCHING: Yawing tendency]
    H1 --> I2[ROLLING: Yawing tendency]
    H1 --> I3[YAWING: Rolling tendency]

    H2 --> J1[STEERING: Pitching tendency]
    H2 --> J2[PITCHING: Rolling tendency]
    H2 --> J3[ROLLING: Yawing tendency]

    H3 --> K1[STABILITY: Overturning vs Skidding]
    H3 --> K2[BANKED ROAD: tan alpha equals v squared over g r]

    H4 --> L1[LEAN ANGLE: tan theta equals v squared over g r]
    L1 --> L2[Dynamic stability from gyroscopic precession]
    L1 --> L3[Trail and caster action assist stability]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Vehicle Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle in Motion] --> B{Condition}
    B -->|Straight Path| C[Directional Stability Check]
    B -->|Curved Path| D{Curve Geometry}

    C --> C1[Check lateral wind force]
    C1 --> C2{Tilt less than critical angle?}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3[STABLE: Heel within limits]
    C2 -->|No| C4[UNSTABLE: Risk of overturning]

    D --> D1[Level Road]
    D --> D2[Banked Road]

    D1 --> D1A[Compute v_o and v_s]
    D1A --> D1B{v_s less than v_o?}
    D1B -->|Yes| D1C[STABLE: Skids first]
    D1B -->|No| D1D[UNSTABLE: Overturns first]

    D2 --> D2A[Compute tan alpha]
    D2A --> D2B{alpha equals v squared over g r?}
    D2B -->|Yes| D2C[IDEAL: No lateral thrust]
    D2B -->|No| D2D[Subideal: Friction required]
```

## 4.3 Free Body Diagram: Automobile on a Banked Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["VEHICLE CROSS SECTION (BANKED CURVE)"]
        V[Vehicle Body] -->|"CG at height h"| CG[Point G]
        CG -->|"Weight mg downward"| W[Vector W]
        CG -->|"Centrifugal mv squared over r outward"| FC[Vector Fc]
        W --> R[Resultant R]
        FC --> R
        R -->|"Angle theta from vertical"| TIRE[Line of Tire Contact]
        BANK[Banked Road Surface] -->|"Angle alpha"| TIRE
    end
    subgraph S2["EQUILIBRIUM EQUATIONS"]
        E1[Vertical: N cos alpha equals mg]
        E2[Horizontal: N sin alpha equals mv squared over r]
        E3[Result: tan alpha equals v squared over g r]
        E1 --> E3
        E2 --> E3
    end
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Two-Wheeler Lean Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Wheeler in Turn] --> B[Velocity v along curved path]
    A --> C[Radius r of turn]
    B --> D[Centrifugal Force Fc outward at CG]
    C --> D
    A --> E[Weight mg downward at CG]
    D --> F[Resultant R]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Angle theta from vertical]
    G --> H{R passes through tire contact patch?}
    H -->|Yes| I[STABLE: No tip-over moment]
    H -->|No| J[UNSTABLE: Rider must adjust lean]
    G --> K[Lean Angle: tan theta equals v squared over g r]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation guide:** In all the schematics above, the arrows represent force vectors, the labeled angles are measured from the vertical, and the equilibrium condition is that the resultant of weight and centrifugal force must pass through the tire-road contact patch (or be perpendicular to the banked surface).
