# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define gyroscopic couple. State the expression for the magnitude of the gyroscopic couple and explain what is meant by reactive gyroscopic couple.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Gyroscopic couple (1 mark):** A gyroscopic couple is the couple required to precess (tilt) the axis of a spinning rotor. It is equal to the rate of change of angular momentum of the rotor.

**Expression (1 mark):** The magnitude of the gyroscopic couple is given by:

$$C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p$$

where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis, $\omega$ is the spin angular velocity, and $\omega_p$ is the angular velocity of precession.

**Reactive gyroscopic couple (1 mark):** The reactive gyroscopic couple is the equal and opposite couple exerted by the spinning rotor on the supporting structure (bearings, frame, fuselage, hull, chassis). It is this reactive couple that produces the observed effects in airplanes, ships, and vehicles.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is the condition for a two-wheel vehicle to be in stable equilibrium while negotiating a curve? Derive the required lean-angle expression.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Condition (1 mark):** The resultant of the weight and the centrifugal force acting at the center of gravity must pass through the line of contact of the tires with the road, so that no overturning couple acts on the vehicle.

**Derivation (2 marks):** Let $m$ be the mass, $v$ the velocity, $r$ the radius of the curve, and $\theta$ the angle of lean. For equilibrium:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{F_c}{W} = \frac{mv^2/r}{mg} = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v^2}{g \cdot r}\right)}$$

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** An airplane has a propeller of moment of inertia $I = 25$ kg·m² spinning at 1800 rpm. The airplane takes off and noses up at a pitch rate of 1 rad/s. The propeller rotates clockwise as seen from the nose. Determine:
> 1. The magnitude of the gyroscopic couple.
> 2. The plane in which the active couple acts.
> 3. The direction of the yawing tendency of the airplane. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A ship has a turbine rotor of moment of inertia $I = 12{,}000$ kg·m² rotating at 2400 rpm. The ship turns to port at a rate of 0.04 rad/s. Determine the gyroscopic couple and state its effect on the ship (Northern Hemisphere, clockwise rotor spin viewed from stern). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Given:**
- $I = 25$ kg·m²
- $N = 1800$ rpm, so $\omega = 1800 \times 2\pi/60 = 188.5$ rad/s
- $\omega_p = 1$ rad/s

**1. Magnitude of the gyroscopic couple (3 marks):**

$$C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p = 25 \times 188.5 \times 1 = 4712.4 \text{ N·m}$$

**2. Plane of the active couple (2 marks):**

The propeller spins about the **longitudinal axis** ($x$-axis). The airplane pitches about the **lateral (transverse) axis** ($y$-axis). The active gyroscopic couple, being the cross product of the spin and precession angular momenta, lies in the **plane containing the transverse axis and the vertical axis** (i.e., the vertical plane normal to the longitudinal axis).

**3. Direction of yawing tendency (2 marks):**

By the right-hand rule, with the propeller spinning clockwise (viewed from the nose) and the airplane pitching nose-up, the active couple on the propeller has its reactive equivalent acting on the fuselage in the **horizontal plane**, producing a **yawing moment to the left** (i.e., the airplane yaws to the left during take-off).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Given:**
- $I = 12{,}000$ kg·m²
- $N = 2400$ rpm, so $\omega = 2400 \times 2\pi/60 = 251.33$ rad/s
- $\omega_p = 0.04$ rad/s

**Gyroscopic couple (3 marks):**

$$C = I \cdot \omega \cdot \omega_p = 12{,}000 \times 251.33 \times 0.04 = 1.206 \times 10^5 \text{ N·m}$$

$$C \approx 120.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Effect on the ship (4 marks):**

- The rotor spins about the **longitudinal axis** (fore-aft).
- The ship yaws (turns) about the **vertical axis**.
- The active gyroscopic couple acts in the **vertical-transverse plane**.
- The reactive couple on the hull acts in the **longitudinal-vertical plane**, producing a pitching motion.

For a ship turning to **port (left)** with the rotor spinning **clockwise viewed from the stern** (Northern Hemisphere):

$$\boxed{\text{The bow dips downward and the stern rises upward.}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** An automobile of mass 1500 kg is traveling on a level curved road of radius 60 m. The center of gravity is at a height of 0.5 m and the track width is 1.5 m. The coefficient of friction between the tires and the road is 0.6. Determine:
> 1. The speed at which the automobile will overturn.
> 2. The speed at which the automobile will skid.
> 3. Whether the automobile will skid first or overturn first. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A two-wheeler of mass 250 kg (with rider) negotiates a curve of radius 25 m at a speed of 18 km/h. Determine:
> 1. The angle of lean required for stability.
> 2. The centrifugal force acting on the two-wheeler. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $r = 60$ m, $h = 0.5$ m, track width $2a = 1.5$ m so $a = 0.75$ m, $\mu = 0.6$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**1. Overturning speed (3 marks):**

$$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot r \cdot a}{h}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 60 \times 0.75}{0.5}}$$

$$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{441.45}{0.5}} = \sqrt{882.9} = 29.71 \text{ m/s} = 106.97 \text{ km/h}$$

**2. Skidding speed (2 marks):**

$$v_s = \sqrt{\mu \cdot g \cdot r} = \sqrt{0.6 \times 9.81 \times 60} = \sqrt{353.16} = 18.79 \text{ m/s} = 67.66 \text{ km/h}$$

**3. Comparison (2 marks):**

Since $v_s = 18.79$ m/s $< v_o = 29.71$ m/s, the automobile **skids before overturning**. This is the safe condition.

**Verification using $\mu$ vs $a/h$:**

$$\frac{a}{h} = \frac{0.75}{0.5} = 1.5 \quad\text{and}\quad \mu = 0.6$$

Since $\mu = 0.6 < a/h = 1.5$, the condition $\mu < a/h$ is satisfied → **vehicle skids before overturning**. ✓

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Given:**
- $m = 250$ kg
- $r = 25$ m
- $v = 18$ km/h $= 18 \times 5/18 = 5$ m/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**1. Angle of lean (4 marks):**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{g \cdot r} = \frac{5^2}{9.81 \times 25} = \frac{25}{245.25} = 0.1019$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.1019) = 5.82^{\circ}$$

**2. Centrifugal force (3 marks):**

$$F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r} = \frac{250 \times 25}{25} = 250 \text{ N}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and where you lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** — The gyroscopic couple formula $C = I\omega\omega_p$ requires $\omega$ in **rad/s**, not rpm. Students who write $C = 25 \times 1800 \times 1$ lose 1 mark immediately. Always do: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up active and reactive couples** — The active couple is on the rotor (used to compute magnitude), while the **reactive couple is on the frame/ship/airplane fuselage** (used to determine observed effect). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Direction of yaw/pitch/roll** — For an airplane, the direction of the reactive couple depends on the **direction of propeller spin and the sense of precession**. A wrong sign on the right-hand rule loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Skipping the stability condition check** — In automobile problems, you must always state explicitly whether the vehicle skids first or overturns first, and **verify with the condition $\mu < a/h$** or $\mu > a/h$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in two-wheeler and automobile problems — A classic 1-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Confusing "lean angle" with "angle of banking"** — Mathematically they have the same form $\tan\theta = v^2/(gr)$, but physically they are different: lean angle is the **rider's action**; banking angle is the **road designer's choice**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Gyroscopic couple magnitude:** Always $C = I\omega\omega_p$ (active couple on rotor; reactive couple on frame is equal and opposite).
- [x] **Airplane pitching → yawing tendency:** Nose up ⇒ yaw left; Nose down ⇒ yaw right (for a clockwise propeller viewed from the nose, Northern Hemisphere convention).
- [x] **Airplane rolling → yawing tendency:** The banking maneuver causes a small yaw.
- [x] **Ship steering → pitching:** Turning port (left) with clockwise rotor (N. Hemisphere) ⇒ bow dips.
- [x] **Ship pitching → rolling:** Wave-induced pitch causes a roll.
- [x] **Ship rolling → yawing:** Beam waves cause a yaw.
- [x] **Automobile overturning speed (level road):** $v_o = \sqrt{gra/h}$.
- [x] **Automobile skidding speed (level road):** $v_s = \sqrt{\mu g r}$.
- [x] **Stability condition for automobile:** Skids first if $\mu < a/h$; overturns first if $\mu > a/h$.
- [x] **Banking angle for zero side thrust:** $\tan\alpha = v^2/(gr)$.
- [x] **Two-wheeler lean angle:** $\tan\theta = v^2/(gr)$.
- [x] **Units check:** $\omega$ in rad/s, $v$ in m/s, $r$ in m, $h$ and $a$ in m, $I$ in kg·m², $C$ in N·m.
- [x] **Right-hand rule** must be applied consistently for all directional effects.
- [x] **Dynamic stability** of a two-wheeler is a combined effect of gyroscopic precession, trail/caster, and rider input — not gyroscopic alone.
- [x] The **gyroscopic effect is a vector phenomenon**: magnitude comes from the scalar product, direction from the cross product.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick exam mnemonic — "PYP, SRP, SRR" for ship gyroscopic effects:**
> **S**teering → **P**itching, **P**itching (waves) → **R**olling, **R**olling (beam waves) → (**Y**awing, sometimes written as YRR).
> This is a very common 2-3 mark short question in KTU exams — remember the cycle!
